title: World Diving Calendar
description: Year-round diving conditions for global destinations, organized by month. Each destination includes monthly surface water temperature (degC), currents, marine-life highlights, conditions/visibility and a season rating (Peak / Good / Shoulder / Low / Closed).

rating_legend.Peak: Best conditions and/or signature marine life
rating_legend.Closed: Not accessible (park closure or no operators)

destinations.description: Some 350 km off Brazil's northeast coast, this volcanic archipelago sits inside a strictly protected marine park where visibility routinely tops 30 m. Day boats work canyons, tunnels and arches like Pedras Secas while huge spinner dolphin pods, turtles and lemon and reef sharks patrol the blue. Most sites suit newer divers in the dry Aug-Dec season, and technical divers get a genuine prize: an intact navy corvette upright at 62 m.
destinations.underwater: Volcanic rock gives these dives their shape: canyons, tunnels and arches at Pedras Secas, a cave mouth at about 24 m sheltering big stingrays and black grouper at Caverna da Sapata, and a pocket of trapped air under the ceiling of Buraco do Inferno. Water sits at 26-28°C all year, and in the August-to-December dry season visibility stretches past 30 m. Leeward sites stay mild; when you want current, drift the huge rock formations at Pontal do Norte.
destinations.encounters: Spinner dolphins are the headline act, pods streaming through the bay from dawn and often escorting the boats out. Below, green and hawksbill turtles graze almost every site, reef and nurse sharks patrol the ledges, and lemon sharks cruise the sand flats in the clear dry-season months. Around March, when the swell is up, the turtles come ashore to nest.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Tadeu Pereira from São Paulo, Brazil (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: No destination puts big sharks in front of divers more reliably than the Bahamas. Baited dives on shallow sandbanks bring tiger and lemon sharks within arm's length, great hammerheads and oceanic whitetips show in winter, and reef sharks patrol nearly every wall. Between shark runs there are upright freighter wrecks, coral-rimmed blue holes and ripping channel drifts like Current Cut. Day boats and liveaboards cover it, with Nov-May the prime window.
destinations.underwater: Most of the marquee dives happen kneeling on white sand in 6-12 m, watching tiger sharks materialise out of the haze at Tiger Beach. Away from the bait crates the terrain shifts fast: Theo's Wreck lies on her port side at 30 m, Lost Blue Hole drops past a coral-rimmed lip at 12 m, and Current Cut fires you through the channel at ripping drift speed. Water runs 24°C in winter to 30°C in late summer, with visibility often past 25 m.
destinations.encounters: Sharks carry every dive: tigers and lemons over the sand, Caribbean reef sharks on nearly every wall, great hammerheads gliding low across the Bimini shallows through the winter months, and oceanic whitetips circling off Cat Island in late spring. Between shark runs, dolphins ride the bow and turtles cross the blue while dense reef fish pack the coral heads.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: User: (WT-shared) Angieal24 at  wts wikivoyage (Public domain) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: Strung along the Mesoamerican Reef off Honduras, Roatan, Utila and Guanaja deliver some of the Caribbean's most affordable wall diving, with seasonal whale sharks as the headline pelagic. Guanaja adds volcanic canyons, a black-coral seamount and the intact Jado Trader wreck at 24-33 m, while the Cayos Cochinos marine reserve keeps pristine elkhorn shallows. Currents stay mostly mild, and shore entries, day boats and liveaboards all work here.
destinations.underwater: Expect classic Caribbean wall diving: you drop over sheer faces like Vertigo and follow drop-offs that simply end in blue. At Jim's Silverlode a tunnel at 21 m spits you into a sandy amphitheatre ringed by grouper, Black Rock Canyon threads volcanic caves lit by silversides and sweepers, and The Pinnacle climbs from 41 m to a black-coral summit at 17 m. Water sits at 27-30°C in a 3 mm, currents stay light, and spring visibility can top 30 m.
destinations.encounters: Whale sharks are the headline, feeding off Utila from late winter into spring and reappearing offshore from October through December. Between sightings the reef carries the day: turtles graze the sponge-covered walls year-round, eagle rays sail past the drop-offs, nurse sharks doze under ledges, and seahorses cling to the inshore seagrass. Tarpon hang in the caverns at Pelican Point in the Cayos Cochinos reserve.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Hector Abouid (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: Bonaire perfected drive-and-dive freedom: rent a truck, load tanks, and roll into any of the marked sites along the leeward west coast whenever you like. The fringing reef starts meters from shore and stays remarkably healthy, from the fish-thick house reefs off Kralendijk to the sponge-wrapped pilings of Salt Pier and the shore-accessible Hilma Hooker wreck at 30 m. Calm, easy conditions make it ideal for new divers and macro hunters chasing frogfish and seahorses.
destinations.underwater: Entries are as simple as diving gets: stride off a dock or wade over rubble, cross a sandy shelf, and the terraced coral begins around 6 m before rolling down past 25 m. Current on the leeward coast is near nil, the water holds 26-29°C, and visibility runs 25 m and better through the calm months. The set-piece dives are structural, the Hilma Hooker's 72 m hull lying on her side at 30 m and Salt Pier's pilings furred in sponge and shafted with light.
destinations.encounters: Turtles are the constant, grazing the shore reefs and cruising the black-coral wall at Forest off Klein Bonaire, while tarpon hold in the shadows under piers. Slow searching on the sandy slopes near the marina turns up seahorses, frogfish and swaying garden eels. Bari Reef carries the Caribbean's highest recorded fish counts, eagle rays pass the drop in spring, and late-summer nights bring coral spawning.

destinations.description: Three islands, one theme: walls that fall away into genuinely deep blue. Little Cayman's Bloody Bay Wall starts at 6 m and plunges past 300 m, Grand Cayman stacks caverns, pinnacles and the diver-friendly Kittiwake wreck off Seven Mile Beach, and Cayman Brac holds a Russian frigate with its guns still mounted. Conditions stay clear and mostly mild, so day boats handle nearly everything, and the famous stingray sandbar makes an easy interval between wall dives.
destinations.underwater: You roll in over white sand at 26-30°C and the bottom simply ends: at Bloody Bay Wall the crest sits at 6 m and the face drops sheer past 300 m, leaving you suspended in 30 m visibility with blue under your fins. The rest stays easy in the mild current — swim the Kittiwake's decks from 5 m, thread Trinity Caves' coral canyons, or push through Devil's Grotto's tunnels as silversides part around your torch.
destinations.encounters: Southern stingrays crowd the North Sound sandbar in barely 3 m of water, brushing past on every pass. Turtles work the wall tops year-round while eagle rays glide by in the blue, and around the February full moon Nassau grouper mass to spawn. In summer, silversides pack the George Town caverns with tarpon hunting through them.

destinations.description: Drift diving doesn't get more effortless than Cozumel. A steady current sweeps divers along the island's Mesoamerican reef walls in gin-clear water while the boat follows, so you simply hang and watch Palancar's coral buttresses, Santa Rosa's swim-throughs and passing eagle rays slide by. Gentle strip reefs suit beginners, Devil's Throat's 40 m tunnel rewards advanced divers, and night dives turn up the endemic splendid toadfish. Diving runs year-round, calmest May-Aug.
destinations.underwater: Buoyancy is the whole game here - trim out and let the Yucatan Current sweep you past coral buttresses and sand chutes in 26 to 29°C water that regularly opens beyond 30 m. Punta Sur's Devil's Throat is the signature moment: you drop into the tunnel at 24 m and exit the wall face at 40. For a change of texture, the C-53 minesweeper sits upright at 24 m, open to careful penetration.
destinations.encounters: The endemic splendid toadfish is the local celebrity, croaking from coral dens and best found on a night dive. Turtles ride the drift year-round and haul out to nest in late spring, while nurse sharks doze under ledges. Eagle rays are the winter draw, sweeping the drop-offs in loose schools once the cooler months set in.

destinations.description: Belize's Great Blue Hole is a flooded ice-age cavern 300 m across and 125 m deep in the middle of Lighthouse Reef, its rim a near-perfect circle visible from the air. The dive itself is a short, deep descent past stalactites at 40 m with Caribbean reef sharks circling in the gloom, best treated as an advanced bounce. The real richness lies on the surrounding atoll walls, so day trips and liveaboards pair the hole with sites like Half Moon Caye Wall.
destinations.underwater: The descent is the dive: you drop along a sheer wall into deepening blue until stalactites the size of tree trunks emerge at 40 m, hanging in near-darkness while reef sharks trace slow circles below. Current is minimal inside the sinkhole; depth and gloom are the real tests. Back on the atoll, Painted Wall's sponge-covered drop and the Silver Caves swim-through, often packed with thousands of silversides, round out the day in 26-29°C water.
destinations.encounters: Caribbean reef sharks are the residents, circling the sinkhole's rim and walls year-round while nurse sharks sweep the sandy ledges below. The surrounding atoll adds turtles, groupers and eagle rays along Half Moon Caye Wall. In spring, trips pair the hole with Gladden Spit, where whale sharks gather to feed around the full moons from March into June.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: LovingOop (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: The largest of Honduras's Bay Islands puts a wall minutes from almost every beach, making it one of the Caribbean's best-value places to dive and to learn. Mild, clear conditions favor beginners, yet the signature dives have teeth: the vertical coral crevice of Mary's Place, ice-age canyons like Spooky Channel, and two of the region's biggest wrecks off Sandy Bay. Shore entries and short boat runs mean three or four relaxed dives a day is the norm.
destinations.underwater: Most dives start on a shallow reef crest and tip over an edge within minutes of the mooring. The water holds 27-30°C year-round, so a 3mm suit covers an hour of finning along sponge-crusted walls with almost no current to fight. Thread the swim-throughs of Blue Channel at 5-15 m, follow Hole in the Wall's sand chute out of the reef at 30 m, or descend the broken hull of El Aguila at 33 m; dry-season visibility can stretch past 30 m.
destinations.encounters: Turtles are the near-guarantee, grazing sponges on the walls of almost every dive, with eagle rays sailing past the drop-offs and nurse sharks dozing under ledges. Look closer for seahorses in the sheltered shallows and morays in the Blue Channel swim-throughs. The dry March-to-September stretch brings the calmest seas and busiest reef; even the rainy months keep turtles working the coral heads.

destinations.description: Small, cheap and endlessly sociable, Utila has certified generations of divers on its calm Bay Islands reefs, and it backs the training-ground reputation with real substance. Whale sharks cruise the open water off the north side year-round, peaking Feb-Apr and Oct-Dec, while offshore seamounts like Black Hills swirl with jacks and barracuda. Day boats reach everything in minutes, from easy sponge-draped walls to the upright Halliburton wreck at 30 m.
destinations.underwater: Utila's diving is warm and easygoing: 27-30°C water, mild current and short boat rides to walls that begin around 10 m and, at CJ's Drop Off, fall away past 40 m. Duppy Waters hangs giant barrel sponges off the reef edge, the Airport Caves thread swim-throughs filled with glassy sweepers, and the Halliburton sits upright and intact on sand at 30 m. Out at Black Hills, an offshore seamount, jacks and barracuda swirl in open blue water.
destinations.encounters: Whale sharks headline, cruising off the north side with the best odds from February to April and again October through December. Between sightings the reef carries you: turtles on nearly every wall, eagle rays gliding the drop-offs, nurse sharks resting under ledges in summer, and seahorses hiding in the shallows for divers who slow down.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: ogwen (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: Sipadan rises 600 m straight from the Celebes Sea floor, an oceanic pinnacle off Malaysian Borneo where daily permits cap diver numbers. Current is the switch that turns the action on: at Barracuda Point a resident school of chevron barracuda coils into its famous tornado, whitetips rest along the ledges, and green and hawksbill turtles are everywhere. Day boats run from nearby islands, conditions favor experienced divers, and the island closes every November.
destinations.underwater: The wall begins a few fin kicks from the jetty: Drop Off plunges 600 m into the Celebes Sea, and most dives hang on some stretch of that vertical face, past the soft-coral terraces of Hanging Gardens and a cavern mouth at 18 m famed for its turtle skeletons. Water is bathwater-warm at 28-30°C, and current sets the tempo — when it runs hard at Barracuda Point and South Point, the action switches on. October visibility can open to 50 m.
destinations.encounters: Green and hawksbill turtles are on every dive, thickest once nesting begins in March. The resident chevron barracuda coil into their tornado over Barracuda Point, bumphead parrotfish parade across Staghorn Crest, and whitetip reef sharks rest along White Tip Avenue. Dive at dawn in season and a hammerhead may slide past in the blue.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Adznee Abas from Shah Alam, Malaysia (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: Stretching more than 2,300 kilometers along Queensland's coast, the Great Barrier Reef is the largest reef system on Earth, and its best diving lives offshore. Liveaboards run the Ribbon Reefs for Cod Hole's tame potato cod and June-July dwarf minke whale encounters, then push into the Coral Sea for shark action at Osprey's North Horn. Day boats from Cairns cover easy coral gardens, while the current-swept SS Yongala wreck rewards advanced divers.
destinations.underwater: The scale changes with every mooring. Coral pinnacles like Steve's Bommie climb from 30 m to within 5 m of the surface, Osprey Reef's walls drop into Coral Sea blue where visibility can pass 40 m, and the current-swept SS Yongala is dived only at slack tide. Inner reefs stay mild while the outer Ribbon Reef walls drift with the tides. Winter water dips to 23°C, summer reaches 30°C, and at Cod Hole giant potato cod meet you on the descent.
destinations.encounters: June and July belong to the dwarf minke whales, which approach hovering divers on the Ribbon Reefs while humpbacks pass on their winter migration. Giant potato cod are resident at Cod Hole, green turtles graze the coral gardens, and grey reef and whitetip sharks patrol North Horn. Mantas arrive with the first plankton of the dry season, and after the November full moon the coral itself spawns.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Ank Kumar (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: Seventy-four islands scatter across the Coral Sea off central Queensland, pairing sheltered fringing reefs with runs to the outer Great Barrier Reef. Hook Island's bays deliver swim-throughs, Maori wrasse, and manta rays from May through September, while Hardy Reef adds giant Queensland grouper on the outer barrier. Humpback whales pass through from June to September. Calm bays and shallow profiles make this one of Australia's easiest places to log relaxed day-boat and liveaboard dives.
destinations.underwater: Diving splits between sheltered island bays and outer-reef runs. Inside, Hook Island bommies like The Cathedral stack coral outcrops and swim-throughs in 8-25 m, and Blue Pearl Bay's gorgonian gullies rarely pass 19 m. Out on the Great Barrier Reef, Bait Reef's Stepping Stones - eighteen flat-topped pinnacles rising from 25 m - are the marquee dive. Water swings from 29°C in summer to 23°C midwinter, tides drive whatever current you meet, and the dry season brings visibility past 30 m.
destinations.encounters: From June to September humpback whales calve in the sheltered passages and their song reaches you underwater, while manta rays sweep into Manta Ray Bay and The Pinnacles from May through September. Year-round, green turtles graze the fringing reefs, Maori wrasse loom over the swim-throughs, reef sharks patrol the deeper bommies, and giant Queensland grouper hold Hardy Reef. On November nights, coral spawn drifts over the reefs.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Jeremymcwhirter (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

dive_sites.name: Coral Cascades

dive_sites.name: Pam's Point
dive_sites.blurb: Reef-edge site on Heron Reef teeming with turtles and reef fish

dive_sites.name: Three Rocks

destinations.description: A true coral cay on the southern Great Barrier Reef, Heron Island puts its dive sites just minutes from the jetty, some reachable straight from shore. Heron Bommie stacks manta rays, eagle rays, and turtles on a single outcrop, and gentle drifts along the Tenements carry you past reef sharks with barely a fin kick. Come May through August for mantas, or midsummer when green and loggerhead turtles nest on the beach. Few places make beginner diving this rich.
destinations.underwater: Everything happens shallow here; most sites hold between 5 and 20 m on the fringing edge of a true coral cay. You circle isolated coral heads like Harry's Bommie and the Three Rocks trio, cruise the fan-coral ledges of Gorgonia Hole, and let mild tidal flow carry you along Coral Cascades toward the Tenements. Summer water sits at 26-28°C; the austral winter cools to 21-22°C but pays you back with the year's best visibility, stretching past 30 m at its clearest.
destinations.encounters: Mantas own the cooler months, circling the cleaning stations from autumn into early spring while humpback whales pass beyond the reef. Summer belongs to turtles: greens and loggerheads nest on the cay nightly, and by late season hatchlings scramble for the water. Reef sharks patrol the drop-offs year-round, wobbegongs rest beneath Harry's Bommie, and epaulette sharks and cod fill out the cast.

destinations.name: Komodo National Park
destinations.currents: Tidal, strongest at full/new moon; powerful drift; Castle Rock/Crystal Rock (reef-hook pinnacles), Batu Bolong (ferocious flank currents), The Cauldron/Shotgun (committed drift); downcurrents at pinnacles. N warm/calm, S cold upwelling/rough
destinations.highlights: Strong tidal drifts; warm north vs cold-upwelling south; mantas year-round by zone.

destinations.description: Tides squeeze between the Pacific and Indian Oceans through Komodo's narrow straits, and the currents they generate power some of the most electric diving in Indonesia. Hook into the reef at Castle Rock or Crystal Rock as sharks and trevally hunt the flow, fire through the Shotgun's channel, then drift Makassar Reef's manta cleaning stations. The warm north and cold, nutrient-rich south fish differently by season, so liveaboards cover it best. Bring drift experience.
destinations.underwater: Current defines every dive. You hook in on Castle Rock's seamount while the flow presses against your mask, get fired through the Shotgun's channel over its bowl, or tuck behind Batu Bolong while the tide rips past both flanks. The north sits warm at 28-29°C; in the dry season the south turns cold and green with upwelling. Long, shallow drifts over Makassar's rubble balance the adrenaline with easy gliding past cleaning stations.
destinations.encounters: Mantas anchor the year, circling the northern and central cleaning stations through the dry months and massing at Manta Alley in the south once the wet season arrives. From August into October, mola mola rise to the southern stations. Reef sharks and trevally hunt the pinnacles in the current, turtles crowd Siaba Besar, and the wet months add Rhinopias and frogfish for macro hunters.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Jakub Hałun (CC BY 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: Off West Papua's Bird's Head Peninsula, Raja Ampat's four big islands and hundreds of karst islets sit at the epicenter of global marine biodiversity; Cape Kri holds the record fish count for a single dive. Nutrient-rich currents pump through the Dampier Strait, feeding seamounts like Blue Magic where oceanic mantas circle above carpeted wobbegongs. Reef hooks earn the fishiest sites, but sheltered cleaning stations and jetty dives balance the itinerary. Dive it by liveaboard or resort, October through April.
destinations.underwater: Current is the engine. On the Dampier Strait seamounts you set a reef hook and hang in the flow, water at 27 to 30°C swinging from a plankton-thick 10 m in the shoulder season to 40 m when it clears. Elsewhere the mood flips completely: Melissa's Garden is a still hard-coral plateau, The Passage a river-like channel of archways, and Arborek's jetty pilings crawl with macro in the shallows. The variety across one itinerary is the real signature.
destinations.encounters: Mantas are the headline, piling into the cleaning stations at Manta Sandy and Manta Ridge through the northwest-monsoon months from December into March. Down on the reef the cast turns odd and small: wobbegongs flattened under ledges, epaulette sharks walking on their fins after dark, pygmy seahorses buried in the sea fans. When the mantas thin, macro and night dives take over.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Elias Levy (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: Japan's subtropical island chain strings across the East China Sea, and its diving swings from easy shore entries to serious drift. Winter sends schooling scalloped hammerheads past Yonaguni's fast currents, near the stepped stone monument that still fuels debate over its origins, while summer mantas hover over Ishigaki's cleaning stations. Around the main island, the glowing Blue Cave suits new divers and the deep USS Emmons wreck demands technical training. Day boats reach nearly all of it.
destinations.underwater: Okinawa's diving shifts shape island by island. Off Yonaguni you drift fast current past a stepped stone monument at 25 m, then hang in open water at 21-22°C in winter, waiting for shadows to resolve into hammerheads. Around the main island the pace softens: the glowing Blue Cave, soft corals along the Sunabe Seawall, and Manza's Dream Hole, a coral chimney that swallows you from 5 m into dark tunnels. Technical divers get the USS Emmons at 36-45 m.
destinations.encounters: The calendar splits cleanly: scalloped hammerheads school off Yonaguni through the winter months, thickest in February, while reef mantas work Ishigaki's cleaning stations from spring to late autumn, with mating trains snaking over the reefs in September. Sea turtles cruise the Kerama Islands' coral gardens year-round, joined by schooling jacks, dense reef fish and the occasional whale shark.

destinations.description: Thirty-six hours of open Pacific separate Cocos Island from mainland Costa Rica, and the payoff is some of the densest scalloped hammerhead schooling on the planet. Hook in on Bajo Alcyone's seamount as the schools stack overhead, watch for whale sharks and tigers off Dirty Rock, then finish with Manuelita's frenzied night dive of hunting whitetips. Strong currents, surge, and thermoclines make this strictly advanced, liveaboard-only diving; the June-November wet season brings the biggest shark numbers.
destinations.underwater: You back-roll into open Pacific, drop fast to the rock, and wedge a reef hook in at 25-35 m while current pulls your body flat like a flag. Thermoclines slide cold 22°C water under a 5-7mm suit and hood, and surge works the pinnacles even in the December-to-May calm, when visibility opens past 30 m. The wet season trades clarity for current — green water, rougher crossings — and the arch at Dos Amigos Grande is wide enough to swallow the whole group.
destinations.encounters: Scalloped hammerheads are the draw, massing in schools of hundreds over the seamounts through the June-to-November wet season, when whale sharks and mantas ride the same plankton-rich currents. The drier months swap them for tiger sharks on the cleaning stations, mobula trains and silkies in the blue; whitetips pile onto Manuelita's reef after dark in any month.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Ministerio de Ambiente y Energía del Gobierno de Costa Rica (Public domain) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: Converging ocean currents collide at this volcanic archipelago 1,000 kilometers off Ecuador, stacking cold, nutrient-loaded water against the equator, which is why nothing else dives like the Galapagos. Liveaboards push north to Darwin and Wolf, where whale sharks cruise past walls of scalloped hammerheads from June to November, while central sites add playful sea lions, mola mola, and feeding marine iguanas. Expect negative entries, down-currents, and surge: this is advanced diving, and worth every degree of chill.
destinations.underwater: This is committed diving: negative entries, a fast drop to the rock, and currents that shove, swirl and sometimes pull down. You wedge into volcanic ledges at 10-25 m and watch the blue while the ocean streams past. In the June-November cool season the water turns green and falls to 21°C under a 7mm suit, hood and gloves; December to May runs warmer and calmer, with easier crossings and visibility stretching past 20 m.
destinations.encounters: Scalloped hammerheads stack into walls at Darwin and Wolf, joined from June to November by whale sharks passing the arch on the current line. Cold upwellings bring mola mola to Punta Vicente Roca, where penguins and red-lipped batfish share the wall, while sea lions barrel through your bubbles at Cousin's Rock and giant mantas work the warm-season sites off Isabela.

destinations.description: Far out in the open Pacific south of Baja California, the volcanic Revillagigedo islands are reached only by liveaboard, and their giant oceanic mantas rank among the friendliest anywhere, banking within arm's reach at El Boiler's cleaning station. Roca Partida's lone spire stacks whitetips on its ledges while hammerheads, silvertips, and curious bottlenose dolphins patrol midwater. Humpback song fills winter dives. Surge and current across exposed pinnacles keep this advanced territory; the season runs November through May.
destinations.underwater: These are open-Pacific dives on volcanic pinnacles with nothing but blue in every direction: you hold position in the surge at El Boiler while giant mantas bank overhead, or hang off Roca Partida, a lone twin-peaked spire with whitetips stacked on its ledges between 10 and 40 m. Water runs from 23°C with thermoclines in the cool months to 28°C late in the season, so the 5mm suit earns its keep, and in winter humpback song carries through entire dives.
destinations.encounters: Giant oceanic mantas define Socorro, circling the cleaning stations and banking within arm's reach at El Boiler. Scalloped hammerheads school in the blue, silvertips, Galapagos and silky sharks cross The Canyon, and playful bottlenose dolphins seek divers out at Cabo Pearce. In the winter months humpbacks pass singing, and the first whale sharks appear when boats return in November.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Presidencia de la República Mexicana (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: Squeezed between Baja California and mainland Mexico, the Sea of Cortez trades coral walls for sheer biomass: sea lion pups that mouth your fins at Los Islotes, tornadoes of jacks over Cabo Pulmo's hard-coral fingers, and mobula rays massing in the blue. Day boats from La Paz add wrecks like the Salvatierra, and whale sharks gather in the bay from October to April. Advanced divers push out to El Bajo seamount for hammerheads; warm, clear late summer is prime.
destinations.underwater: This is rocky-reef and seamount diving rather than coral walls: boulder slopes at Los Islotes in 5-18 m where sea lion pups tug your fins, the fish-covered Salvatierra ferry at 18-20 m, and the three peaks of El Bajo dropping toward 40 m in open blue, where currents can run strong. Conditions swing hard with the seasons - 20°C green water and 10 m visibility in late winter, then 30°C and 30 m-plus by September, the calmest and clearest diving of the year.
destinations.encounters: California sea lions are the constant, darting around the rocks whenever their colony is open outside the pupping closure. Mobula rays mass in spring, whale sharks feed in the bay from autumn through the cooler months, and late summer sends schooling hammerheads over El Bajo. Add tornadoes of jacks at Cabo Pulmo, bull sharks on the El Vencedor wreck and humpbacks passing offshore.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: AlexisSalinas01 (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: Hawaii's twin dive hubs pack volcanic drama into calm leeward water. Off Kona, dive lights draw plankton, and feeding manta rays, to Garden Eel Cove nearly every night of the year, while Maui day boats run to Molokini, a sunken crescent crater with visibility pushing 45 m and a sheer back wall dropping past 100 m. Lava caverns off Lanai and turtle-thick shore entries round out a destination that serves new divers and wall-drift veterans alike.
destinations.underwater: Lava built the terrain here: ceiling cracks send light beams into the Cathedrals' rooms off Lanai, arches and caves thread Five Caves at Makena in 3-12 m, and Kona's Au Au Crater cuts a V-shaped canyon with up to 60 m of vertical relief. Leeward water stays calm at 24-27°C, easy in a 3 mm, with summer visibility past 30 m. The signature moment comes after dark at Garden Eel Cove, kneeling at 10-14 m as mantas barrel-roll through your light.
destinations.encounters: Manta rays are the constant - the Garden Eel Cove night dive connects on roughly nine dives out of ten, any month you show up. Green sea turtles queue at Turtle Pinnacle's cleaning station while wrasses work them over, whitetip reef sharks rest under Mala Wharf's collapsed pilings, and spinner dolphins cruise the warm shallows. In winter, humpback whale song runs beneath every dive as the channel fills.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Patrick  McNally from Molokai, United States (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Maldives
destinations.currents: Monsoon-driven channel (kandu) drift; NE monsoon (Dec-Apr) dive east side for viz/pelagics, mantas west; SW monsoon (May-Nov) dive west side, mantas/whale sharks east; strongest at full/new moon
destinations.highlights: Monsoon-driven channel drifts; Hanifaru manta aggregation Jul-Oct.

dive_sites.name: Fuvahmulah (Tiger Zoo)
dive_sites.blurb: Oceanic island famous for year-round tiger shark encounters

dive_sites.name: Alimatha Jetty
dive_sites.blurb: Vaavu night dive with dozens of nurse sharks and stingrays

destinations.description: Scattered across 26 atolls in the Indian Ocean, the Maldives runs on current. Monsoon-driven flow funnels through channel mouths and over isolated thilas, stacking grey reef sharks, eagle rays and schooling snapper wherever it pushes hardest. December to April brings the clearest water; May to November shifts mantas and whale sharks to the plankton-rich eastern atolls. Liveaboards cover the most ground, resorts add house-reef ease, and solid drift skills pay off everywhere.
destinations.underwater: You dive the flow, not just the reef: drop at a kandu mouth on an incoming tide, hook in at the corner, and let the blue deliver sharks and eagle rays to you. Thilas like Maaya and Kandooma rise mid-atoll into the current, flanks packed with snapper, while Fotteyo's caves and overhangs offer shelter between blasts. The water holds 28-30°C year-round; visibility swings from 30-40 m in northeast-monsoon calm to barely 10 m when southwest-monsoon plankton thickens the water.
destinations.encounters: Grey reef sharks are the baseline, holding station in nearly every channel. In the northeast monsoon, hammerheads rise at dawn off Rasdhoo; when the southwest monsoon greens the water, mantas gather at Hanifaru Bay in the hundreds and whale sharks feed off South Ari. Tiger sharks patrol Fuvahmulah in every month, and dozens of nurse sharks mob the Alimatha night dive.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Frédéric Ducarme (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Seychelles
destinations.currents: Inner islands (Mahe/Praslin) mild & beginner-friendly; SE trades May-Oct bring 20-30kt winds, surge, upwelling; outer islands/Aldabra liveaboard/permit only
destinations.highlights: Granite-boulder reefs; whale shark season Aug-Nov; calm transition-month diving.

destinations.description: Granite sets the Seychelles apart from anywhere else in the Indian Ocean: ancient boulders tumble off the beaches and continue underwater as sculpted reefs, swim-throughs and fish-wrapped pinnacles. Inner-island diving off Mahe and Praslin stays mild and beginner-friendly, with hawksbill turtles and reef sharks routine and whale sharks cruising through from August to November. The remote outer atolls, Aldabra among them, are liveaboard territory for divers chasing wilder, sharkier walls.
destinations.underwater: Granite gives these dives their shape: rounded boulders stack into swim-throughs and pinnacles, from Brissare Rocks at 8-16 m to the exposed drop-offs and caves of Trois Bancs at 40 m. Inner-island sites off Mahe stay mild and forgiving, with 26-30°C water and visibility that can stretch past 30 m in the calm transition months. Shark Bank's offshore plateau carries some current, the Ennerdale's tanker hull rests near 30 m, and L'Ilot comes alive after dark.
destinations.encounters: Whale sharks are the headline, feeding on plankton blooms off the Amirantes in April and May and returning in force to northwest Mahe from August into November, when manta numbers climb too. Hawksbill turtles are dependable year-round — Ave Maria Rocks near La Digue is the reliable spot — with nesting through the northwest monsoon. Reef and nurse sharks, eagle rays and dense reef fish fill the granite between.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Radosław Botev (CC BY 3.0 pl) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: A fossilized dune ridge a few kilometers off South Africa's KwaZulu-Natal coast, Aliwal Shoal delivers raw big-animal diving that begins with a surf launch through the breakers. From June to November, ragged-tooth sharks crowd its amphitheaters and sandy gullies; baited dives pull in blacktips and tiger sharks, and the winter sardine run can erupt into bait-ball chaos. Currents, surge and bouncing RIB rides make this one for divers who like their adrenaline unfiltered.
destinations.underwater: The Cathedral is the set piece: a 27 m amphitheatre in the fossilised reef where ragged-tooth sharks hang almost still, unbothered, through the winter. Water swings from a summer 27°C to a raw 21°C in July, viz a workable 16 to 24 m. The baited dive is a different animal - open water, blacktips wheeling in close and the odd tiger sliding through. The Produce and Nebo wrecks add rusting steel around 30 m for those wanting structure.
destinations.encounters: Ragged-tooth sharks are the residents from winter into spring, packing the caves and gullies. The baited dives bring blacktips in numbers and, in the warmer months, tiger sharks. Then there's the sardine run: through June and July the cold water erupts into bait balls, with dolphins, blacktips and passing humpback whales tearing through the chaos.

destinations.name: Ningaloo Reef
destinations.currents: Sheltered lagoon mild; outer reef and drift sites moderate
destinations.highlights: Whale shark snorkeling on the reef plus year-round mantas and turtles

Apr.conditions: warm and calm with great visibility

Jun.conditions: cooler and calm with good visibility

Aug.conditions: coolest water of the year with great visibility

destinations.image_credit: Photo: NeilsPhotography (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: Australia's largest fringing reef hugs the Western Australian coast, its coral gardens starting a short swim off the beach. The diving runs mellow and animal-rich: manta rays circling the cleaning station at Gulliver's, reef sharks queuing over cabbage coral at Asho's Gap, green turtles napping in limestone swim-throughs. The Exmouth Navy Pier alone justifies the trip, its pylons crowded with wobbegongs and frogfish, and whale sharks gather along the reef from March to July.
destinations.underwater: Most of Ningaloo's diving sits shallow, 6 to 18 m over limestone craters, tunnels and cabbage-coral plateaus, with a gentle lagoon and only the tidal passes needing slack-water timing. You weave The Labyrinth's swim-throughs past napping green turtles, hang at Gulliver's cleaning station as mantas circle in, and pick frogfish and wobbegongs off the pylons of the Exmouth Navy Pier. Water runs 22°C in the austral winter to 29°C in summer, with visibility past 30 m on the calmest days.
destinations.encounters: Whale sharks headline from March to July, drawn in by the coral spawn, and as their season fades humpback whales stream past the reef into October. Mantas and green turtles are the year-round constants, reef sharks queue over the cabbage coral at Asho's Gap, and the Muiron Islands add rays and the occasional dugong. Turtles nest on the beaches through the summer months.

dive_sites.name: P29 Patrol Boat
dive_sites.blurb: 52m ex-East German patrol boat scuttled 2007 at Cirkewwa, upright at 35m

dive_sites.name: MV Karwela

dive_sites.name: Santa Maria Caves (Comino)

dive_sites.name: Rozi Tugboat

destinations.description: Few places make shore diving this easy. The Maltese islands drop straight into clear blue Mediterranean water, and most of the classics, from the Blue Hole at Dwejra to the Cirkewwa arch to the scuttled tanker Um El Faroud, are reached by walking in with your kit. Limestone shapes the character here: caverns, chimneys and light-shafted grottoes at every level, plus deliberately sunk wrecks deep enough to reward advanced divers. May through October brings warm, settled conditions.
destinations.underwater: Shore entries lead straight into sculpted rock: an 80 m tunnel runs from the Inland Sea out to a sheer drop-off, and two natural arches stack on top of each other off Xwejni Bay. Scuttled steel adds structure — the P29 patrol boat sits upright at 35 m and the Karwela's famous staircase waits near 40 m. Visibility rarely drops below 20 m in any month; the sea runs 26°C in August but only 15°C by late winter.
destinations.encounters: Groupers hold territory on the wrecks and ledges, barracuda school thickest over the reefs in high summer when tuna flash past the drop-offs, and octopus work the rubble year-round. This is subtle Mediterranean life rather than big-animal diving: nudibranchs carpet the walls through winter, and seahorses turn up in the seagrass in the quiet December weeks.

destinations.description: Seven limestone islets off Spain's Costa Brava, the Medes Islands show what decades of strict protection do for the Mediterranean: outsized, fearless dusky groupers that swim out to inspect divers, clouds of barracuda, and gorgonian-draped walls falling toward 50 m. Tunnels like El Dofi thread clean through the rock with theatrical light shafts. Boat rides from L'Estartit take minutes, currents stay mild, and the sites layer neatly from easy caverns to deep pinnacle dives.
destinations.underwater: Limestone does the work here. El Dofi's tunnel runs 90 m clean through the rock with theatrical light at both ends, La Vaca pierces Meda Gran from an entrance at 12 m, and Carall Bernat drops gorgonian-covered walls from 5 m toward 45 m. Currents stay mild inside the reserve, visibility sits around 15-20 m in season, and summer water reaches 21-25°C, so a 5mm suit keeps it all comfortable.
destinations.encounters: Dusky groupers are the show: decades of protection have made them huge and curious, and they will swim out to meet you on almost any wall. Barracuda school over the pinnacles through summer, moray eels and octopus work the boulders, red coral colours the shaded walls, and in high summer there is an outside chance of a sunfish at the deeper sites.

destinations.name: Palau
destinations.currents: Tidal, strongest at full/new moon; Blue Corner (reef hooks, viz 28m+ incoming), Ulong Channel (high-speed drift), German Channel (gentle, manta cleaning), Peleliu (dangerous down-currents, dry-season only)
destinations.highlights: Blue Corner drift & reef-hook diving; lunar spawning aggregations.

Aug.conditions: wet with strong west winds and reduced visibility

destinations.description: Tides drive everything in Palau. Water pours through this Micronesian archipelago's reef passes, and at Blue Corner divers hook into the reef lip while grey reef sharks parade past in the flow; German Channel trades speed for manta rays circling a cleaning station. Between drifts come the vertical shafts of Blue Holes, the air-filled chambers of Chandelier Cave and lunar spawning aggregations. Day boats and liveaboards both work, but the strong currents flatter experienced divers.
destinations.underwater: Tidal current is the engine here: you drop onto the reef lip at Blue Corner, set a reef hook and let flow of up to three knots stream you like a flag while the show comes to you. Between drifts the terrain turns hollow — four shafts drop into the huge cavern of Blue Holes, Siaes Tunnel bores through the wall at 20-40 m, and in Chandelier Cave you surface among stalactites. Water holds 28-30°C, with 30-40 m visibility in the settled early months.
destinations.encounters: Grey reef sharks parade nonstop at the corners, and mantas circle German Channel's cleaning station in every season, feeding in formation when the plankton thickens. Palau runs on a lunar calendar: red snapper spawn on the full moon, bumphead parrotfish mass on the new moon in spring, camouflage grouper erupt in early summer — each event pulling in predators, with whale sharks cruising past in the dry season.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Luka Peternel (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Truk (Chuuk) Lagoon

destinations.description: History rests on the floor of Truk Lagoon. A 1944 air raid caught Japan's wartime supply fleet at anchor inside this Micronesian barrier reef, and more than 50 ships sank into calm, sheltered water where they still lie, holds packed with fighter planes, tanks and trucks, hulls dressed in coral. Mild currents and no thermocline keep conditions simple; depth sets the challenge, from 9 m deck tours to technical penetrations past 50 m. Liveaboards maximize bottom time.
destinations.underwater: Every dive is a wreck dive, and the lagoon keeps it simple: water sits near 29°C from surface to seabed with no thermocline, current is negligible, and depth is the only real variable. You can tour the Fujikawa Maru's Zero fighters between 9 and 34 m, hover over the Betty Bomber's intact fuselage on sand at 18 m, or push past 50 m on technical dives to the tank-laden San Francisco Maru. Dry-season visibility runs 24-32 m; the wet season roughly halves it.
destinations.encounters: The ships themselves are the ecosystem: more than fifty hulls dressed in coral, their guns and masts wrapped in clouds of reef fish. Reef sharks haunt the deeper wrecks, turtles graze the encrusted superstructures, and eagle rays cross the sandy channels between hulls, with an occasional manta gliding through the lagoon in the drier months.

destinations.name: Cenotes of Yucatán Peninsula
destinations.currents: Virtually none; still freshwater with haloclines in caverns and caves
destinations.highlights: Crystal-clear freshwater caverns with stalactites, haloclines and dramatic light beams

Feb.marine_life: geological formations glowing under light beams while the halocline shimmers between fresh and salt water

Mar.marine_life: light beams cutting through the cavern dark while stalactites hang over the passages
Mar.conditions: clear water with the strongest light beams of the year

Apr.marine_life: the halocline shimmering at depth while dramatic geology frames every swim-through
Apr.conditions: clear water with warming surface air

May.marine_life: light beams strengthening through the openings while formations glow in the still fresh water
May.conditions: clear water with hotter days and busier sites

Jun.marine_life: geological wonders unchanged below while the halocline blurs the line between layers
Jun.conditions: rains beginning while the water stays clear

Jul.marine_life: light beams flooding the cavern mouths while stalactites drip toward mirror-still pools
Jul.conditions: rainy season though the water stays clear

Aug.marine_life: the halocline rippling like oil while limestone formations arch over the tunnels
Aug.conditions: wet season with occasional sediment stirred in

Sep.marine_life: geological passages glowing where light beams punch through the jungle canopy openings
Sep.conditions: heaviest rains of the year with possible tannins

Oct.conditions: rains easing with clarity returning

Nov.marine_life: light beams sharpening as the sun angles low while formations gleam in clear water
Nov.conditions: dry season with crystal clear water

Dec.conditions: clear water and ideal settled conditions

dive_sites.blurb: Two connected 'eyes'; the world's most famous cavern dive, Barbie and Bat Cave lines

dive_sites.blurb: 119 m sinkhole; light beams pierce a hydrogen-sulphide cloud around 30 m

destinations.description: Beneath the Yucatan's jungle, rain has dissolved the limestone into vast flooded cave systems, and cenotes are the sinkhole windows that let divers in. Expect gin-clear fresh water with virtually no current, cathedral light beams, stalactite-hung passages and haloclines that blur vision like oil in water. Guided cavern lines at Dos Ojos and Gran Cenote welcome any certified diver; El Pit and Angelita, with their eerie sulfide clouds at 30 m, reward deeper experience.
destinations.underwater: You descend through a jungle sinkhole into water that never moves and barely changes: 25°C every month, with winter visibility reaching 60 m. Cross the halocline and the diver ahead smears into a shimmer until you sink beneath it. Each cavern has its own trick - a breathable air dome at Chac Mool, fossils in Taj Mahal's limestone halls, bell-shaped speleothems in Zapote's deep funnel, lily pads glowing over the entrance at Car Wash. A 5 mm suit earns its keep on long, slow dives.
destinations.encounters: Life here is sparse by design; the geology is the encounter. Catfish shoal in the open pools, blind cave fish hover where daylight gives out, turtles paddle the shallows at Gran Cenote, and the odd tarpon patrols mangrove-lined Casa Cenote. The other residents are the light beams: strongest in the dry months when the water is clearest, they slant through the cavern mouths like stage lighting.

destinations.name: Azores
destinations.currents: Offshore seamounts have strong currents; pelagic drift dives
destinations.highlights: Atlantic pelagic diving - blue/mako sharks and mantas at seamounts

Feb.marine_life: resident reef fish holding the ledges while the islands wait out the Atlantic winter

dive_sites.name: Princess Alice Bank
dive_sites.blurb: Open-ocean seamount ~45nm off Pico; mobula rays and pelagics in 30m+ viz

dive_sites.name: Formigas / Dollabarat Bank
dive_sites.blurb: Remote marine-reserve islets and bank; mantas, sharks and huge fish schools

dive_sites.name: Gruta dos Camarões
dive_sites.blurb: Shrimp-filled cave off Faial with entrance at 35m; cave-trained divers only

destinations.description: Nine volcanic Portuguese islands anchor the mid-Atlantic, and their diving happens in serious blue water. Offshore seamounts like Princess Alice Bank draw squadrons of mobula rays, Condor Bank stages blue and mako shark encounters, and visibility routinely tops 30 meters. Currents run strong out there, so the banks are boat trips for confident divers, though coastal wrecks and Terceira's anchor graveyard offer easier days. July through October is prime season.
destinations.underwater: This is blue-water diving: a long run offshore, a descent down the line with nothing but open Atlantic beneath you, and a seamount top materialising at 35-40 m in visibility that regularly hits 30 m. Currents push hard across Princess Alice and Condor Banks, so you hold the line and let the pelagics come to you. Water climbs from 17°C in winter to about 24°C in late summer; closer in, the Dori wreck at 9-20 m and Terceira's anchor-strewn seabed give calmer, shallower days.
destinations.encounters: Summer is the show: blue sharks work the banks from July into September, makos join them at Condor, and squadrons of mobula rays school over Ambrósio and Princess Alice from late spring. Mola mola drift past the seamounts and dolphins often escort the boats out. Through winter the pelagics thin, leaving groupers and morays holding the volcanic ledges.

dive_sites.name: The Knoll Pins

dive_sites.name: Gannets' Bay

dive_sites.name: MV Robert
dive_sites.blurb: Intact 1975 coaster upright at 25m off the east coast, Lundy's best wreck

dive_sites.name: HMS Montagu

dive_sites.name: Iona II

dive_sites.name: Brazen Ward

destinations.description: A granite outpost in the Bristol Channel, Lundy became the UK's first marine reserve, and its grey seals treat divers as entertainment, tugging fins in the shallows of Gannets' Bay. The Knoll Pins pile on color with jewel anemones and rare cup corals, while the battleship HMS Montagu and the upright coaster MV Robert add wreck history. Tides run hard here, so day boats work the slack, June through September.
destinations.underwater: This is cold-water diving timed to the tide: boats drop you at slack, because between windows the Bristol Channel runs hard over the reefs. Underwater it is granite — kelp-topped gullies, boulder slopes and the twin Knoll Pins coated in jewel anemones and rare cup corals. Visibility runs 8-15 m and summer water peaks near 16°C, so a drysuit earns its keep. The MV Robert sits upright and intact at 25 m; the Montagu's battleship wreckage scatters the shallows off Shutter Point.
destinations.encounters: Grey seals own the show, and they come to you — in the sheltered eastern coves they nose at fins and spiral around divers, boldest at the height of summer. Jewel anemones and nudibranchs supply the colour, rare spiny lobsters haunt the exposed western gullies, and on calm July and August days a sunfish may drift past offshore.

destinations.description: In 1919 the interned German High Seas Fleet was scuttled in this Orkney anchorage, leaving battleships and light cruisers scattered across the seabed, still bristling with guns. The dreadnoughts lie deep, 38 to 45 meters, and reward advanced and technical divers; cruisers like SMS Karlsruhe and the light-filled Burra Sound blockships keep recreational depths honest. Cold, dark, and utterly compelling, it is dived by hardboat from Stromness, May to September.
destinations.underwater: Everything is done under a drysuit and torchlight. Descend onto a battleship's upturned hull - Kronprinz Wilhelm's 12-inch guns still under a century of growth - and plumose anemones carpet every rail and capstan in feeding white. Water holds 7 to 14°C, viz a modest 8 to 15 m, clearest in the cold months. Seals grow bold on the shallower wrecks in summer, buzzing divers mid-dive. The scale of the steel looming out of the dark is the whole point.
destinations.encounters: The wildlife here is the wrecks themselves, colonised into artificial reef. Plumose anemones smother the steel in white and orange, thickest through the summer feeding months, softening every gun and railing. Seals are the charismatic draw, growing bold around the shallower hulls from spring onward and often escorting divers across a dive. Inside the hulls, fish life is sparse but atmospheric.

dive_sites.name: Silfra Big Crack
dive_sites.blurb: Silfra's narrowest section, drifting between the tectonic plates

dive_sites.name: Silfra Hall
dive_sites.blurb: Wide section where the 100m+ glacial visibility truly opens up

dive_sites.name: Silfra Cathedral
dive_sites.blurb: 23m-deep canyon, Silfra's most dramatic flight over the rift

dive_sites.name: Silfra Lagoon
dive_sites.blurb: Shallow exit lagoon with algae fields and views across 100m of water

dive_sites.name: Davíðsgjá
dive_sites.blurb: Silfra's darker sibling, a long 21m-deep fissure in Lake Þingvallavatn

destinations.description: Glacial meltwater, filtered through lava for decades, fills this crack between the North American and Eurasian tectonic plates in Iceland's Thingvellir National Park, delivering visibility beyond 100 meters. You drift gently through the Big Crack, over the 23-meter Cathedral canyon, and out across the algae-green lagoon. There is almost no life to watch; the geology is the show. A drysuit shore dive in 2-4 C water, easy enough for any certified diver.
destinations.underwater: The cold reaches your lips first, 2-4°C in every season under a drysuit, and then the clarity registers: rock walls sharp at 100 m, as if the water were not there. A faint current carries you between the plates with barely a fin kick, from the tight squeeze of the Big Crack into the wide-open Hall and down the Cathedral's canyon to 23 m. Davidsgja, a darker 21 m fissure in the lake nearby, adds a second dive for those who want more.
destinations.encounters: Silfra inverts the usual equation: the visibility is the wildlife. Beyond the neon-green 'troll hair' algae carpeting the shallow lagoon and catching the light like a meadow, there is virtually nothing living to watch; this is a pure geology dive between the North American and Eurasian plates. Under the long daylight of the summer months, the light alone fills the dive.

destinations.description: Cold, current-fed Pacific water turns this British Columbia coastline into an invertebrate riot: Browning Wall is carpeted stem to stern in soft corals and anemones, wolf eels and giant Pacific octopus den in the rocks, and Steller sea lions buzz divers at Race Rocks. Big artificial reefs like HMCS Saskatchewan add wreck steel. Slack-window boat diving in a drysuit, at its clearest October through March, with Ogden Point offering an easy shore option.
destinations.underwater: Dives are timed to the tide table: you drop at slack in cold 7-16°C drysuit water, onto rock so thickly overgrown it hides the stone — plumose anemones, cloud sponges and soft coral from 10 m down. Winter is the reward season, when the plankton dies back and visibility opens to 20-30 m over all that colour. For adrenaline there are tidal passes like Dodd Narrows, where flow can hit 9 knots; for steel, the 134 m HMCS Cape Breton at 21-43 m.
destinations.encounters: Wolf eels grin from their dens and pair up in late winter, while giant Pacific octopus are at their boldest in December, hunting the reef in the open. Steller sea lions mob divers at Race Rocks in fast, curious gangs from October, harbour seals shadow you along Snake Island Wall, and nudibranchs speckle the walls right through the coldest months.

destinations.description: Tidal currents ripping through the Somosomo Strait feed the densest soft-coral gardens in the Pacific, and when the white corals on the Great White Wall open to feed, the whole reef seems to cascade. Beqa Lagoon counters with a famous baited shark dive that brings in big bull sharks, while Namena's Grand Central Station funnels barracuda and grey reefs past like commuters. Resort day boats and liveaboards cover it all; July through October is best.
destinations.underwater: Fiji's best dives are timed to the tide. In the Somosomo Strait you drift walls like the Great White Wall as the current switches on and the soft corals inflate into full colour; miss the flow and the reef stays half-closed. Water runs 25-29°C in a 3mm, visibility can pass 30 m in the July-October dry season, and at Beqa Lagoon you settle in at 15-30 m while the shark dive unfolds in front of you.
destinations.encounters: Bull sharks dominate the Beqa Lagoon feed, part of a cast of up to eight shark species, and grey reef sharks pack tidal passes like Nigali year-round. Manta rays arrive in the channels once the dry season clears the water, barrel-rolling through plankton lines from May to October, while Namena's Grand Central Station streams barracuda and trevally past in dense schools.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo via Pexels

destinations.description: The Tuamotu atolls concentrate the drift diving of your life into their passes: ride the incoming tide through Rangiroa's Tiputa alongside a hundred grey reef sharks and resident dolphins, or hang at Fakarava South while the famous wall of sharks stacks overhead. Bora Bora adds a lagoon manta cleaning station for calmer days. The big passes reward divers comfortable in current, reached by resort day boats or liveaboard, June through October.
destinations.underwater: This is pass diving: you drop outside the reef on the incoming tide and let it fire you through the cut into the lagoon. In Rangiroa's Tiputa Pass the current can be mask-ripping; Fakarava South is gentler, letting you hold at 5-30 m while sharks stack overhead. Water runs a warm 26-29°C under a 3mm suit, and in the June-October dry season visibility opens to 30-40 m of clear blue. Sheltered pinnacles like The Aquarium fill the slack-tide windows.
destinations.encounters: Grey reef sharks are the currency here: a hundred-plus riding the current in Tiputa, hundreds more stacked into Fakarava's wall of sharks, thickest in the dry season when the grouper spawn draws hunters into the pass. Resident dolphins work Tiputa, silvertips hold Avatoru, lemon sharks make close passes off Bora Bora and Tahiti, mantas visit lagoon cleaning stations, and from July humpbacks sing offshore.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: The TerraMar Project (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Guadalcanal & Western Province
destinations.currents: Generally mild reef currents; some channels and passages drift
destinations.highlights: WWII wrecks and pristine reefs with rich macro life

Jan.marine_life: reef sharks cruising the points while macro life crowds the coral slopes
Jan.conditions: wet season with variable visibility

Feb.marine_life: mandarinfish rising to mate at dusk while reef life carries on through the rains
Feb.conditions: the heart of the wet and cyclone season

Mar.marine_life: reef sharks working the channels while macro subjects keep the muck sites busy
Mar.conditions: wet season with lower visibility

Apr.marine_life: reef sharks patrolling the drop-offs while the war wrecks emerge from clearing water

dive_sites.name: Toa Maru (Gizo)
dive_sites.blurb: 140m Japanese transport, the Solomons' finest recreational wreck, 12-37m

dive_sites.name: Grand Central Station (Njari Island)
dive_sites.blurb: 270+ fish species recorded on a single dive at this current-swept point

destinations.description: The Solomon Islands wear their WWII history underwater: beached Japanese transports at Bonegi drop from shore-entry shallows to 55 meters, an intact B-17 bomber rests off Honiara, and Gizo's Toa Maru ranks among the Pacific's finest recreational wrecks. Out in the Western Province, reefs like Njari's Grand Central Station have logged 270-plus fish species on a single dive. Mild currents, mandarinfish macro, and empty sites make this a rewarding resort or liveaboard trip, April through November.
destinations.underwater: You can wade off a Guadalcanal beach and drop straight onto a Japanese transport: the Hirokawa Maru starts at 5 m and slides down to 55 m, while an intact B-17 bomber rests in 10-20 m off Honiara. Out west the diving shifts to current-swept reef points like Grand Central Station and the 140 m Toa Maru at Gizo. Water holds a steady 28-29°C year-round, currents stay mostly mild, and dry-season visibility opens beyond 30 m.
destinations.encounters: Grey reef sharks work the points and channels, hammerheads hang deeper off Munda's Shark Point, and barracuda spiral in silver towers overhead. Njari's Grand Central Station has logged more than 270 fish species on a single dive, mandarinfish rise to mate at dusk in the shallows, and turtles and rare critters keep the sheltered macro sites busy.

destinations.description: Just across the channel from Tahiti, Moorea keeps its diving relaxed: mild currents, sun-drenched coral canyons off Opunohu Bay, and lemon sharks that cruise in close at Tiki Point among blacktips and greys. Eagle rays file down lagoon corridors, turtles are everywhere, and from July through October humpback song carries through the water on nearly every dive. Easy day-boat sites in the 10-20 meter range suit newer divers, with the deep Rose Garden reserved for advanced ones.
destinations.underwater: Diving stays mostly in the friendly 10-20 m band: coral canyons in the north off Opunohu Bay, sandy lagoon corridors where eagle rays file past in squadrons, and the relaxed east-coast reef at Temae. A carved tiki statue stands at 13 m on the northwest point, and advanced divers can drop to 40 m onto a field of rose-shaped Montipora corals off Opunohu Pass. Water holds 26-29°C, current is minimal with some surge, and dry-season visibility reaches 30 m.
destinations.encounters: Sharks set the rhythm: big lemon sharks patrol the outer slope and the northwest point, blacktips dart across the shallow lagoon, and grey reef sharks work the passes. Eagle rays glide through in formation and turtles graze among the coral heads. From July to October humpbacks move through, at their most abundant in September, breaching and singing just beyond the reef.

dive_sites.name: Rikoriko Cave
dive_sites.blurb: World's largest sea cave by volume; 20m+ at the entrance

dive_sites.name: Blue Maomao Arch
dive_sites.blurb: Signature swim-through packed with schooling blue maomao

dive_sites.name: Northern Arch
dive_sites.blurb: Vertical arch to 38m; stingrays hover mid-water in the current

dive_sites.name: Middle Arch
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered arch with kelp, nudibranchs, snapper and demoiselles

dive_sites.name: Tie Dye Arch

dive_sites.blurb: Winter aggregations of bronze whaler sharks in peak visibility

destinations.description: Two volcanic islands off New Zealand's Northland coast, the Poor Knights sit inside a strict marine reserve washed by a warm subtropical current. The diving happens in arches, tunnels and sea caves - Rikoriko is the world's largest by volume - where stingrays hover mid-water, blue maomao school in the light beams, and kelp-fringed walls crawl with nudibranchs. Boat-only day trips run year-round, best January through April, with sites from sheltered arches to pinnacles dropping past 45m.
destinations.underwater: Volcanic rock does the work: you fin through arches and tunnels where the light goes cathedral-blue, drop into the huge dome of Rikoriko Cave, and follow walls that fall from kelp into sponge-crusted rock. Northern Arch is the test piece, a vertical slot to 38 m where you hang in open water among the rays as current pushes through. Summer brings 21-22°C water and visibility near 30 m; by late winter it is down to 15°C and a thick suit.
destinations.encounters: Stingrays are the signature, gathering in the arches through the summer months while kingfish charge the demoiselle schools and mola mola drift past the pinnacles. Winter compensates in miniature and in kind: nudibranchs bloom across the walls, morays stay out hunting, and bronze whaler sharks aggregate at Cream Garden. Snapper and subtropical reef fish hold the sheltered arches all year.

destinations.description: One base, two completely different dives: day boats cross from Dumaguete to Apo Island's protected coral gardens, where green turtles graze in real numbers and drifts like Coconut Point spin with schooling jacks, then return to Dauin's dark-sand muck slopes for frogfish, ghost pipefish and sunset mandarinfish. Currents at Apo run mild to moderate while the muck sites stay calm and shallow, which makes this stretch of the Philippines ideal for photographers and mixed-ability groups.
destinations.underwater: You dive two seabeds in one day here. Off Apo, coral gardens start at 3 m and run out to points where the current takes over — Coconut Point can spin like its washing-machine nickname while jacks wheel overhead — and Chapel Point's wall is cut with crevices and small caverns. Back on Dauin's dark sand everything turns calm, shallow and slow, a patient hunt across rubble and mooring blocks. The water holds 27-29°C year-round, with visibility past 30 m in the flat spring months.
destinations.encounters: Green turtles are everywhere on Apo's sanctuary reef, resting on coral heads while trevally and snapper school off the points. The Dauin slopes carry the macro weight: frogfish, ghost pipefish, seahorses and parading nudibranchs, plus mimic, blue-ring and coconut octopus at Secret Corner — octopus activity peaks around the October mating season. At dusk, mandarinfish spiral up over the rubble.

destinations.description: The walls at Bunaken drop sheer from a shallow reef crest into deep Sulawesi blue, and that vertical geometry defines the diving. Drifting along the Lekuan faces - locals call them the city of turtles - you pass green and hawksbill turtles at a rate that stops being remarkable by day two, while sea fans hide pygmy seahorses and visibility runs long. Day boats out of Manado reach everything, and the mild wall currents suit newer divers.
destinations.underwater: The walls fall away vertically from a shallow crest into deep blue, and drifting those faces at 28 to 29°C is the whole experience. Mild currents nudge you along Sachiko's Point and the Mandolin drop-off, where gorgonians reach off the rock and viz can stretch past 30 m in the dry months. Fukui breaks the pattern - a gentler coral slope with garden eels swaying in the sand and a row of giant Tridacna clams wedged into the reef.
destinations.encounters: Turtles define Bunaken - green and hawksbill both, dozing on ledges and cruising the walls so often they stop feeling like an event by the second day. Barracuda hang in schools over the drop-offs, reef fish swarm the coral shelves, and the sea fans hide pygmy seahorses for a patient eye. The dry-season months bring the clearest water and the most fish.

destinations.description: Three small coral-fringed islets off Lombok's northwest coast, the Gilis deliver Indonesia at its most relaxed: warm, easy reefs where green and hawksbill turtles are all but guaranteed - Turtle Heaven stacks them on a single pinnacle. Short day-boat rides reach sloping reefs, the coral-crusted Bounty Wreck and gentle walls like Meno's, with drift and channel sites adding pace when the current runs. It is an ideal place to learn or ease back into diving.
destinations.underwater: Expect warm, unhurried diving: 27-29°C year-round in a 3mm suit, over sloping reefs and coral heads that rarely demand more than easy finning. When the Lombok Strait current runs, Halik's ridges and canyons turn into a proper drift, and Deep Turbo drops you onto sea-fan-covered seamounts at 24-32 m with garden eels rippling over the sand. Han's Reef swaps scenery for critter hunting on the rubble. The signature moment is a green turtle lifting off a coral head close enough to fill your mask.
destinations.encounters: Green and hawksbill turtles are the constant, grazing, sleeping and queuing at cleaning stations at nearly every site. Whitetip reef sharks rest under ledges and patrol the deeper slopes, cuttlefish hover and flare over the rubble, and Han's Reef delivers seahorses, frogfish, ribbon eels and octopus. The dry months from May to September bring the clearest water and the busiest reefs.

dive_sites.name: Chocolate Island
dive_sites.blurb: Shallow soft-coral islet packed with macro critters

destinations.description: Pre-dawn boats leave this small island off Cebu for one reason: pelagic thresher sharks rising from the deep to cleaning stations on sunken shoals, a near-daily encounter found almost nowhere else. The threshers now gather at Kimud Shoal, while Monad - the original site - draws tiger sharks. Between dawn runs there is real variety: Gato Island's swim-through tunnel, the Dona Marilyn ferry wreck and sunset mandarinfish at Lighthouse. Early starts and deeper shoal dives favor comfortable, experienced divers.
destinations.underwater: The alarm goes off in the dark: thresher boats leave before sunrise so you're settled on Kimud Shoal at 18-30 m as first light greys the water above. Currents on the shoal stay gentle outside the full moon, and 27-29°C water keeps a 3mm comfortable through three or four dives. The rest of the day is gentler still — whitetips and sea snakes in rocky swim-throughs, steep coral walls off Calanggaman's sandbar, soft-coral shallows at Chocolate Island.
destinations.encounters: Pelagic thresher sharks are the constant, rising from deep water to the cleaning stations at dawn in every month of the year. March to May adds mantas and devil rays gliding over the shoal, and Monad now draws tiger sharks. Inshore it's a macro island: seahorses in the seagrass, and mandarinfish pairing off at Lighthouse as the sun drops.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Vyacheslav Argenberg (CC BY 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: Granite defines the Similans: huge smooth boulders tumble from white beaches to well past 40m, forming the swim-throughs and canyons of Elephant Head Rock on the exposed west, with gentle coral bommies and sand on the east. Liveaboards work the chain during the short open season, pushing north to Koh Bon's manta cleaning station and Richelieu Rock, a soft-coral pinnacle with Thailand's best whale shark odds. February to April brings the calmest Andaman seas and the biggest animals.
destinations.underwater: Granite sets the terrain: smooth boulders the size of houses stack into swim-throughs and canyons that drop past 40 m at Elephant Head Rock and Deep Six, torchlit gaps opening suddenly into blue. The east side is gentler, coral bommies on white sand at Anita's Reef. Water holds 28-30°C in a 3mm, and in the February-April calm visibility runs beyond 30 m. The season is short: the park closes for the monsoon from mid-May to mid-October.
destinations.encounters: Mantas circle the cleaning station on Koh Bon's west ridge, and Richelieu Rock gives Thailand's best whale shark odds, strongest from February to April. Leopard sharks doze on the sand throughout the open season, napoleon wrasse patrol Shark Fin Reef's long ridge, and clouds of reef fish and the odd octopus keep the granite busy between the big visitors.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Lakshmi Sawitri (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.description: Marooned in the middle of the Sulu Sea, Tubbataha's two atolls and Jessie Beazley Reef are reachable only by liveaboard during a short season peaking April to June. The reward is the Philippines at full strength: sheer walls draped in gorgonians, grey reef and whitetip sharks on nearly every dive, and mantas or hammerheads riding the flow at sites like Washing Machine. Strong oceanic currents make drift diving the norm, so this UNESCO park suits experienced divers.
destinations.underwater: You dive Tubbataha from a live boat, drifting sheer walls that drop out of sight into the Sulu Sea, gorgonians fanning off faces like Amos Rock and current running on nearly every dive. The water is 28-30°C, and in the April-May heart of the short season visibility stretches past 30 m over the drop-offs. Sites shift character fast: a broad plateau where whitetips rest at Shark Airport, confused water at the aptly named Washing Machine, wreckage swarmed by snappers at Delsan.
destinations.encounters: Grey reef and whitetip sharks are on nearly every dive — resting in ranks on Shark Airport's plateau, stacking in the current along the walls — while mantas glide past and jacks spin into tornadoes overhead. Washing Machine's unpredictable water can pull in hammerheads, whale sharks cruise the reef edges in the calm mid-season, and the Malayan Wreck adds nudibranchs and crustaceans for macro eyes.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Ron Van Oers (CC BY-SA 3.0 igo) via Wikimedia Commons

dive_sites.name: Ball's Pyramid
dive_sites.blurb: World's tallest sea stack; Galapagos whalers, kingfish, rare angelfish

dive_sites.name: Admiralty Islands
dive_sites.blurb: Volcanic islets with 30+ sites of pinnacles, canyons and caves

dive_sites.name: Erscott's Hole
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered 8m lagoon hole; doubleheader wrasse and spangled emperor

dive_sites.name: Comets Hole
dive_sites.blurb: Coral-ringed lagoon hole teeming with resident reef fish

dive_sites.name: Tenth of June

dive_sites.name: Rupert's Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Classic outer-reef coral dive on the world's southernmost coral reef

dive_sites.name: Deacon's Delight
dive_sites.blurb: Popular boat-dive reef among Lord Howe's 60+ charted sites

destinations.description: Six hundred kilometers off the Australian mainland, Lord Howe grows the world's southernmost coral reef, and the crossover shows underwater: tropical corals and temperate species share sites with fish found nowhere else, like the doubleheader wrasse. Sheltered lagoon holes give easy 8m dives, the volcanic Admiralty Islands stack up pinnacles, canyons and caves, and boat runs out to Ball's Pyramid - the world's tallest sea stack - bring Galapagos whalers and hunting kingfish. December through May is prime.
destinations.underwater: The lagoon eases you in: sheltered coral holes like Erscott's and Comets bottom out at 8-10 m, ringed by resident fish. Boat sites raise the stakes - the volcanic Admiralty islets stack pinnacles, canyons, caves and swim-throughs in 10-30 m, and the run out to Ball's Pyramid, the world's tallest sea stack, means strong drift over reef at 15-30 m. This is subtropical diving: 25°C with 30 m visibility at summer's height, 18°C in a 5 mm suit by late winter.
destinations.encounters: Galapagos whaler sharks own the warm months, patrolling the reef passes and sometimes buzzing divers on the descent, while kingfish blast through the bait schools. On the world's southernmost coral reef, tropical and temperate fish share the same dive, headlined by the doubleheader wrasse of the lagoon holes, with grazing turtles and Spanish dancers rounding out the cast.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Photographer: Richard Ling <wikipedia@rling.com> (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: The drawcard at this New South Wales mid-north-coast town is a single rock with a 125m cave running clean through it. Divers enter Fish Rock's deep entrance, pass wobbegongs and bull rays in the dark, and exit the shallow end into gutters patrolled by critically endangered grey nurse sharks - sometimes twenty or thirty at once. Tidal currents and surge demand some experience, and grey nurse numbers peak May to December on straightforward day-boat runs.
destinations.underwater: This is warm-temperate diving with an edge: 19°C and a thick wetsuit in late winter, 24-25°C when the East Australian Current pushes clear blue water down in summer, and tidal current and surge that keep it an advanced destination. The centrepiece dive threads Fish Rock's 125 m tunnel between 12 and 24 m, torch-lit the whole way. Outside, The Pinnacle's sheer walls overlook sandy gutters, the fish-packed Aquarium corner sits by the shallow entrance, and Black Rock adds morays, lionfish and schooling morwong.
destinations.encounters: Grey nurse sharks are the reason most divers come, aggregating at Fish Rock from May through December and hovering almost motionless in the gutters while you settle in behind them. Wobbegongs and bull rays hold the darker recesses of the cave, black cod lurk along the reef, and green turtles and eagle rays ride in on the warm current through summer. In winter, humpback whales pass offshore on migration.

destinations.name: Nusa Penida
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm (bring 5mm for Jul-Oct upwellings, which can drop to 15-18C at Crystal Bay depths)
destinations.current_note: Fast drifts and occasional down-currents at Crystal Bay, Toyapakeh and the north-coast points
destinations.currents: The Indonesian Throughflow funnels between the islands, driving fast, shifting drifts and sudden down-currents that demand solid buoyancy and guide-led planning.
destinations.highlights: Oceanic sunfish on cold winter upwellings and reef mantas cruising the cleaning stations all year, wrapped in some of Bali's wildest drift diving.
