dive_sites.name: Crystal Bay
dive_sites.blurb: The classic mola-mola site, where cold Jul-Oct upwellings and possible down-currents bring sunfish to cleaning stations on the deep slope.

dive_sites.name: Manta Point
dive_sites.blurb: Reef mantas glide over cleaning stations year-round on the exposed south coast, often in surge and swell.

dive_sites.name: Manta Bay
dive_sites.blurb: A shallow bay where schooling reef mantas feed and get cleaned, a calmer alternative to Manta Point.

dive_sites.name: Toyapakeh
dive_sites.blurb: A vivid coral wall in the channel with fast currents and occasional down-currents, and a chance of mola-mola in the deep.

dive_sites.name: SD Point
dive_sites.blurb: A north-coast high-speed drift over sloping reef with turtles, reef sharks and big schools when the current runs.

dive_sites.name: Gamat Bay
dive_sites.blurb: A sheltered white-sand bay with coral bommies, strong macro life and clear water, best for calmer conditions.

dive_sites.name: Ceningan Wall
dive_sites.blurb: A dramatic drop-off between Penida and Ceningan with strong currents, big fans and pelagic action for experienced divers.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Andrepiazza (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: Twenty minutes across the channel from Bali, Nusa Penida sits square in the Indonesian Throughflow, and its diving runs on that moving water: high-speed drifts along the north coast, reef mantas circling the south-coast cleaning stations all year, and - when cold upwellings arrive from July to October - oceanic sunfish rising to be cleaned at Crystal Bay. Currents shift fast and can pull downward, so dive it with sharp buoyancy and a guide who reads the water.
destinations.underwater: The current sets the tone. On the north coast you fly over sloping reef at SD Point; in the channel, Toyapakeh's coral wall demands respect for sudden down-currents. The south coast is different, surgey manta bays at 5-15 m and the deep slope of Crystal Bay, where dry-season upwellings drop the water from 29°C to around 24°C. That thermocline shiver is the tell: cold, clear water with 25 m-plus visibility is exactly when mola-mola climb to the cleaning stations.
destinations.encounters: Reef mantas work the south-coast cleaning stations in every month of the year, feeding hardest when wet-season plankton thickens the water. The oceanic sunfish are strictly seasonal, rising on the coldest dry-season upwellings. Between the headliners, whitetip reef sharks and eagle rays patrol the walls, bumphead parrotfish and hawksbill turtles ride the drifts, and Gamat Bay hides serious macro.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Chainwit. (CC BY 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Fuvahmulah
destinations.best_months: Dec-Apr (calm dry-season seas and best visibility; tiger sharks year-round)
destinations.current_note: open blue-water drop-offs, tide-driven
destinations.currents: This is exposed oceanic diving off a single deep-water island, where tidal currents sweep the drop-offs and blue-water dives are done as drifts.
destinations.highlights: The tiger shark capital of the world, where a shallow harbour plateau puts you eye-to-eye with resident tigers while deep drop-offs deliver threshers, oceanic mantas and schooling hammerheads.

Feb.marine_life: tiger sharks holding year-round while silvertips arrive in the hundreds with hammerhead schools and whale sharks
Feb.conditions: dry northeast monsoon with settled surface and gin-clear water

Mar.marine_life: tiger sharks daily while hammerheads school, oceanic mantas arrive, threshers clean at depth and whale sharks pass
Mar.conditions: late dry season with warm calm seas and top visibility

Apr.marine_life: tiger sharks holding year-round while oceanic mantas build toward mating with whale sharks and lingering hammerheads
Apr.conditions: tail of the dry northeast monsoon, calm before the seasonal change

May.marine_life: tiger sharks daily while oceanic mantas gather to mate, clean and feed
May.conditions: southwest monsoon setting in with warmest water and variable surface

Jun.conditions: wet southwest monsoon with rougher surface and reduced visibility

Jul.conditions: southwest monsoon, wetter and choppier with more current

Aug.conditions: wet southwest monsoon at full strength with unsettled surface and greener water

Sep.marine_life: tiger sharks daily while threshers stay reliable and some years extra pelagic action arrives
Sep.conditions: southwest monsoon easing, still wet with variable visibility

Oct.conditions: monsoon transition with seas settling and clarity improving

Nov.marine_life: resident tiger sharks daily while thresher season continues and hammerhead schools grow as the thermocline shallows
Nov.conditions: early dry northeast monsoon with calmer surface and rising visibility

Dec.marine_life: tiger sharks holding year-round while thresher sharks crowd the cleaning stations and hammerheads school dependably
Dec.conditions: northeast monsoon dry season returning with calm seas and clear water

dive_sites.name: Tiger Harbour
dive_sites.blurb: The harbour-mouth plateau that made Fuvahmulah famous, where named resident tiger sharks patrol at close range in shallow, clear water.

dive_sites.name: Tiger Zoo
dive_sites.blurb: The most consistent spot to sit with numbers of resident tigers throughout the year, often with silvertips and nurse sharks in the mix.

dive_sites.name: Farikede
dive_sites.blurb: Southern reef with thresher shark cleaning stations that rise from deep water, one of the rarest big-animal encounters in the Maldives.

dive_sites.name: Rasgefanno
dive_sites.blurb: Rocky-bottomed stage for tiger sharks alongside silvertips and leopard sharks, with oceanic mantas occasionally cruising past.

dive_sites.name: Neregando Fannu
dive_sites.blurb: Steep oceanic wall drifted in current for hammerhead schools, oceanic mantas and passing pelagics off the deep blue.

dive_sites.name: Thoondu
dive_sites.blurb: Northern point off the famous white-pebble beach, an exposed corner that channels current and pelagic traffic.

dive_sites.name: Razwa Faru
dive_sites.blurb: Southern deep reef and drop-off known for threshers on the cleaning stations plus silvertips and schooling hammerheads.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Ibrahim Asad (CC BY 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: A lone oceanic island in the Maldives' far south, Fuvahmulah has no sheltering atoll, just deep water on every side and the most reliable tiger shark diving on Earth. Named resident tigers patrol the shallow harbor plateau at arm's length, while drifts along the drop-offs deliver thresher shark cleaning stations, schooling hammerheads and oceanic mantas. Day boats run everything, and the exposed blue-water diving favors confident, current-comfortable divers.
destinations.underwater: There is no lagoon to hide in — the reef falls straight into oceanic depth on every side, so most dives run as live drifts along walls and drop-offs with the blue at your shoulder. The counterpoint is the harbour plateau: 5-12 m of clear water over rock where tiger sharks pass close enough to fill your mask. The water sits at 28-30°C year-round; visibility reaches 35 m in the northeast monsoon and drops nearer 25 m in the wet southwest season.
destinations.encounters: Resident tiger sharks show daily in every month — that is the guarantee. Around them the calendar rotates: thresher sharks rise to the deep cleaning stations most reliably from October into December, scalloped hammerheads school through the dry northeast monsoon, and oceanic mantas gather to clean, feed and mate around April and May. Silvertips can mass in the hundreds in February, with whale sharks passing through the dry months.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Ibrahim Asad (CC BY 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Layang Layang
destinations.current_note: Wall sites can run strong; sheltered lagoon dives are calm
destinations.currents: Currents range from mild inside the atoll to strong along the exposed drop-offs where the pelagics gather.
destinations.highlights: A remote oceanic atoll where walls plunge 2,000 m and springtime brings schooling scalloped hammerheads in gin-clear water.

Jan.conditions: northeast monsoon leaves the island unreachable

Feb.conditions: monsoon tailing off but the resort stays closed

Apr.conditions: calmest month with superb visibility

May.marine_life: hammerhead schooling continues while dogtooth tuna and barracuda hunt the walls
May.conditions: excellent visibility, warm and settled seas

Jun.marine_life: reef sharks and turtles cruising while big schools of jacks and tuna sweep past
Jun.conditions: reliable wall diving as hammerheads thin out

Jul.marine_life: grey reef sharks patrolling with mantas gliding by and resident pelagics on the walls
Jul.conditions: steady conditions with good visibility

Aug.marine_life: reef sharks and turtles cruising while macro life dots the reef flats
Aug.conditions: season winding down with occasional wind and swell

Sep.marine_life: no access with the resort closed
Sep.conditions: resort closes for the monsoon

Oct.marine_life: no access with the resort closed
Oct.conditions: monsoon building leaves the island unreachable

Nov.marine_life: no access with the resort closed
Nov.conditions: northeast monsoon with rough seas

Dec.conditions: peak monsoon with no operators running

dive_sites.name: Gorgonian Forest
dive_sites.blurb: Sea-fan draped drop-off with strong current - the prime spot for schooling hammerheads

dive_sites.name: Dogtooth Lair
dive_sites.blurb: Deep-water wall patrolled by big dogtooth tuna, barracuda and jacks

dive_sites.name: The Valley
dive_sites.blurb: Hard-coral slope with snappers, sweetlips and turtles; hammerheads pass in spring

dive_sites.name: Shark's Cave
dive_sites.blurb: Overhangs sheltering resting whitetip, nurse and leopard sharks

dive_sites.name: D-Wall
dive_sites.blurb: Sheer vertical wall dropping into 2,000 m of blue - a magnet for pelagics

dive_sites.name: Wreck Point
dive_sites.blurb: Reef corner near the resort channel with reef sharks and cruising trevally

dive_sites.name: Snapper Ledges
dive_sites.blurb: Easy sheltered coral garden thick with snapper and colourful reef fish

destinations.image_credit: Photo: matthew lee (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: Stranded in the South China Sea far off Borneo, Layang Layang is a tiny oceanic atoll whose walls fall away 2,000 m into open blue. Divers come in spring, when schooling scalloped hammerheads rise along sea-fan-draped drop-offs like Gorgonian Forest, joined by dogtooth tuna and passing mantas. Inside the lagoon the diving stays calm and easy; outside, current-swept walls reward experience. Everything runs from the atoll's single fly-in resort.
destinations.underwater: These are oceanic walls dropping into thousands of metres of blue, and diving them means committing to current-swept edges far from anywhere. Dogtooth Lair is patrolled by big tuna and jacks, Shark's Cave shelters whitetip, nurse and leopard sharks resting under its overhangs, and the water runs a warm 28 to 29°C with visibility that can hit 40 m in April. The clarity is the standout - you see the pelagics coming from a long way off.
destinations.encounters: Spring is all about the hammerheads - scalloped hammerheads school along the walls from March into May, the reason most divers make the long trip out. Around them cruise grey reef sharks, dogtooth tuna, barracuda and the occasional manta gliding the drop-offs. As the hammerheads thin through summer, resident reef sharks and green turtles carry the diving until the monsoon closes the atoll.

destinations.name: Malpelo Island
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm (7mm or hooded for Dec-May thermoclines down to ~14C)
destinations.current_note: Fierce; reef hooks and drift skills essential
destinations.currents: Powerful, shifting currents and surge scour the exposed pinnacles and walls, so reef hooks, good buoyancy and solid drift-diving skills are mandatory.
destinations.highlights: Walls of 200-plus schooling scalloped hammerheads, silky shark hordes and the rare smalltooth sand tiger on a remote volcanic seamount 500km offshore.

Jan.marine_life: scalloped hammerhead schools stacking up while smalltooth sand tiger rises and Galapagos sharks patrol
Jan.conditions: dry season with strong currents, cool thermoclines and plankton cutting visibility

Feb.marine_life: dense hammerhead schools swirling while smalltooth sand tiger holds at El Monstruo and Galapagos sharks cruise
Feb.conditions: dry season with powerful currents, cold thermoclines and greenish plankton-rich water

Mar.marine_life: hammerhead schools still swirling in numbers while smalltooth sand tiger and Galapagos sharks hold
Mar.conditions: dry season winding down with strong currents, cool water and moderate visibility

Apr.marine_life: hammerheads holding while silky shark numbers build and eagle rays glide the currents
Apr.conditions: transition with easing currents, warming water and improving visibility

May.marine_life: hammerheads schooling while silky shark aggregations grow and Galapagos sharks patrol the seamount
May.conditions: warmer with deeper thermoclines, calmer seas and clearer water

Jun.marine_life: hammerheads schooling while big silky shark schools gather and the first whale sharks converge
Jun.conditions: wet season onset with warm water, deep thermoclines and good visibility

Jul.marine_life: whale sharks arriving while hundreds of silky sharks swirl and hammerhead schools patrol
Jul.conditions: warm calm seas with occasional rain and strong visibility

Aug.marine_life: whale sharks cruising in while massive silky shark aggregations swirl and hammerheads school
Aug.conditions: warmest water with calm seas and excellent blue-water visibility

Sep.marine_life: whale sharks feeding while silky shark schools swirl and hammerheads patrol the seamount
Sep.conditions: warm with wet-season rains topside, calm seas and superb visibility

Oct.marine_life: late whale sharks lingering while silky sharks taper off and hammerheads school
Oct.conditions: warm and rainier topside with generally calm seas and good visibility

Nov.marine_life: hammerhead schools rebuilding while Galapagos and silky sharks patrol the seamount
Nov.conditions: transition to dry season with strengthening currents and still-good visibility

Dec.marine_life: largest scalloped hammerhead schools of the year swirling while Galapagos sharks patrol
Dec.conditions: dry season with strong currents returning, cooling thermoclines and dropping visibility

dive_sites.name: La Nevera (The Fridge)
dive_sites.blurb: Signature cleaning station on the northeast side where scalloped hammerheads and Galapagos sharks queue among reef fish; cold upwelling gives it its name.

dive_sites.name: El Bajo del Monstruo (Monster Seamount)
dive_sites.blurb: Offshore pinnacle famous for hundreds of hammerheads and the elusive smalltooth sand tiger that rises to ~21m in the cold season; divable only in good conditions.

dive_sites.name: La Gringa
dive_sites.blurb: Exposed monolith on the south side pierced by a swim-through cave between 24-40m; hammerheads, Galapagos and silky sharks, tuna and eagle rays in fierce current.

dive_sites.name: El Freezer
dive_sites.blurb: Volcanic wall partly clad in hard coral, named for the cold water on this flank; walls of hammerheads and passing pelagics dropping into the blue.

dive_sites.name: Los Tres Mosqueteros
dive_sites.blurb: Trio of rocks off the north coast delivering classic pinnacle diving with silky and Galapagos sharks plus schooling tuna.

dive_sites.name: Sahara
dive_sites.blurb: Southern site where silky sharks congregate in large numbers over a current-swept slope.

dive_sites.name: El Altar de la Virgen
dive_sites.blurb: Colorful hard-coral pinnacle reliable for hammerhead sightings and cruising eagle rays.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Jdmacarenoq (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: Five hundred kilometers off Colombia's Pacific coast, Malpelo is a bare volcanic rock rising from a seamount patrolled by sharks in staggering numbers. Walls of 200-plus scalloped hammerheads stack up over cleaning stations like La Nevera, silkies swarm by the hundred, and cold-season dives can turn up the rarely seen smalltooth sand tiger. Reached only by liveaboard, its surge-scoured pinnacles and powerful currents demand reef hooks and serious drift experience.
destinations.underwater: This is blue-water big-animal diving at its rawest: you back-roll into open Pacific, drop fast onto a volcanic pinnacle or wall at 15-40 m and hook in while the current pulls. Cool-season thermoclines can slam through at around 14°C, and plankton often trims visibility to 12-15 m, the same soup that feeds the shark walls. La Gringa threads a swim-through cave between 24 and 40 m, and the signature moment is looking up at hammerheads stacked overhead at La Nevera.
destinations.encounters: Sharks dominate every dive. Scalloped hammerheads school in their hundreds through the dry season, peaking around the turn of the year, when the rare smalltooth sand tiger rises to about 21 m at El Bajo del Monstruo. The warm months trade cold thermoclines for whale sharks and silky shark aggregations by the hundred, while Galapagos sharks, tuna and eagle rays patrol year-round.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: NOAA (Public domain) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Yap
destinations.current_note: short (channel currents draw mantas)
destinations.currents: Tidal currents funnel through the reef channels and switch with the tide, so most manta dives are timed as short drifts or hooked hangs at the cleaning stations.
destinations.highlights: Resident reef mantas queue at channel cleaning stations year-round, peaking in the Dec-Apr mating season, with dusk mandarinfish and shark-lined caverns on the side.

Jan.marine_life: courting trains of resident reef mantas stacking up at the Mi'l Channel cleaning stations
Jan.conditions: dry season with calm seas, minimal current and rare typhoons

Feb.marine_life: mantas mating in full swing with multi-ray gatherings across the barrier reef
Feb.conditions: dry-season peak with calm settled seas and excellent clarity

Mar.marine_life: mantas still aggregating to mate while reef sharks patrol and mandarinfish spawn reliably
Mar.conditions: late dry season with calm water and long visibility

Apr.marine_life: mantas closing out the mating stretch while the cleaning stations stay busy
Apr.conditions: dry-to-wet transition with mostly calm seas and first passing showers

May.marine_life: resident mantas cruising the cleaning stations while macro and reef life stay steady
May.conditions: transition to wet season with occasional showers and choppier surface

Jun.marine_life: mantas shifting toward the Goofnuw and Mi'l channels for summer morning cleanings
Jun.conditions: early wet season with more rain, building plankton and low typhoon risk

Jul.marine_life: mantas feeding on plankton-rich water while pelagics visit the outer walls
Jul.conditions: wet-season core with frequent rain and stronger channel currents

Aug.marine_life: mantas continuing their summer cleanings in the channels alongside sharks and eagle rays
Aug.conditions: wet season with warmest water, greener plankton-laden visibility and firm currents

Sep.marine_life: mantas still congregating in Goofnuw into early fall while macro life stays strong
Sep.conditions: late wet season, showery with moderate currents and uncommon typhoons

Oct.marine_life: resident mantas working the cleaning stations as the summer aggregations wind down
Oct.conditions: wet-to-dry transition with seas calming and visibility rebuilding

Nov.marine_life: mantas returning to the barrier-reef stations as the winter build-up starts
Nov.conditions: early dry season, drier and calmer with improving clarity

Dec.marine_life: mantas beginning to court as trains form in the Mi'l Channel
Dec.conditions: dry season, calm and clear with light currents

dive_sites.name: Mi'l Channel
dive_sites.blurb: A 9m coral ridge squeezes the tidal flow into a natural cleaning station where up to 30 resident mantas hover to be groomed by wrasse, at its busiest during the Dec-Apr mating season.

dive_sites.name: Valley of the Rays
dive_sites.blurb: Coral outcrops rising from the sandy channel floor, including the lettuce-coral 'Merry-Go-Round', act as cleaning stations where mantas queue and hang almost motionless overhead.

dive_sites.name: Goofnuw Channel
dive_sites.blurb: Summer and early-fall manta hotspot where the rays line up each morning at the cleaning stations as the tide pushes through the channel.

dive_sites.name: Yap Caverns
dive_sites.blurb: Canyons, chimneys and chasms in the coral with whitetip reef sharks resting in sandy bowls, grey reef sharks patrolling the outer mini-wall and lionfish tucked in the crevices.

dive_sites.name: Lionfish Wall
dive_sites.blurb: A crevice-riddled wall named for its resident lionfish, with mantas, eagle rays, turtles, sharks and tuna cruising the adjacent blue.

dive_sites.name: Rainbow Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Shallow coral rubble where divers gather at dusk to watch mandarinfish pair off and spiral up into the water column in their nightly mating ritual.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Original uploader was Bartek.cieslak at pl.wikipedia (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: Few places deliver mantas as reliably as Yap, a quiet Micronesian island where resident reef mantas queue at cleaning stations in the tidal channels year-round. Dives are timed to the tide as short drifts or hooked hangs at Mi'l and Goofnuw Channels, peaking during the December-to-April mating season. Between manta runs there are shark-lined caverns to thread and a dusk mandarinfish ritual on the rubble, all dived comfortably by day boat.
destinations.underwater: You settle low behind a coral head in 28-30°C water, tuck in against the tidal push, and wait. At Mi'l Channel the flow squeezes over a 9 m ridge, and a manta banks in above the cleaner wrasse, stalling close enough to fill your vision. The channels are the show, but the outer reef adds range: canyons and chimneys at Yap Caverns, whitetips resting in sand bowls, and Lionfish Wall's crevices falling to 30 m through dry-season visibility near 30 m.
destinations.encounters: Reef mantas are resident, so they show in any month, but December through April is the spectacle, when courting trains of rays stack up in Mi'l Channel; summer shifts the action to Goofnuw's morning cleanings. Grey reef and whitetip sharks patrol the outer reef, eagle rays pass the walls, and at dusk mandarinfish spiral up over Rainbow Reef's rubble to mate.

destinations.name: Cenderawasih Bay
destinations.best_months: Jun-Oct (whale sharks year-round; best liveaboard season)
destinations.current_note: calm, almost currentless
destinations.currents: Sheltered by its near-enclosed geography, the bay stays calm and virtually currentless, so the shallow whale-shark and wreck dives feel effortless.
destinations.highlights: The only place on Earth to scuba year-round with resident whale sharks feeding under fishermen's bagan platforms, plus coral-clad WWII wrecks in glassy, currentless water.

Jan.marine_life: whale sharks feeding under the Kwatisore bagans while wrecks and endemic reef fish stay active
Jan.conditions: wetter season keeps the bay calm but transits get lumpy

Feb.marine_life: resident whale sharks hovering under the bagans while hard-coral gardens and endemics cover the Shinwa Maru
Feb.conditions: wet-season swell offshore while sheltered inner-bay dives stay flat and warm

Mar.marine_life: whale sharks holding at the platforms while macro and endemic reef life dot the walls
Mar.conditions: tail of the wetter season with rougher transits limiting liveaboard scheduling

Apr.marine_life: whale sharks cruising under the bagans while wrecks and soft-coral reefs teem with fish
Apr.conditions: seas settling as the dry season opens with liveaboard conditions returning

May.marine_life: whale sharks reliable at Kwatisore while barracuda and trevally schools sweep the reefs
May.conditions: dry-season calm building with warm water and easy shallow dives

Jun.marine_life: whale-shark encounters under the bagans while wrecks and endemic reef fish thrive
Jun.conditions: liveaboard season begins with calmest seas and driest weather

Jul.marine_life: multiple whale sharks feeding under the platforms while coral-encrusted wrecks thrive with life
Jul.conditions: peak season with glassy water, minimal current and top visibility

Aug.marine_life: resident whale sharks hovering under the bagans while barrel sponges, endemics and wreck life flourish
Aug.conditions: peak liveaboard season, calm, dry and highly reliable

Sep.marine_life: whale sharks cruising the platforms while WWII wrecks and soft-coral reefs draw divers
Sep.conditions: excellent late-dry conditions with flat seas and strong visibility

Oct.marine_life: whale sharks holding year-round while healthy reefs and coral-clad wrecks flourish
Oct.conditions: end of peak season, still calm and clear before the wetter months

Nov.marine_life: whale sharks continuing under the bagans while wrecks and endemics stay active
Nov.conditions: transition into the wetter season, inner bay calm but transits lumpier

Dec.marine_life: resident whale sharks feeding under the platforms while reef and wreck life stays steady
Dec.conditions: wetter season underway with sheltered dives calm but fewer boats operating

dive_sites.name: Kwatisore Bagans
dive_sites.blurb: The signature dive: divers descend beside the anchored fishing platforms where resident whale sharks hang vertically to suck baitfish from the nets, giving unhurried, close scuba encounters in warm, currentless water.

dive_sites.name: Tanjung Mangguar
dive_sites.blurb: One of the bay's prettiest reefs, blanketed in soft coral and swarmed by barracuda, trevally, fusiliers and surgeonfish along an easy sloping wall.

dive_sites.name: Shinwa Maru
dive_sites.blurb: A 120m Japanese WWII cargo ship lying on its port side, largely intact and now a coral-encrusted reef draped in hard corals and hosting dense fish life.

dive_sites.name: Cross Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A shallow, easy Japanese wartime wreck coated in hard coral and marked by a memorial cross, ideal for relaxed exploration and macro life.

dive_sites.name: Tridacna Atoll
dive_sites.blurb: Deep drop-offs and fringing reefs carpeted in soft corals, sea whips, giant clams and massive barrel and elephant-ear sponges.

dive_sites.name: P-40 Tomahawk Plane Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A remarkably preserved WWII fighter aircraft resting upright with instrumentation and pilot's seat still in place, sitting quietly on the calm bay floor.

dive_sites.name: Purup Island Reefs
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered fringing reefs rich in West Papua's endemic reef fish, healthy hard corals and abundant macro subjects in flat, easy conditions.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Rogistira (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: Tucked into West Papua's northern coast, Cenderawasih Bay is the only place to scuba with resident whale sharks year-round, the giants hanging vertically beneath fishermen's bagan platforms to sip baitfish from the nets. The bay's near-enclosed geography keeps the water glassy and virtually currentless, so the shallow shark encounters, coral-clad WWII wrecks and endemic-rich reefs all dive effortlessly. Access is by liveaboard, and the calm conditions welcome any certification level.
destinations.underwater: The bay's near-enclosed geography kills the current, so you hover in flat, 27-30°C water while one of diving's strangest scenes plays out: whale sharks hanging vertically beneath the Kwatisore bagans at 3-12 m, sucking baitfish from the nets an arm's length away. Elsewhere the diving stays just as calm. The 120 m Shinwa Maru lies on its port side under hard coral at 16-34 m, and a P-40 fighter rests upright with its instruments still in place.
destinations.encounters: Whale sharks here are resident, not seasonal; they feed under the platforms all year, often several at once in the calm June-October window. Around them the bay layers endemic West Papuan reef fish, barracuda and trevally sweeping Tanjung Mangguar, giant clams and barrel sponges on Tridacna Atoll's drop-offs, and easy macro on the coral-coated wartime wrecks.

destinations.name: Jupiter
destinations.best_months: Jan-Mar (hammerheads, lemon sharks); Aug-Sep (goliath grouper spawn)
destinations.current_note: Gulf Stream drift dives; boats live-follow divers, hooks not used
destinations.currents: Persistent north-flowing Gulf Stream drift; often 1-2+ knots
destinations.highlights: Gulf Stream drift diving with winter great hammerheads and lemon shark aggregations, plus a late-summer goliath grouper spawn on the wrecks

Jan.marine_life: lemon shark aggregation building while great hammerheads and bull sharks patrol the drift
Jan.conditions: cooler Gulf Stream with brisk drift and visibility variable with fronts

Feb.marine_life: lemon sharks massing while great hammerheads and bull sharks work the shark dives
Feb.conditions: winter shark season with strong drift and cooler water

Mar.marine_life: great hammerheads patrolling while lemon sharks taper and bull sharks cruise the drift
Mar.conditions: tail of shark season with drift diving and improving visibility

Apr.marine_life: reef sharks and turtles cruising while rays glide and resident goliaths hold on the wrecks
Apr.conditions: warming water with steady drift and good visibility

May.marine_life: turtles and reef sharks cruising while rays, snook and jacks crowd the wrecks
May.conditions: warm with calmer seas and clear Gulf Stream drift

Jun.marine_life: turtles nesting while reef fish swarm and goliaths gather on the wrecks
Jun.conditions: warm and generally clear with occasional summer storms

Jul.marine_life: goliath groupers building on the wrecks while turtles and reef sharks cruise past
Jul.conditions: warmest and calm with good visibility and afternoon storms

Aug.marine_life: goliath grouper spawning aggregation with dozens stacked on the wrecks
Aug.conditions: warm with mild drift, clear water and deep wreck profiles

Sep.marine_life: goliath grouper spawn peaking in big schools around the new moon
Sep.conditions: warm, clear and calm with hurricane season to watch

Oct.marine_life: goliaths thinning out while reef sharks and turtles cruise big bait schools
Oct.conditions: warm with steady drift and good visibility between fronts

Nov.marine_life: reef sharks and turtles cruising while the first winter sharks arrive
Nov.conditions: cooling with more fronts, swell and variable visibility

Dec.marine_life: lemon sharks arriving while bull sharks gather and the early hammerheads move in
Dec.conditions: winter shark season begins, cooler with brisk drift

dive_sites.name: Jupiter Wreck Trek (Zion Train)
dive_sites.blurb: Drift across three wrecks - the Zion freighter, the Miss Jenny barge and the upright Esso Bonaire - a goliath grouper stronghold in the spawn

dive_sites.name: The Ledge
dive_sites.blurb: West-facing double-shelf ledge system with drop-offs 2-4.5 m high, home to reef sharks, turtles and dense fish life on the drift

dive_sites.name: Captain Mike's
dive_sites.blurb: Ledge site on the same reef line as Area 51 and Scarface, good for goliaths, sharks and passing pelagics

dive_sites.name: MG-111
dive_sites.blurb: Broken-up Mississippi River barge north of Jupiter Inlet in about 20 m, a reliable goliath grouper haven

dive_sites.name: Lemon Drop
dive_sites.blurb: Signature winter shark drift where lemon sharks aggregate in numbers, with great hammerheads and bull sharks in season

dive_sites.name: Tunnels / Loggerhead Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Ledge and swim-through reef stretch running with turtles, morays and reef fish on an easy Gulf Stream drift

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Clinton & Charles Robertson from RAF Lakenheath, UK & San Marcos, TX, USA & UK (CC BY-SA 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: On Florida's Atlantic coast, Jupiter puts the Gulf Stream to work: nearly every dive is a one-to-two-knot drift over ledges and wrecks in 15-30 m of blue water. Winter draws great hammerheads and thick lemon shark aggregations to sites like Lemon Drop; late summer packs goliath groupers onto the Wreck Trek for their spawn. Day boats run it all, and the constant current makes this advanced-leaning big-animal diving.
destinations.underwater: Every dive is a drift: you splash in, the Gulf Stream carries you north at one to two knots, and the boat follows your bubbles — no hooks, no fixed lines. Ledges with undercuts 2-4.5 m high scroll past at 15-28 m, and the Wreck Trek links three wrecks in a single 27-30 m glide. Water swings from 22°C under a 5mm in winter to 30°C in a 3mm at summer's height, with visibility usually either side of 20 m.
destinations.encounters: Winter is shark season: lemon sharks aggregate in numbers at Lemon Drop from December, with great hammerheads and bull sharks patrolling the drift into March. Late summer belongs to the goliath grouper spawn, when dozens stack on the wrecks around the new moon. Between the two, loggerhead turtles, reef sharks and rays ride the same current you do.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Bureau  of Land Management (Public domain) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Protea Banks
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm full year-round; 7mm or hooded 5mm in winter for cold thermoclines, 3mm possible on warmest summer days
destinations.current_note: Offshore fossil-dune reef 7-8 km out swept by the Agulhas Current; drift dives are the norm and current can run hard north or south, sometimes shifting mid-dive.
destinations.currents: Strong, often unpredictable drift; blue-water descents and negative entries are standard, and dives are frequently ended with a free ascent in open water.
destinations.highlights: South Africa's shark-diving capital: a deep 27-40 m reef of caves, pinnacles and gullies where Zambezi (bull) sharks patrol year-round, wintering ragged-tooth sharks pack the caves, and summer brings tiger sharks and schools of scalloped hammerheads. The sardine run passes offshore in June-July. Strictly for experienced, current-comfortable divers.

Jan.marine_life: bull and tiger sharks patrolling the Southern Pinnacles while scalloped hammerheads pass with plentiful game fish
Jan.conditions: warm summer water and strong drift with plankton sometimes clouding visibility

Feb.marine_life: bull sharks holding steady while tiger sharks, the odd hammerhead and warm-water pelagics chase game-fish shoals
Feb.conditions: high summer warmth with often strong visibility and powerful current

Mar.marine_life: bull and tiger sharks working the pinnacles while the autumn changeover mixes species on every dive
Mar.conditions: lingering warm water with more variable seas and visibility in transition

Apr.marine_life: male ragged-tooth sharks arriving at the Northern Pinnacles while bull sharks keep patrolling the deeper ledges
Apr.conditions: cooling water, often calmer seas, winter clarity starting to build

May.marine_life: ragged-tooth sharks gathering in the caves before the mating aggregation while bull sharks cruise as always
May.conditions: drier winter season improving visibility with cooler water and thermoclines appearing

Jun.marine_life: ragged-tooth sharks packing the Northern caves while bull sharks patrol and the sardine run passes offshore
Jun.conditions: clear dry winter, the year's best visibility, cold thermoclines, strong drift

Jul.marine_life: ragged-tooth mating aggregation filling the caves while bull sharks patrol and the sardine run draws predators
Jul.conditions: cold clear winter water with excellent visibility and brisk deep-reef current

Aug.marine_life: ragged-tooth sharks filling the Northern caves while bull sharks cruise and guitarfish rest on the sand
Aug.conditions: clear winter water, cold on the bottom, with strong drift dives

Sep.marine_life: late ragged-tooth sharks overlapping with the first scalloped hammerheads while bull sharks hold the reef
Sep.conditions: warming water and good clarity with current running hard either direction

Oct.marine_life: bull sharks patrolling while scalloped hammerhead schools build and tiger sharks return with the warming water
Oct.conditions: spring transition with warming water and generally good visibility

Nov.marine_life: huge schools of scalloped hammerheads sweeping past while bull and tiger sharks work the Southern Pinnacles
Nov.conditions: returning warm summer water with strong drift and usually good visibility

Dec.marine_life: scalloped hammerheads schooling while bull and tiger sharks round out the Southern Pinnacles pelagic mix
Dec.conditions: warm water, powerful drift, often excellent visibility barring plankton blooms

dive_sites.name: Northern Pinnacles
dive_sites.blurb: The colder-water end of the reef and the heart of the winter ragged-tooth season, a series of pinnacles, ledges and cave systems where 'raggies' shelter from the current in June-November.

dive_sites.name: Southern Pinnacles
dive_sites.blurb: The summer pelagic arena, dived as a drift over pinnacles and sand in search of bull and tiger sharks and schooling scalloped hammerheads.

dive_sites.name: Sharks' Gullies (Northern Caves)
dive_sites.blurb: Twin overhang and cave systems in the Northern Pinnacles where dozens of ragged-tooth sharks congregate at close quarters during the winter mating aggregation.

dive_sites.name: Southern Cave
dive_sites.blurb: The usual start of the Southern Pinnacle dive, a rock overhang packed with game fish that opens onto the drift toward the gullies and sand.

dive_sites.name: Kingfish Gully
dive_sites.blurb: An overhanging rock beyond the Southern Cave holding dense shoals of kingfish, yellowtail and potato bass, with sharks often cruising the edge.

dive_sites.name: Sand Shark Gully (The Arena)
dive_sites.blurb: A large sandy patch at the reef's deepest, roughly 40 m, known as the Arena and home to giant guitarfish; a classic spot to hang for passing pelagics.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Ossewa (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: Protea Banks starts where most reefs end: a deep offshore reef of caves, pinnacles and gullies lying 27-40 m down off South Africa's KwaZulu-Natal coast, dived as fast blue-water drifts with negative entries and open-water ascents. Zambezi (bull) sharks patrol year-round, wintering ragged-tooth sharks pack the caves, and summer adds tiger sharks and schooling hammerheads, with the sardine run streaming past in June and July. This is uncompromising, experienced-divers-only territory.
destinations.underwater: A dive here starts with a negative entry and a blue-water descent, because the reef top sits at 27 m and the Agulhas Current does not wait. You land on fossil-dune pinnacles, ledges and cave systems, drift wherever the current decides - it can reverse mid-dive - and finish with a free ascent in open water. Winter brings the clearest water, up to 25 m, with cold thermoclines around 20°C; summer runs 25-26°C with occasional plankton haze.
destinations.encounters: Bull (Zambezi) sharks patrol the pinnacles in every month of the year. Through winter and into spring, ragged-tooth sharks pack the Northern caves in a mating aggregation you can hover beside, while the sardine run streams past offshore. Summer swaps them for tiger sharks and schools of scalloped hammerheads over the Southern Pinnacles, with giant guitarfish resting on the sand of the Arena at 40 m.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo via Pexels

destinations.name: Tofo
destinations.best_months: Oct-Mar (whale sharks + mantas, warm water, 20-30m viz)
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm (add hood and vest in Jun-Aug winter)
destinations.current_note: Outer reefs (Manta Reef, Giants Castle, The Office) are drift dives with moderate-to-strong current at 20-26m; inshore sites are milder
destinations.currents: Mild inshore; moderate to strong drift on the deeper offshore cleaning-station reefs
destinations.highlights: Year-round reef manta cleaning stations at Manta Reef and Giants Castle, whale sharks off the beach Oct-Mar, and migrating humpbacks Jun-Oct, all reached by RIB launches through the surf

Jan.marine_life: whale sharks cruising the bay while resident mantas and reef sharks work the deeper reefs
Jan.conditions: wet-season warmth with twenty to thirty metres of visibility

Feb.marine_life: whale sharks feeding along the coast while mantas and leopard sharks work the reefs
Feb.conditions: warm wet season with occasional rain and strong visibility

Mar.marine_life: whale sharks thinning out while mantas hold the cleaning stations and reef sharks patrol
Mar.conditions: wet season ending with warm water and good visibility

Apr.marine_life: mantas gliding over the cleaning stations while reef sharks and leopard sharks hold the ledges
Apr.conditions: drying weather with calm seas and clear water

May.marine_life: mantas queueing at the cleaning stations while reef sharks cruise the walls below
May.conditions: dry and pleasant with visibility beyond twenty metres

Jun.marine_life: mantas working the stations while the first humpback whales arrive along the coast
Jun.conditions: early dry winter turning windier with cooler water

Jul.marine_life: mantas holding station while humpback whales pass offshore and reef sharks patrol the reef
Jul.conditions: dry windy weather, cooler water, visibility down to five to fifteen metres

Aug.marine_life: humpback whales streaming past in numbers while mantas and reef sharks hold the reef
Aug.conditions: coolest water of the year with windy dry-season days and variable visibility

Sep.marine_life: humpback whales breaching offshore while mantas and reef sharks keep working the reef
Sep.conditions: winds easing with warming water and improving visibility

Oct.marine_life: whale sharks returning to the bay while late humpbacks pass and mantas hold the stations
Oct.conditions: calm seas with warming water and clearing visibility

Nov.marine_life: whale sharks cruising the plankton lines while mantas and reef sharks fill the deeper sites
Nov.conditions: warm early wet season with strong visibility

Dec.marine_life: whale sharks feeding inshore while mantas circle the stations and leopard sharks rest on the sand
Dec.conditions: warm calm wet season with twenty to thirty metre visibility

dive_sites.name: Manta Reef
dive_sites.blurb: World-famous amphitheatre reef with two large manta cleaning stations; a north-to-south drift best suited to divers with current experience

dive_sites.name: Giants Castle
dive_sites.blurb: Deep drift over walls, ridges and drop-offs with a second reef manta cleaning station; for advanced divers with deep-drift experience

dive_sites.name: The Office
dive_sites.blurb: Offshore reef about 10km out with clearer water; sandy bowl and ridge patrolled by whitetip, grey reef and leopard sharks plus honeycomb and marble rays

dive_sites.name: Salon
dive_sites.blurb: Relaxed shallow reef of stone boulders and sand famed for its cleaner-shrimp stations and easy conditions

dive_sites.name: Amazon
dive_sites.blurb: Coral-covered inshore reef with dense fish life, moray eels and frequent turtle and ray sightings

dive_sites.name: Sherwood Forest
dive_sites.blurb: Reef named for its forest of soft corals and sea fans, a reliable spot for reef sharks and passing mantas

destinations.image_credit: Photo: jon hanson (CC BY-SA 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: A laid-back beach town on Mozambique's Inhambane coast, Tofo built its name on megafauna. Reef mantas hold court year-round at deep cleaning stations like Manta Reef and Giants Castle, whale sharks cruise offshore from October to March, and humpbacks migrate past between June and October. Dives launch by RIB through the surf; shallow inshore reefs suit newer divers, but the signature manta sites are deep drifts for experienced hands.
destinations.underwater: You start each dive soaked, the RIB punching through the shore break, and the signature reefs reward it: Manta Reef is a natural amphitheatre at 22-26 m dived as a north-to-south drift, Giants Castle runs walls and ridges to 30 m in moderate-to-strong current, and The Office, 10 km out, adds a clearer-water sandy bowl. Inshore it eases off - Salon's boulders sit at 10-14 m with cleaner-shrimp stations. Water spans 22°C in the windy winter to 28°C in summer, when visibility runs 20-30 m.
destinations.encounters: Reef mantas are the residents, queuing at the cleaning stations in every month of the year. Whale sharks cruise the plankton lines off the beach through the warm season, and from June to October humpback whales stream past on migration. Below the headliners, leopard sharks rest on the sand, whitetip and grey reef sharks patrol The Office alongside honeycomb and marble rays, and bottlenose dolphins make regular appearances.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Ana Raquel S. Hernandes from Sao Paulo, Brazil (CC BY-SA 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Larnaca
destinations.current_note: Sheltered Larnaca Bay is usually calm; mild drift and surge can build on exposed reefs and inside the Zenobia when wind picks up, and deeper penetration carries silt-out and depth hazards.
destinations.currents: mild
destinations.highlights: The Zenobia, a 172m roll-on/roll-off ferry that sank in 1980 and now lies on her port side from 16m to a 42m seabed, is the centrepiece and ranks among the world's great wreck dives, its cargo of articulated lorries still chained to the deck. Beyond it Larnaca Bay strings together more accessible wrecks and reefs, while day trips reach the caves, tunnels and arches of Cape Greco. Warm, clear summer water and an easy shore-and-boat mix make it a year-round Mediterranean base that peaks from June to October.

Jan.marine_life: grouper, moray and bream holding on the wrecks while pelagic traffic thins to almost nothing
Jan.conditions: sixteen degree water and winter fronts, though calm windows still dive well

Feb.marine_life: resident wreck fish staying active while moray eels and octopus hunt and pelagics stay scarce
Feb.conditions: coldest water of the year with short weather windows between fronts

Mar.marine_life: nudibranchs carpeting the reefs while grouper and bream show themselves more as life stirs
Mar.conditions: settling early-spring weather with still-cool water around seventeen degrees

Apr.marine_life: barracuda and amberjack appearing over the Alexandria while more fish settle onto the wrecks
Apr.conditions: milder settled weather and generally clear water

May.marine_life: barracuda schooling with amberjack overhead while grouper and turtles become common sightings
May.conditions: calm seas and pleasant water with reliable Zenobia boat access

Jun.marine_life: barracuda and amberjack schooling over the wrecks while grouper hold structure and turtles pass through
Jun.conditions: warm settled summer with excellent visibility and easy access everywhere

Jul.marine_life: barracuda and amberjack swarming the Zenobia while grouper, moray and turtles fill out the reefs
Jul.conditions: warmest calmest stretch of the year with long clear days

Aug.marine_life: pelagics sweeping the wrecks and reefs while grouper, morays and turtles crowd every dive
Aug.conditions: glassy seas and top visibility with busy traffic on the Zenobia

Sep.marine_life: large barracuda and amberjack schools circling while grouper hold the wrecks and turtles cruise past
Sep.conditions: warm stable water, clear and less crowded than midsummer

Oct.marine_life: fish still teeming over the wrecks while lingering pelagics work the slowly cooling water
Oct.conditions: warm autumn water around twenty-four degrees with generally settled seas

Nov.marine_life: resident grouper, moray and bream holding reliably on the wrecks as the reefs quieten
Nov.conditions: first autumn fronts arriving with boat trips needing weather watching

Dec.marine_life: resident wreck fish settling in while moray eels and octopus hunt and pelagic movement fades
Dec.conditions: cooling unsettled water with calm-window planning for the Zenobia

dive_sites.name: The Zenobia
dive_sites.blurb: A 172m roll-on/roll-off ferry that sank in 1980 and now lies on her port side, ranked among the world's finest wreck dives. Recreational routes explore the upper hull and twin propellers while deeper penetration into the car decks and cargo of chained lorries is a technical, advanced-only undertaking.

dive_sites.name: The Alexandria
dive_sites.blurb: A fishing trawler that went down in a 2006 storm while under tow, now sitting upright and richly encrusted. Schools of barracuda and amberjack swirl overhead while grouper, triggerfish and nudibranchs work the hull.

dive_sites.name: HMS Cricket
dive_sites.blurb: A WWI-era British river gunboat used as an RAF target and sunk in 1947, resting upside down with a sandy swim-through beneath the hull. Grouper, moray eels, bream and lionfish shelter around the wreckage.

dive_sites.name: Green Bay
dive_sites.blurb: A shallow, sheltered bay with seagrass and submerged statues that is ideal for shore entries, training and relaxed second dives. Cuttlefish, wrasse, bream and the occasional turtle keep it lively.

dive_sites.name: Cape Greco Caves
dive_sites.blurb: A day-trip playground of caves, tunnels, arches and rocky reef along a dramatic headland. Sunlit swim-throughs shelter octopus, moray eels, grouper and passing barracuda.

dive_sites.name: Cape Greco - The Caverns
dive_sites.blurb: Shallower cavern and gully diving on the Cape Greco coast with easy topography and good light. Bream, wrasse, octopus and nudibranchs populate the rock, making it a strong all-round reef dive.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Jetlife2 (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: One wreck made Larnaca a pilgrimage: the Zenobia, a 172 m ferry that rolled onto her port side in 1980 and settled between 16 and 42 m, her cargo of articulated lorries still chained to the decks. Recreational divers tour the hull and twin propellers while technical teams thread the car decks, and the surrounding bay adds easier wrecks, shore entries and the sunlit caves of Cape Greco. Mild currents and warm, clear summers keep this Cyprus base busy from June through October.
destinations.underwater: The Zenobia rearranges your sense of up: you descend onto a hull lying on its side at 16 m, follow railings that run vertically, and pass lorries hanging in the gloom on the way to the twin propellers near 42 m. Around the bay, HMS Cricket rests upside down with a sandy swim-through beneath the hull, and Cape Greco's tunnels and arches fill with Mediterranean light. Summer water reaches 27°C with visibility to 30 m; winter drops to 16°C but stays clear.
destinations.encounters: Dusky grouper hold the wrecks year-round, and moray eels and octopus keep hunting the structure even through winter. Summer brings the pelagics, with barracuda and amberjack schooling over the Zenobia and the Alexandria from late spring into September. Nudibranchs carpet the reefs as spring stirs, sea turtles turn up in Green Bay's seagrass, and lionfish shelter around the Cricket.

destinations.name: Kaş
destinations.current_note: Most sheltered reefs are calm, but exposed sites like the Canyon can run a brisk southerly current across the shallow gorge before the wall.
destinations.currents: Generally light around the harbour reefs; moderate at exposed headlands and the Canyon, where a southerly flow may need to be crossed to reach the walls.
destinations.highlights: The premier dive base on Turkey's Turquoise Coast, with 30-plus sites minutes from the harbour: dramatic walls and the Canyon, the intact sunken DC-3 Dakota, a full-size replica of the Bronze Age Uluburun wreck, amphora fields and swim-through caverns, all in famously clear warm-summer water.

Jan.marine_life: reef fish and grouper holding quietly on the walls while pelagics stay scarce
Jan.conditions: cool winter sea with thick suits and reduced operator schedules

Feb.marine_life: resident morays and octopus tucked into crevices while grouper sit deep and little else moves
Feb.conditions: coldest water of the year with winds often cancelling boats

Mar.marine_life: grouper and wrasse stirring on the reefs as life slowly returns with the warming water
Mar.conditions: still-cold water, unsettled early-spring weather and a quiet town

Apr.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus getting active across the reefs while the occasional amberjack passes through
Apr.conditions: season opening with cool water, thick suits and improving weather

May.marine_life: amberjack and barracuda gathering around the walls while grouper station themselves on the reef
May.conditions: comfortable water, generally settled seas and full operator schedules

Jun.marine_life: grouper and amberjack thickening on the reefs while barracuda school and the first turtles arrive
Jun.conditions: warm water with calm sunny days and excellent visibility

Jul.marine_life: loggerhead turtles cruising the reefs while amberjack schools pass and grouper and octopus show everywhere
Jul.conditions: warm calm sea with superb clarity in the busiest season

Aug.marine_life: turtles gliding over the reefs while pelagics sweep through on nearly every dive
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year with flat calm days and long visibility

Sep.marine_life: turtles feeding in the shallows while amberjack and barracuda hunt over abundant reef fish
Sep.conditions: still-warm sea with settled weather and outstanding visibility

Oct.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus staying busy while passing pelagics linger into the autumn
Oct.conditions: pleasant water, mostly calm, with the season winding down late

Nov.marine_life: resident grouper, moray and octopus holding on while the visiting fish thin out
Nov.conditions: cooling sea with more unsettled weather and shorter operator schedules

Dec.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus tucked deep into the structure while the reefs sit quiet
Dec.conditions: cold early-winter sea with thick suits and limited boat availability

dive_sites.name: The Canyon
dive_sites.blurb: Kaş signature dive: cross a shallow gorge, often against a southerly current, into a rift between two steep walls dropping past 20m, cloaked in yellow cluster anemones and sponges with a small cave and red shrimp.

dive_sites.name: Big Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A classic sloping reef and wall system with terraces, cracks and overhangs; grouper, moray, octopus and passing amberjack against superb Turquoise-Coast visibility.

dive_sites.name: DC-3 Dakota Plane Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A WWII-era Dakota transport plane deliberately sunk as an artificial reef; the intact fuselage sits on sand at around 25m, now colonised by fish, morays and encrusting growth, with a swim-through interior for trained wreck divers.

dive_sites.name: Uluburun Replica (Kaş Archaeopark)
dive_sites.blurb: A full-size replica of the Late Bronze Age Uluburun ship laid out in Hidayet Bay with copper ingots and amphorae, an accessible museum-style dive celebrating the region's underwater archaeology.

dive_sites.name: Flying Fish (Dimitri) Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A small coastal wreck on a rocky slope that has become a fish magnet, with amberjack, barracuda and grouper patrolling the structure and morays in the recesses.

dive_sites.name: Amphora Field
dive_sites.blurb: A scatter of ancient clay amphorae across a gentle reef, evidence of the coast's trading past, with octopus and moray sheltering inside the vessels.

dive_sites.name: Fener (Lighthouse) Cave
dive_sites.blurb: A dramatic wall pierced by caverns and swim-throughs near the lighthouse point, with shafts of light, lobster and shrimp in the dark recesses and grouper along the drop-off.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: HALUK COMERTEL (CC BY 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: Set on Turkey's Lycian coast, Kaş packs 30-plus dive sites within minutes of its harbor, all in the famously clear water of the Turquoise Coast. History does the heavy lifting: an intact DC-3 Dakota rests on sand at around 25 m, a full-size Bronze Age Uluburun replica sits in a shallow bay, and ancient amphorae litter the reefs. Walls, caverns and the current-brushed Canyon add drama, with day boats serving everyone from new divers to seasoned wall hunters.
destinations.underwater: Clarity is the constant — often 30 m or more, so the Canyon reads like a corridor as you cross its shallow gorge, sometimes against a southerly push, and drop into the rift between anemone-cloaked walls. History fills the rest: the DC-3's intact fuselage on sand near 25 m, the Bronze Age Uluburun replica laid out with copper ingots at easy depth, amphorae scattered across the reefs. Summer water reaches 28°C; winter falls to 16°C and thick suits.
destinations.encounters: Dusky grouper hold territory on the walls year-round, joined by morays and octopus that shelter in the crevices — and inside the ancient amphorae themselves. Summer brings the movement: greater amberjack sweep the reefs, barracuda school over the drop-offs, and loggerhead turtles cruise the shallows from early summer into September. In winter the residents tuck deep into the rock and the visitors thin out.

destinations.name: Ustica
destinations.current_note: Sheltered bays and pinnacles are usually calm, but exposed offshore seamounts like Secca della Colombara and the pinnacle at Scoglio del Medico can pick up moderate current when wind and swell build.
destinations.currents: variable
destinations.highlights: Italy's first Marine Protected Area (established 1986), where decades of protection have produced crystal water, walls draped in gorgonians and sponges, and unusually bold dusky groupers. Volcanic topography carves caves, arches and tunnels, headlined by the offshore basalt pinnacle Scoglio del Medico. Two underwater archaeological trails let divers hover over ancient anchors and amphorae in situ.

Jan.marine_life: groupers and resident reef fish holding on while the winter reefs go quiet
Jan.conditions: cool quiet winter water with occasional storms and few dives running

Feb.marine_life: resident groupers and morays holding in the caves while little else moves in the cold
Feb.conditions: coldest sea of the year with winter swells and centres closed

Mar.marine_life: marine life slowly stirring across the reefs as the water bottoms out and begins to turn
Mar.conditions: still cool and weather-dependent with limited pre-season diving

Apr.marine_life: groupers working the walls while nudibranchs crowd the ledges in plankton-rich water
Apr.conditions: warming spring water with settled spells as dive centres reopen

May.marine_life: groupers, barracuda and amberjack turning reliable while nudibranchs keep up a strong showing
May.conditions: comfortable early-season water with climbing visibility and common calm days

Jun.marine_life: bold dusky groupers greeting divers while barracuda schools, amberjack and octopus spread across the sites
Jun.conditions: warm calm water with excellent visibility and full operator schedules

Jul.marine_life: unafraid groupers approaching close while barracuda, amberjack, morays and octopus fill the sites
Jul.conditions: warmest clearest conditions with long settled spells in the busiest season

Aug.marine_life: big groupers holding court while pelagic amberjack and barracuda hunt at their most active
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year with superb visibility and busy boats

Sep.marine_life: groupers, barracuda and amberjack still swarming the walls as the action carries on
Sep.conditions: still warm and clear with thinning crowds

Oct.marine_life: groupers and morays holding reliable while pelagic activity gradually eases off the walls
Oct.conditions: warm water around twenty-two degrees with strong visibility on calm days

Nov.marine_life: resident groupers persisting while the surrounding reef life slows in the cooling water
Nov.conditions: end-of-season diving, increasingly weather-dependent as most operators close

Dec.marine_life: groupers and morays holding in the caves while the winter reef sits quiet
Dec.conditions: cool stormy winter water with centres largely closed

dive_sites.name: Scoglio del Medico
dive_sites.blurb: Signature offshore basalt outcrop off the west coast, riddled with grottoes, gorges, tunnels and canyons. Enclosed passages around 15-18m lead into a cave through the rock and the large cavity known as La Balena, with careful penetration to about 26m.

dive_sites.name: Grotta dei Gamberi
dive_sites.blurb: Cave near Punta Gavazzi at the southern tip whose walls glow orange with countless narval shrimp. An advanced dive averaging 20-25m, with its main entrance deeper at around 40m.

dive_sites.name: Secca della Colombara
dive_sites.blurb: The finest dive on the north coast, west of Grotta dell'Oro, famed for vast gorgonian fans and Ustica's most beautiful sponges on an exposed offshore seamount that can catch current.

dive_sites.name: Punta Galera
dive_sites.blurb: One of the most popular and versatile sites, an easy sheltered bay with a green cave, turquoise water and frequent sightings of barracuda, amberjack and grouper.

dive_sites.name: Punta Cavazzi Archaeological Trail
dive_sites.blurb: The first underwater archaeological itinerary set in the Mediterranean, where ancient anchors of various eras rest roughly where they were found, offering an easy plunge back in time.

dive_sites.name: Punta Falconiera Archaeological Trail
dive_sites.blurb: The deeper of Ustica's two heritage itineraries, descending toward 30m to hover over ancient anchors and amphorae in situ within the protected zone.

dive_sites.name: Grotta dell'Oro
dive_sites.blurb: A colourful volcanic cave-and-wall dive on the north coast, its rock walls carpeted in sponges and encrusting life typical of the well-protected reserve.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Lauraguarnieri (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: A volcanic speck in the Tyrrhenian Sea north of Sicily, Ustica became Italy's first marine protected area in 1986, and four decades of protection show underwater: crystal water, walls thick with gorgonians and sponges, and dusky groupers bold enough to follow you around. Lava rock carves the caves, arches and tunnels of the Scoglio del Medico pinnacle, while two archaeological trails let you hover over ancient anchors and amphorae. Shore and day-boat diving runs from sheltered beginner bays to current-brushed offshore seamounts.
destinations.underwater: Grotta dei Gamberi is the standout - a cave near the southern tip whose walls glow orange with countless narval shrimp, its main entrance dropping toward 40 m. On the exposed north coast, Secca della Colombara flies vast gorgonian fans and Ustica's finest sponges on a seamount that catches current when swell builds. Water climbs from a cool 14°C in winter to 26°C by August, and viz on these offshore sites can open to 40 m.
destinations.encounters: Decades of protection show in the fish: dusky groupers here are bold enough to follow you around the walls, unbothered by divers. Through the warm months barracuda school and amberjack hunt the pinnacles at their most active, while morays and octopus work the ledges year-round. In the plankton-rich spring water, nudibranchs crowd the rock in numbers before the summer pelagics take over.

destinations.name: Vis Island
destinations.current_note: Sheltered reefs and inshore wrecks are usually slack; deeper offshore wrecks and exposed points can run when wind and tide align, so plan drops around slack water.
destinations.currents: Generally light with mostly negligible flow inshore, picking up to moderate on exposed sites and in deeper open water.
destinations.highlights: The B-17 Flying Fortress, one of the best-preserved WWII aircraft wrecks in the world, resting intact on sand at 65-72m for technical divers. Historic steamer wrecks including the shallow Teti and the ~105m Greek cargo ship Vassilios T., still laden with coal. The Blue Cave of Biševo, where midday sun floods the chamber with electric blue light. Reefs, drop-off walls and swim-through caves rich in Adriatic life around Vis and its outer islets.

Jan.marine_life: grouper and conger holding deep in cover while the cold reefs stay slow and quiet
Jan.conditions: cold winter sea near thirteen degrees with storms and few operators

Feb.marine_life: fish activity dropping to its minimum while the reefs wait out the coldest water
Feb.conditions: coldest sea of the year with unsettled winter weather

Mar.marine_life: scattered grouper, scorpionfish and octopus hunkering in the shallows while the water stays cold
Mar.conditions: cool sea with variable spring weather and limited boat cover

Apr.marine_life: octopus and moray moving about more as the reefs wake up with the warming water
Apr.conditions: cool water with calmer spells as operators reopen

May.marine_life: grouper, lobster and conger coming out across the reefs while spring plankton can bloom
May.conditions: warming sea and settling weather with the season underway

Jun.marine_life: grouper and lobster showing on every reef and wreck while the full Adriatic cast assembles
Jun.conditions: warm sea with calm settled conditions and clear water

Jul.marine_life: conger and grouper holding reliably on the wrecks while octopus watch from the cracks
Jul.conditions: warm calm summer weather with excellent clarity

Aug.marine_life: fish thronging the wrecks and walls while every site delivers its fullest abundance
Aug.conditions: warmest sea of the year, flat calm and at its clearest

Sep.marine_life: grouper, lobster and octopus staying plentiful while the warm water keeps the reefs active
Sep.conditions: still-warm sea with calm settled weather and superb visibility

Oct.marine_life: grouper and conger staying active while the reefs hold their liveliness before the cool-down
Oct.conditions: mild sea with occasional autumn fronts and still very clear water

Nov.marine_life: fish retreating into cover as the cooling reefs wind down for the winter
Nov.conditions: cooler sea with more unsettled weather and fewer boats running

Dec.marine_life: reef activity turning sparse while the cold quiet season settles over the island
Dec.conditions: cold sea and winter storms with minimal operations

dive_sites.name: B-17 Flying Fortress
dive_sites.blurb: A near-intact WWII American bomber that ditched off Vis in 1944, sitting upright on sand as one of the best-preserved aircraft wrecks anywhere; a deep technical dive only.

dive_sites.name: Vassilios T.
dive_sites.blurb: A ~105m Greek cargo steamer resting on its port side, still holding its cargo of coal; among the largest wrecks in the Adriatic and needing several dives to see.

dive_sites.name: Teti
dive_sites.blurb: A broken-up steamship on a slope where conger eels lurk in the plating by day; the shallower sections make an accessible, life-rich wreck dive.

dive_sites.name: Blue Cave (Bisevo)
dive_sites.blurb: A sea cave off nearby Bisevo island where midday sunlight refracts through an underwater opening to bathe the chamber in glowing blue light.

dive_sites.name: Stoncica Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A sloping reef and drop-off near the Stoncica lighthouse with grouper, scorpionfish and octopus among the rocks and gorgonians.

dive_sites.name: B-24 Liberator
dive_sites.blurb: The scattered remains of an American four-engine WWII bomber on sand, a deep recreational wreck for experienced divers exploring the debris field.

dive_sites.name: Ravnik / Green Cave
dive_sites.blurb: A through-cave on the islet of Ravnik lit green from a ceiling opening, with an easy swim-through and reef life around the entrance.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: Borut Furlan (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: Croatia's farthest-flung inhabited island spent decades as a closed military zone, and Vis now rewards divers with one of the Adriatic's densest wreck collections: the B-17 Flying Fortress, near-intact on sand at 65-72m for technical divers, the 105m coal steamer Vassilios T., and the shallower, life-rich Teti. Between wrecks, day boats run to grouper-patrolled reefs and sun-shot sea caves like Bisevo's Blue Cave. Recreational divers eat well here; tec divers eat better.
destinations.underwater: Wreck depth sets the day's ambition: the Teti's broken plating starts at 10 m with congers coiled inside, the coal-laden Vassilios T. takes several dives to cover between 20 and 55 m, and the B-17 waits at 65-72 m for technical teams. Between wreck days, Stoncica's gorgonian-dressed drop-off and the sun-shafted chambers of the Blue and Green caves give lighter dives. Summer water reaches 25°C with 30 m visibility and mostly slack conditions inshore; spring and autumn call for a 5mm suit.
destinations.encounters: This is Mediterranean life rather than tropical spectacle: dusky grouper holding territory on the reefs and wrecks, conger and moray eels in the shadows, European lobster in the cracks, and scorpionfish and octopus posted among the rocks. Activity peaks in the warm months from June to September, when every site shows its fullest cast; by winter the cold reefs turn slow and quiet.

destinations.name: Chania (Crete)
destinations.current_note: Generally sheltered water on the north coast; mild currents possible around exposed capes, islets and the cave entrances, stronger with meltemi wind.
destinations.currents: mild, occasionally moderate at exposed sites
destinations.highlights: Western Crete's main dive base, best known for the submerged Elephant Cave (Elefas) at Cape Drepano with its fossil stalactite-and-stalagmite wall and Ice Age elephant-bone finds. Clear blue Aegean/Mediterranean water, dramatic caverns, walls and reefs, plus a few wreck dives, wrapped around a long April-to-November summer season and quiet, cool winters.

Jan.marine_life: resident grouper, moray and octopus still pottering about while the winter reefs stay quiet
Jan.conditions: coolest water of the year with winter swell and few boats

Feb.marine_life: winter life staying sparse on the walls and caverns while little moves in the cold
Feb.conditions: cold sea and variable wind with mostly shore diving

Mar.marine_life: reef life slowly returning as the water begins to warm again
Mar.conditions: cool water with changeable early-spring conditions

Apr.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus moving about more as the reefs wake for spring
Apr.conditions: season opening with cool clearing water and calmer spells

May.marine_life: barracuda schools forming off the walls while the reefs turn busy and active
May.conditions: warming settled water with strong visibility

Jun.marine_life: abundant Mediterranean life spilling across the caverns and reefs on every dive
Jun.conditions: warm calm early-summer diving with excellent clarity

Jul.marine_life: grouper and barracuda showing on every dive while moray and octopus fill the cracks
Jul.conditions: warm clear water with occasional afternoon meltemi wind

Aug.marine_life: life thronging the reefs, walls and caverns at its most prolific
Aug.conditions: warmest sea of the year with superb visibility though meltemi can build

Sep.marine_life: big-fish activity hitting its best of the year while the reefs stay rich
Sep.conditions: warm settled water and outstanding clarity

Oct.marine_life: reef life staying lively while the sea slowly cools into autumn
Oct.conditions: mild water with calm autumn spells and great visibility

Nov.marine_life: resident species holding on while the visiting life thins out for winter
Nov.conditions: cooling water with more wind and swell as the season winds down

Dec.marine_life: reef life sitting quiet through the cold season with only residents about
Dec.conditions: cold water and winter weather with limited boat diving

dive_sites.name: Elephant Cave (Elefas)
dive_sites.blurb: Chania's signature dive at Cape Drepano off Souda Bay. The entrance sits around 10 m and opens into a large chamber with a spectacular fused stalactite-and-stalagmite wall; fossilised bones of an extinct dwarf elephant were found here. Broad and shallow with air pockets, but still an overhead environment for trained divers.

dive_sites.name: Menies (Diktynna)
dive_sites.blurb: Reef and boulder dive off the remote Menies coast on the Rodopou peninsula, near the ancient Diktynna sanctuary. Clear water, sloping rock terraces and good numbers of grouper, moray and octopus.

dive_sites.name: Islet of Marathi
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered, scenic reef dive off the Akrotiri peninsula with easy access and crystal-clear water, ideal for training and relaxed exploration among rocky reef and seagrass.

dive_sites.name: Ammoudi
dive_sites.blurb: Easy shore and shallow reef dive in some of the clearest turquoise water in the area; gentle rocky reef with damselfish, octopus and nudibranchs, popular for check-out and refresher dives.

dive_sites.name: Lighthouse Wall
dive_sites.blurb: A dramatic drop-off wall dive, one of the finer walls in the Chania area, with cracks and overhangs sheltering grouper and moray and passing barracuda in the blue.

dive_sites.name: Seal Cave
dive_sites.blurb: A rocky cavern dive named for the monk seals occasionally seen along this coast; swim-throughs and shafts of light with cavern-dwelling life on the walls.

dive_sites.name: Dia Island
dive_sites.blurb: Boat dive around the uninhabited, Natura 2000-protected islet off Crete's north coast, with exceptionally clear water, healthy reef and wall terrain and depths to suit both newer and experienced divers.

destinations.image_credit: Photo: dronepicr (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.description: Western Crete anchors its diving in Chania, where day boats and easy shore entries work a coastline of clear Aegean water, caverns and drop-offs. The headline act is the Elephant Cave at Cape Drepano, a chamber walled with fused stalactites and stalagmites where Ice Age dwarf-elephant bones were found. Around it sit sheltered training reefs, the Lighthouse Wall and grouper-thick boulder slopes off Rodopou, a mix that suits new and experienced divers alike across a long June-to-October prime season.
destinations.underwater: You drop to a 10 m cave mouth and fin into a broad chamber where torchlight picks out the fossil stalactite wall — an overhead environment with air pockets, shallow but for trained divers only. Outside, the Aegean stays remarkably clear, 25-30 m visibility through the season, warm enough for a 3mm in the 26°C of August but a 5mm most other months. Lighthouse Wall's cracked overhangs drop to 35 m; Menies offers sloping rock terraces in open blue water.
destinations.encounters: Dusky groupers anchor the cast, holding cracks in the walls alongside morays, with octopus on every reef and nudibranchs for the patient. Barracuda schools form off the drop-offs from late spring and big-fish activity peaks in the warm September water; through winter only the residents remain, pottering around quiet reefs. Monk seals are an occasional, lucky sighting along the cavern coast.

destinations.name: Bat Islands
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm full (thermoclines can plunge below 21°C even when the surface is warm)
destinations.current_note: Exposed offshore pinnacles with strong, variable currents, surge and downcurrents around the rock; the ~45km open-water crossing from Playas del Coco can be rough. Advanced certification and solid buoyancy are expected.
destinations.highlights: Costa Rica's premier bull shark dive: at the Big Scare (El Gran Susto) a dozen or more 2.5-3.5m bull sharks cruise volcanic pinnacles at 20-30m, joined by giant Pacific mantas, squadrons of eagle and cow-nose rays and dense schools of jacks and snapper. A committing offshore day-boat trip from the Guanacaste coast, best in the warm green season.

Jan.marine_life: rays and turtles cruising through big schools of fish while bull sharks make only occasional appearances
Jan.conditions: windy Papagayo season with cooler water, surface chop and better visibility

Feb.marine_life: eagle and cow-nose rays schooling with jacks and turtles while bull shark sightings stay sparse
Feb.conditions: strong offshore winds can cancel the crossing amid the coldest thermoclines

Mar.marine_life: rays and turtles working the pinnacles with reef fish while bull sharks stay largely absent
Mar.conditions: windy and cool with variable currents and weather-dependent trips

Apr.marine_life: mantas and rays gliding through as the water warms while early bull shark encounters become possible
Apr.conditions: winds easing and water warming with generally good visibility

May.marine_life: bull sharks returning to the Big Scare while mantas, eagle rays and heavy schooling fish gather
May.conditions: warm water and calmer surface though rain can cut visibility

Jun.marine_life: bull shark aggregations forming while giant Pacific mantas, rays and baitballs of jacks and snapper swirl
Jun.conditions: warm green-season water with strong pinnacle currents and swinging visibility

Jul.marine_life: a dozen or more bull sharks circling the Big Scare while mantas and rays pass through
Jul.conditions: warmest water of the year with brief downpours and variable visibility

Aug.marine_life: bull sharks massing in their thickest numbers while mantas, cow-nose ray squadrons and turtles stream past
Aug.conditions: warm green-season diving with runoff sometimes dropping the visibility

Sep.marine_life: bull sharks staying abundant while big-animal encounters hold up right through the wet months
Sep.conditions: wettest stretch with occasional rough crossings and reduced visibility

Oct.marine_life: bull sharks tapering off gradually while mantas and rays still turn up on most dives
Oct.conditions: green season winding down with warm water and variable visibility

Nov.marine_life: bull shark odds easing while rays, turtles and schooling fish keep the pinnacles busy
Nov.conditions: drier weather arriving with warm water and improving visibility

Dec.marine_life: rays and turtles cruising with big schools in the clearing water while bull sharks turn scarce
Dec.conditions: returning Papagayo winds with cooler thermoclines, choppier crossings and sharper visibility

dive_sites.name: Big Scare (El Gran Susto)
dive_sites.blurb: The signature site: exposed rocks breaking the surface where six to a dozen large bull sharks patrol the volcanic pinnacles, joined by giant mantas, jacks, snapper and the occasional sailfish or marlin.

dive_sites.name: Black Rock (Bajo Negro)
dive_sites.blurb: A steep pinnacle rising from around 30m toward the surface off Santa Rosa, drawing squadrons of eagle rays, dense schooling fish, occasional bull sharks and cruising manta rays.

dive_sites.name: Punta Gorda
dive_sites.blurb: A rocky point known for gliding groups of common and spotted eagle rays over the boulders, with turtles and reef fish in shallower water.

dive_sites.name: The Point
dive_sites.blurb: Current-swept ridges and boulder gardens holding schooling grunts, snapper and pompano, with rays and turtles working the edges.

dive_sites.name: Coral Gardens
dive_sites.blurb: A slightly more sheltered rocky-reef area used when conditions are rougher, with morays, invertebrates and reef fish and lighter current than the sharky pinnacles.

dive_sites.name: Bat Island Wall
