dive_sites.blurb: Lava flows and black volcanic boulders around the caldera's active young islet, with a WWII boat and a deeper tug lying nearby.

dive_sites.name: Adiavati Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A reef inside the caldera rim — locally tied to the Atlantis legend — crawling with crustaceans, invertebrates, groupers and barracuda.

dive_sites.name: Aspronisi (White Island) Walls
dive_sites.blurb: Sponge-encrusted lava walls on the caldera's western edge that start at snorkel depth and plunge past 40m into deep blue.

dive_sites.name: The Caves
dive_sites.blurb: Shallow volcanic caverns, canyons and swim-throughs on the south coast, lit by shafts of sun and packed with glassfish and octopus.

dive_sites.name: Perissa Rock
dive_sites.blurb: Maze-like volcanic rock formations off the black-sand beach, an easy classroom reef full of wrasse, morays and crustaceans.

destinations.description: Santorini is diving inside a drowned volcano: sheer lava walls plunge from the caldera rim into deep cobalt water, and the shallow Santa Maria wreck rests off the still-steaming islet of Nea Kameni. Life is classic Mediterranean — groupers, morays, octopus, passing barracuda — sparse but sharply lit by 30m visibility and near-zero current. Best for divers who value geology, wrecks and effortless conditions over fish density, from easy shore reefs to deep caldera walls.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Andreas Pantziarides (CC BY 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.underwater: The stillness registers first — there is barely any current inside the caldera, so you hang motionless against black lava faces that fall from a few metres to beyond 40 m at Aspronisi. Everything is volcanic: boulder fields, canyons, sun-shafted caverns along the south coast. The Santa Maria rests at 16-19 m off Nea Kameni, an easy wreck in water clear to 30 m. Summer peaks at 24-25°C; spring and autumn run cooler and want a thick suit.
destinations.encounters: Life here is Mediterranean and resident: dusky grouper stationed on the rock, morays in every second crevice, octopus hunting across the boulders. Summer lifts the tempo — anthias cloud the walls, barracuda school, tuna pass offshore and glassfish pack the caverns and the Santa Maria's hull. As the water cools in autumn, octopus and cuttlefish work the black sand while the reefs settle quiet.

destinations.name: Tenerife

Jan.marine_life: angel sharks lying on the sand while stingrays gather and turtles cruise El Puertito
Jan.conditions: coolest water but crisp winter visibility with the south coast staying sheltered

Feb.marine_life: angel sharks encountered often while rays pair up in the caves beside morays and octopus
Feb.conditions: cool and clear with occasional Atlantic swell on exposed corners

Mar.marine_life: last reliable angel sharks lingering while stingrays, cuttlefish and trumpetfish are everywhere
Mar.conditions: clear late-winter water with seas settling as spring arrives

Apr.marine_life: angel sharks thinning out while turtles, barracuda schools and reef fish carry the show
Apr.conditions: transitional water still cool but generally calm with good visibility

May.marine_life: turtles about while bull rays cruise the drop-offs beside big schools of bream and roncadores
May.conditions: calm spring seas with warming water and reliably clear conditions

Jun.marine_life: rays staying active while turtles show daily at El Puertito with barracuda and bastard grunt schools
Jun.conditions: warm settled early summer with trade winds picking up above water

Jul.marine_life: turtles and stingrays about while trumpetfish cloud up and juveniles fill the volcanic reef
Jul.conditions: warm and busy with brief upwellings trimming visibility on some days

Aug.marine_life: stingray aggregations building off Las Galletas with turtles, octopus and cuttlefish about
Aug.conditions: warmest air and lively resorts with calm sea and occasional plankton haze

Sep.marine_life: bull rays and big barracuda cruising warm water while turtles and dense fish schools gather
Sep.conditions: sea at its warmest and calmest with long settled spells

Oct.marine_life: rays and turtles everywhere while roncador schools pack thick over the wrecks
Oct.conditions: warm water lingering with glassy autumn seas and excellent visibility

Nov.marine_life: stingrays massing while turtles roam and the first angel sharks edge into the shallows
Nov.conditions: still-warm water with quiet sites and clear autumn conditions

Dec.marine_life: angel sharks moving back in while rays, turtles and cuttlefish work the sand flats
Dec.conditions: mild winter with sheltered south coast diving and visibility sharpening

destinations.highlights: Volcanic Atlantic diving off Europe's doorstep — green turtles over the El Puertito seagrass, stingray aggregations off Las Galletas, the basalt-column Condesito wreck, and one of the world's few reliable winter windows on critically endangered angel sharks.
destinations.currents: The leeward south coast is well sheltered from the prevailing northeast trades, so most sites see little to no current. Exposed headlands and offshore banks can pick up moderate flow when Atlantic swell or spring tides run.
destinations.current_note: Mostly calm on the sheltered south coast
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm full year-round; add a hooded vest or 7mm Dec-Apr, 3mm fine Aug-Oct

dive_sites.name: El Puertito
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered sandy bay near Armeñime where resident green turtles graze the seagrass at snoozing-pace depths.

dive_sites.name: El Condesito
dive_sites.blurb: Cement carrier lost in 1971, its scattered hull draped against hexagonal basalt columns and patrolled by trumpetfish and morays.

dive_sites.name: Los Chuchos
dive_sites.blurb: The 'city of stingrays' minutes off Las Galletas harbour, with overhangs, a small sunken fishing boat and the island's densest ray population.

dive_sites.name: Montaña Amarilla
dive_sites.blurb: Shore-access lunar landscape of yellow volcanic tuff with terraces, swim-throughs and winter angel shark sightings.

dive_sites.name: Tabaiba Wreck (El Peñón)
dive_sites.blurb: Intact 35m tug scuttled off Tabaiba, upright at 30m with engine-room penetration for advanced divers.

dive_sites.name: Palm-Mar Cave
dive_sites.blurb: Galleried lava cave below a 17m plateau where Atlantic stingrays gather to court in winter.

destinations.description: Tenerife turns volcanic geology into a year-round Atlantic playground: basalt columns, lava arches and yellow tuff terraces in water that never leaves the high teens. Green turtles hold court at El Puertito, stingrays mass off Las Galletas, and from December to March the sand hides critically endangered angel sharks — one of the few places on earth you can count on them. Easy shore entries and short boat rides suit new divers; wrecks and lava caves keep experienced hands busy.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Tuxyso (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.underwater: Volcanic geology shapes every dive. El Condesito's cement carrier lies scattered against hexagonal basalt columns in easy shallows, while Palm-Mar's galleried lava cave drops below a 17 m plateau for advanced hands. Los Chuchos, the 'city of stingrays', hides overhangs and a small sunken boat off Las Galletas harbour. Water holds in the high teens to low 20s°C with viz commonly 18 to 25 m, and the sheltered south coast stays calm through most of the year.
destinations.encounters: The winter draw is the angel shark - critically endangered and lying camouflaged on the sand from December into March, one of the few reliable windows on earth. Green turtles graze the El Puertito seagrass year-round, and round stingrays mass off Las Galletas, thickening through late summer and autumn. Bull rays cruise the drop-offs while trumpetfish, octopus and Canary morays fill the volcanic reef.

destinations.name: Zanzibar

Jan.marine_life: whale sharks passing offshore while turtles pack Mnemba and dolphins work the channel
Jan.conditions: kaskazi monsoon bringing warm calm seas and superb visibility

Feb.marine_life: reef fish forming spawning aggregations while green turtles and resident bottlenose dolphins patrol
Feb.conditions: hot and settled with glassy mornings and top-of-year visibility

Mar.marine_life: turtles and Napoleon wrasse holding while late-season whale sharks linger before the rains
Mar.conditions: calmest seas of the year as kaskazi fades with the warmest water

Apr.marine_life: reef life carrying on unchanged while runoff and plankton limit the sightings
Apr.conditions: masika long rains with heavy downpours, reduced visibility and some boats pausing

May.marine_life: macro life rewarding those who go while the big animals stay scarce
May.conditions: tail end of the long rains with choppy seas and patchy visibility

Jun.marine_life: healthy reef action returning while turtles and schooling trevally gather at Leven Bank
Jun.conditions: dry season beginning with a kusi breeze and cooling clearing water

Jul.marine_life: pelagics working the northern banks while dense reef fish cover Mnemba
Jul.conditions: kusi monsoon wind with some chop on crossings but good visibility

Aug.marine_life: first humpback whales migrating past Leven Bank and the Mnemba channel
Aug.conditions: breezy kusi winds with coolest water and reliable visibility

Sep.marine_life: humpback whales gathering in numbers while barracuda and tuna school offshore
Sep.conditions: winds easing with dry clear weather and excellent visibility

Oct.marine_life: late humpbacks overlapping with the first whale sharks while turtles roam everywhere
Oct.conditions: calm inter-monsoon lull with flat seas and superb visibility

Nov.marine_life: whale sharks building in numbers while dolphins and rays fill the channels
Nov.conditions: short vuli rains bringing brief showers with seas mostly workable

Dec.marine_life: whale sharks showing most frequently while nesting turtles stay active at Mnemba
Dec.conditions: kaskazi setting in with warm water and visibility climbing again

destinations.highlights: Mnemba Atoll's turtle-packed coral slopes and the current-swept pelagic seamount of Leven Bank, with humpback whales passing in September and whale sharks from October to February.
destinations.currents: Tides of up to five metres drive currents that range from slack to brisk on the Mnemba and Tumbatu reefs, making gentle drifts the norm. Leven Bank in the open Pemba Channel is another matter, with strong, unpredictable current that demands drift experience.
destinations.current_note: gentle reef drifts inshore; strong and shifty at Leven Bank
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm shorty or full suit Nov-Apr; 3mm-5mm full in the cooler Jun-Sep season

dive_sites.name: Kichwani
dive_sites.blurb: Mnemba's classic sloping wall drift, dense with hard and soft coral, trevally, morays and well-camouflaged leaf fish.

dive_sites.name: Wattabomi
dive_sites.blurb: A gentle Mnemba slope of bommies and plate coral where green turtles are near-guaranteed and dolphins sometimes cruise through mid-dive.

dive_sites.name: Big Wall
dive_sites.blurb: Mnemba's vertical face of overhangs and sponge-crusted ledges, drawing tuna, barracuda, rays and the occasional hammerhead off the blue.

dive_sites.name: Leven Bank
dive_sites.blurb: An open-ocean seamount in the Pemba Channel where strong current pulls in kingfish, tuna, big morays and schooling barracuda.

dive_sites.name: Hunga Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A circular open-water reef off Nungwi known for huge coral bommies and consistently sharp visibility.

dive_sites.name: Tumbatu Coral Gardens
dive_sites.blurb: Quiet, current-brushed gardens off Tumbatu Island regarded as Zanzibar's healthiest coral, with seahorses and turtles among the highlights.

destinations.description: Zanzibar pairs easy tropical reef diving with a genuine big-animal calendar. Day boats work Mnemba Atoll's coral slopes, where green turtles and dolphins are routine, while Leven Bank's current-raked seamount gives experienced divers kingfish, tuna and barracuda in the Pemba Channel. Two monsoons shape the year: kaskazi brings warm, glassy water from December to March, kusi cooler humpback-season seas from June to October, and the April-May long rains close the window. Suits new divers and drift-hungry veterans alike.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: I've Got It On Film! (CC BY 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.underwater: Tides of up to 5 m set the tempo, so most Mnemba dives are gentle drifts: you ride the flow along Kichwani's coral-dense slope and settle over Wattabomi's bommies as turtles graze on cue. Big Wall drops a vertical face of overhangs and sponge-crusted ledges from 14 m toward 70 m, while Leven Bank is a different animal, an open-ocean seamount where strong, shifting current demands drift experience. Water holds 25-29°C, and visibility runs about 25 m in the calm dry seasons.
destinations.encounters: Green turtles are near-guaranteed on Mnemba's slopes, and bottlenose dolphins sometimes sweep through mid-dive. The big-animal calendar does the rest: humpback whales pass from August into October, and whale sharks show through the kaskazi months, most frequently around December. Leven Bank stacks kingfish, tuna and schooling barracuda, while sharp eyes find leaf fish at Kichwani and seahorses off Tumbatu.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Niceley (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Dahab

Jan.marine_life: resident reef fish, morays and blue-spotted rays; quiet, fishy reefs
Jan.conditions: coolest water, occasional wind chop at exposed entries, clear Gulf of Aqaba viz

Feb.marine_life: healthy coral gardens, octopus and nudibranchs for slow macro dives
Feb.conditions: cold-for-the-Red-Sea water, breezy days, otherwise calm shore entries

Mar.marine_life: reef life waking up; garden eels, lionfish and schooling anthias
Mar.conditions: water at its coolest, wind easing, viz staying reliably clear

Apr.marine_life: anthias clouds thicken, turtles grazing the seagrass, first pelagics passing
Apr.conditions: warming fast, flat mornings, comfortable multi-dive days

May.marine_life: busy reefs, unicornfish and fusilier schools, frogfish finds for sharp eyes
May.conditions: warm, calm and settled — classic easy Dahab shore diving

Jun.marine_life: full-summer reef activity, jacks and barracuda off the walls, turtles common
Jun.conditions: flat glassy water, long days, excellent visibility

Jul.marine_life: pelagics closer inshore, dolphins occasionally cruise the Blue Hole
Jul.conditions: hot topside, warm calm sea, viz climbing toward its best

Aug.marine_life: warmest water brings dense fish life and the odd reef shark sighting
Aug.conditions: very hot on land, dead-calm water, peak 30m-plus visibility days

Sep.marine_life: reefs at their liveliest — trevally hunts, turtles, morays out in the open
Sep.conditions: warm water lingering, calm seas, superb visibility

Oct.marine_life: abundant reef fish, rays on the sand, great macro on the house reefs
Oct.conditions: ideal balance of warm water, mild air and clear, settled seas

Nov.marine_life: reefs still busy; octopus, seahorses in the seagrass, schooling snapper
Nov.conditions: cooling gently, mostly calm with occasional breezy afternoons

Dec.marine_life: resident reef community, uncrowded sites, relaxed critter hunting
Dec.conditions: cooler water and windier spells, but shore diving continues year-round

destinations.highlights: Walk-in Gulf of Aqaba diving at its most laid-back — the Blue Hole via El Bells, the Canyon's cathedral cracks, Lighthouse house-reef ease and a deep technical scene, all from the beach.
destinations.currents: The Gulf of Aqaba is sheltered and tide-weak, so most Dahab sites sit in still or gently drifting water. Wind can add surface chop and a mild longshore push at exposed entries like the Blue Hole, but true current is rare.
destinations.current_note: calm, near-currentless shore diving most days
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm in high summer (Jul-Sep); 5mm spring and autumn; 5-7mm with hood Dec-Mar

dive_sites.name: Blue Hole & El Bells
dive_sites.blurb: The classic drop down the Bells chimney, along the outer wall and over the 7m saddle into the world's most famous sinkhole.

dive_sites.name: The Arch (Blue Hole)
dive_sites.blurb: The infamous tunnel through the Blue Hole's wall at 55m — a serious, training-and-trimix-only technical dive.

dive_sites.name: The Canyon
dive_sites.blurb: A narrow crack in the reef plate that swallows divers into a fishbowl cavern lit by shafts of blue light.

dive_sites.name: Lighthouse Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Dahab's house reef in the middle of town — easy sloping coral, resident frogfish and seahorses, and the go-to night dive.

dive_sites.name: Eel Garden
dive_sites.blurb: A sandy amphitheatre where hundreds of garden eels sway in formation below a pretty coral entrance channel.

dive_sites.name: The Islands
dive_sites.blurb: A maze of hard-coral pinnacles and swim-through lagoons south of town — arguably Dahab's prettiest coral dive.

dive_sites.name: Gabr el Bint
dive_sites.blurb: A remote fan-coral wall reached by boat or camel along the coast, rewarding the trek with pristine gorgonians.

destinations.description: Dahab is the Red Sea at its most relaxed: a small Sinai town on the Gulf of Aqaba where nearly everything is dived from the beach. Gear up, wade in, and you're on the Canyon's glowing cavern, Lighthouse's critter-rich house reef or the Blue Hole via the Bells. Water stays calm and clear most of the year, winters run cooler, and a serious technical community pushes the deep lines. Ideal for low-stress repetitive diving, training and long stays.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: No machine-readable author provided. Nowic assumed (based on copyright claims). (Public domain) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.underwater: Entries here are walks, not boat drops: across the reef plate and into water that barely moves, since the Gulf of Aqaba is tide-weak and true current is rare. Down the wall the terrain turns theatrical — a sandy amphitheatre of swaying garden eels, the hard-coral maze of The Islands, Gabr el Bint's gorgonian fans reached by boat or camel — while trained technical divers push the Arch at 55 m. Summer runs 28°C with 30 m visibility; winter cools to 21°C.
destinations.encounters: Napoleon wrasse and green turtles are the big residents, turtles grazing the seagrass while blue-spotted ribbontail rays settle on the sand between coral heads. The reefs run on smaller finds — resident frogfish and seahorses on the house reef, hundreds of garden eels swaying in formation, octopus and nudibranchs in the cooler months — with jacks, barracuda and the odd reef shark off the walls in high summer.

destinations.name: Hurghada

Jan.marine_life: resident reef fish, moray eels, blue-spotted rays; quiet reefs and occasional dolphins in the lagoons
Jan.conditions: coolest water, frequent northerly wind and choppy crossings; sheltered sites stay diveable and shallow viz can be superb

Feb.marine_life: reef fish dense on nearshore reefs, Napoleon wrasse, big morays; little pelagic action
Feb.conditions: coldest month at 21-22C, windy with surface chop; outer reefs like Careless often blown out

Mar.marine_life: reefs waking up, spinner dolphins returning to Shaab El Erg, eagle rays passing the walls
Mar.conditions: water warming, winds easing but still gusty spells; clear water on calm days

Apr.marine_life: spinner dolphins reliable, turtles, schooling snapper and fusiliers thickening on the pinnacles
Apr.conditions: calm, predictable seas and light wind; comfortable crossings to Abu Nuhas

May.marine_life: dolphins, grey reef and whitetip sharks patrolling Careless Reef, big jacks and barracuda
May.conditions: flat seas, 25-26C water, excellent light; prime window for the wreck runs

Jun.marine_life: reef sharks at the offshore pinnacles, tuna and trevally hunting, turtles nesting season
Jun.conditions: warm settled summer sea, gentle breeze; long days ideal for two-tank wreck trips

Jul.marine_life: peak fish density on the reefs, occasional hammerhead in the blue off Careless and Umm Gamar
Jul.conditions: hot topside, 28C water and mostly calm seas; some afternoon wind chop

Aug.marine_life: everything on the reef plus pelagics in deeper water; mild plankton haze feeds the food chain
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year at 28-29C; hot deck, easy diving, slightly softer viz

Sep.marine_life: sharks and jacks still active, dolphins daily, reef life at its densest after summer
Sep.conditions: seas settle again, water still 28C, crowds thin; superb all-round conditions

Oct.marine_life: predators drawn in by baitfish, spinner dolphin pods, eagle rays over the plateaus
Oct.conditions: calm seas, 27C water and some of the clearest viz of the year

Nov.marine_life: dolphins, Napoleon wrasse, hunting trevally; last of the warm-water pelagic action
Nov.conditions: still 25C and mostly calm early month; wind builds late November

Dec.marine_life: reef life steady, dolphins sheltering in the lagoons; pelagics scarce
Dec.conditions: cooling to 23C with windy days and winter chop; site choice shifts to sheltered reefs

destinations.highlights: Easy warm-water reef diving out of a big dive town, with the Giftun Island coral gardens, the four-wreck graveyard of Abu Nuhas and shark-patrolled offshore pinnacles all within day-boat range.
destinations.currents: Most Giftun-area reefs see gentle to moderate currents that suit relaxed drift dives. Exposed offshore pinnacles like Careless Reef and the northern tips of Umm Gamar can run stronger when the wind is up.
destinations.current_note: mild on inshore reefs, stronger at exposed pinnacles
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full in summer (Jun-Oct); 5mm in spring and autumn; 5-7mm with hood for 21-23C winter water (Dec-Mar)

dive_sites.name: Giannis D
dive_sites.blurb: Tilted Greek freighter on Abu Nuhas with an intact stern and a glassfish-filled engine room that makes one of the Red Sea's best easy penetrations.

dive_sites.name: Carnatic
dive_sites.blurb: An 1869 steam-sail ship whose exposed ribs are now sheathed in soft coral, the most atmospheric of the Abu Nuhas graveyard.

dive_sites.name: Chrisoula K
dive_sites.blurb: The 'tile wreck' of Abu Nuhas, a cargo ship still stacked with Italian floor tiles and riddled with swim-throughs.

dive_sites.name: Careless Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Twin pinnacles rising from open water an hour offshore, the area's most reliable spot for grey reef sharks and the odd hammerhead in the blue.

dive_sites.name: Umm Gamar
dive_sites.blurb: Crescent island with a plateau of coral towers, a cavern swim-through at 28m and eagle rays cruising the drop-off.

dive_sites.name: Shaab El Erg (Dolphin House)
dive_sites.blurb: Horseshoe reef between Hurghada and El Gouna whose sheltered lagoon is a daytime rest stop for resident spinner dolphins.

dive_sites.name: Small Giftun
dive_sites.blurb: Classic drift along a soft-coral wall on Giftun Island's east side, dense with fan corals, turtles and passing eagle rays.

destinations.description: Hurghada is the Red Sea's workhorse dive hub, and it earns the traffic: warm water all year, 20-30m visibility, and day boats fanning out to the Giftun reefs, dolphin lagoons and the four-wreck graveyard at Abu Nuhas. Sheltered coral gardens make it a natural place to learn, while the Carnatic and Giannis D give wreck divers real substance. Winter brings wind and chop, so spring and autumn are the sweet spots.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Caprice4540 (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.underwater: Day boats split the diving three ways: gentle drifts along Small Giftun's soft-coral wall, the four-wreck run at Abu Nuhas, where the Giannis D's engine room swirls with glassfish and the Carnatic's coral-sheathed ribs date to 1869, and exposed pinnacles like Careless Reef rising from open water an hour offshore. Summer water reaches 28-29°C in a 3mm; winter cools to 21-22°C and pushes boats onto sheltered reefs. Visibility holds a reliable 20-30 m.
destinations.encounters: Spinner dolphins rest up in the lagoon at Shaab El Erg and appear almost daily through the warm months. Grey reef sharks patrol Careless Reef from late spring, with the occasional hammerhead in the blue at midsummer, while Napoleon wrasse, giant morays and blue-spotted rays anchor the reefs year-round and eagle rays cruise the plateaus in autumn.

destinations.name: Marsa Alam
destinations.difficulty: beginner-friendly bays to advanced offshore walls

Jan.marine_life: Dugong and green turtles in the seagrass bays; hammerheads gathering at Daedalus for liveaboards
Jan.conditions: Coolest water, windy with chop on offshore crossings; inshore viz at its annual peak

Feb.marine_life: Turtles and dugong inshore; big pelagic chances offshore for hardy divers
Feb.conditions: Cool 22-23C water and brisk north winds; crystal winter visibility

Mar.marine_life: Reef life waking up; turtles, napoleons and early manta sightings on outer reefs
Mar.conditions: Winds easing, water still cool; long clear sightlines

Apr.marine_life: Spring pelagic pulse — oceanic whitetip and hammerhead encounters pick up offshore
Apr.conditions: Warming, calmer seas open up Elphinstone and the far south

May.marine_life: Hammerheads schooling at Daedalus; spinner dolphins resident at Sataya and Samadai
May.conditions: Calm, warm and comfortable — prime crossing weather

Jun.marine_life: Dolphins, turtles and dense reef fish activity; mantas possible on outer walls
Jun.conditions: Hot and settled, warm 27C water, viz softens slightly with plankton

Jul.marine_life: Spinner dolphin pods at their most reliable; turtles nesting season inshore
Jul.conditions: Very hot ashore, bathwater sea, flat mornings

Aug.marine_life: Warmest water draws reef sharks, dolphins and rays; whitetips begin patrolling Elphinstone
Aug.conditions: 29C water and calm seas but the busiest reefs of the year

Sep.marine_life: Oceanic whitetips arriving at Elphinstone; dolphins, turtles and dugong all in play
Sep.conditions: Warm water, mild wind, thinning crowds — the sweet spot begins

Oct.marine_life: Oceanic whitetip season in full swing on the offshore plateaus; hammerheads at Daedalus
Oct.conditions: Warm 27-28C water, superb 30m+ visibility, settled seas

Nov.marine_life: Peak oceanic whitetip encounters at Elphinstone; dugong and turtles steady inshore
Nov.conditions: Excellent viz, comfortable water, first north winds returning

Dec.marine_life: Whitetips lingering offshore into early winter; seagrass bays busy with turtles
Dec.conditions: Cooling water and breezier crossings; inshore diving stays easy and clear

destinations.highlights: Southern Red Sea base pairing easy fringing-reef shore diving and dugong seagrass bays with world-class pelagic walls — oceanic whitetips at Elphinstone from October to December, hammerheads at Daedalus and spinner dolphin pods at Sataya.
destinations.currents: Sheltered bays and house reefs sit in near-still water, but the offshore reefs are another game: Elphinstone and Daedalus run north-south currents that can exceed a knot, with occasional downcurrents off the plateau edges.
destinations.current_note: calm inshore; strong, shifting drift on offshore pinnacles
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full Jun-Sep; 5mm in spring and autumn; 7mm or hooded 5mm Dec-Mar

dive_sites.name: Elphinstone Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Open-sea finger reef whose north and south plateaus draw patrolling oceanic whitetip sharks from October to December.

dive_sites.name: Abu Dabbab
dive_sites.blurb: Sandy seagrass bay entered from the beach, famous for resident green turtles and rare dugong encounters.

dive_sites.name: Sha'ab Samadai (Dolphin House)
dive_sites.blurb: Horseshoe lagoon sheltering a resident spinner dolphin pod, with sunlit caverns threading the reef's south side.

dive_sites.name: Sataya (Dolphin Reef)
dive_sites.blurb: Vast crescent reef in the deep south where big spinner dolphin pods rest inside the lagoon.

dive_sites.name: Daedalus Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Remote liveaboard-range pinnacle ringed by sheer walls, the Red Sea's most reliable stop for schooling scalloped hammerheads.

dive_sites.name: Marsa Shagra House Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Classic fringing-reef shore dive with twin entry channels and unhurried coral gardens divable on your own schedule.

destinations.description: Marsa Alam is the quieter, wilder counterweight to Egypt's northern resorts: a long ribbon of fringing reef where you can shore dive straight off the sand, then boat out to serious pelagic theatre. Elphinstone's plateaus host oceanic whitetips from October to December, Abu Dabbab's seagrass shelters dugong and green turtles, and Daedalus delivers hammerheads in liveaboard range. It suits relaxed reef divers and shark-focused drift specialists alike — often on the same trip.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Kora27 (CC BY-SA 4.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.underwater: The fringing reef starts at the sand, so you can wade in at Marsa Shagra and drop over unhurried coral gardens on your own schedule. Offshore is a different animal: Elphinstone and Daedalus rise as sheer walls to 40 m, swept by north-south currents that can top a knot, with occasional downcurrents off the plateau edges. Water runs from a 7mm-cold 23°C in late winter to 29°C in August, and winter visibility inshore hits its 30 m annual peak.
destinations.encounters: Oceanic whitetips patrol Elphinstone's plateaus from October into December, and Daedalus is the Red Sea's most reliable stop for schooling scalloped hammerheads. Inshore, the seagrass at Abu Dabbab shelters green turtles and, on a lucky day, a dugong, while resident spinner dolphin pods rest inside the lagoons at Samadai and Sataya. Napoleon wrasse and dense reef fish fill out the walls.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo: Panegyrics of Granovetter (CC BY-SA 2.0) via Wikimedia Commons

destinations.name: Sharm El Sheikh

Jan.marine_life: reef sharks and eagle rays cruise the walls; dense anthias and reef fish year-round
Jan.conditions: coolest water (22-23C) and windy days in the Straits; calm days bring superb 30m+ viz

Feb.marine_life: manta and eagle ray sightings pick up; reef life steady, few schooling events
Feb.conditions: chilly water, occasional northerly blows cancel Tiran runs; clear blue water

Mar.marine_life: spring plankton pulses draw rays and the odd whale shark rumour; turtles active
Mar.conditions: water warming from 22C; some plankton haze and breezy crossings

Apr.marine_life: reef fish spawning begins, turtles and Napoleon wrasse reliable at Ras Mohammed
Apr.conditions: settling seas, 23-24C water, viz cleaning up nicely

May.marine_life: first snapper and batfish aggregations gather off Shark Reef; pelagics returning
May.conditions: calm, warm (25C) and clear — a comfortable pre-peak window

Jun.marine_life: summer schooling season kicks off: bohar snapper, unicornfish and barracuda mass at Ras Mohammed
Jun.conditions: flat seas, 27C water, excellent 30m visibility

Jul.marine_life: spawning aggregations at their densest — snapper walls, batfish, jacks; hammerheads off Jackson Reef
Jul.conditions: hot, glassy summer conditions and the year's best visibility

Aug.marine_life: schooling fish still thick; scalloped hammerheads in the blue at Tiran
Aug.conditions: warmest water (28-29C), calm crossings to Tiran and the Thistlegorm

Sep.marine_life: late-season schools linger, hammerhead chances remain, reef life in full colour
Sep.conditions: warm 28C water, settled seas and thinner boat traffic

Oct.marine_life: aggregations disperse but eagle rays, turtles and reef sharks are dependable
Oct.conditions: superb autumn window — 27C water, calm and clear

Nov.marine_life: healthy reef action, Napoleon wrasse and grey reef sharks on the points
Nov.conditions: cooling to 25C, mostly calm with very clean water

Dec.marine_life: quieter blue water; anthias-covered walls and macro carry the diving
Dec.conditions: 24C dropping, occasional wind chop, winter clarity on calm days

destinations.highlights: Anthias-carpeted walls at Ras Mohammed, the summer spawning aggregations of snapper and barracuda off Shark and Yolanda reefs, drift dives across the four Tiran reefs and day trips to the SS Thistlegorm.
destinations.currents: Tidal currents funnel through the Straits of Tiran and sweep the exposed corners of Ras Mohammed, so most signature dives are run as drifts. Sheltered local bays and house reefs stay gentle, which is why the area trains so many new divers.
destinations.current_note: strong on Tiran and Ras Mohammed corners; sheltered bays stay calm
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm full Jun-Oct; 5mm in spring and autumn; 7mm (or 5mm + hooded vest) Dec-Mar at 22-23C

dive_sites.name: Shark & Yolanda Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Twin pinnacles at the tip of Ras Mohammed where summer snapper and barracuda schools swirl above the scattered toilet-bowl cargo of the Yolanda.

dive_sites.name: SS Thistlegorm
dive_sites.blurb: WWII supply ship whose holds are still packed with motorbikes, trucks and rifles — the Red Sea's most famous wreck, reached on early-start day boats.

dive_sites.name: Jackson Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Northernmost Tiran reef with a soft-coral 'garden' and summer scalloped hammerheads hanging off its exposed north face.

dive_sites.name: Thomas Reef
dive_sites.blurb: Smallest and prettiest of the Tiran four, ringed by soft corals and gorgonians, with a deep canyon that draws technical divers.

dive_sites.name: Ras Um Sid
dive_sites.blurb: Local headland wall famous for one of the Red Sea's densest gorgonian fan forests, diveable from shore or boat in most weather.

dive_sites.name: Jackfish Alley
dive_sites.blurb: Sandy-alley drift inside Ras Mohammed with swim-through caves at the start and resting whitetips and jacks along the run.

destinations.description: Sharm El Sheikh is the northern Red Sea's classic dive hub, where desert mountains drop into anthias-carpeted walls at Ras Mohammed and tidal drifts sweep the four reefs of the Tiran Strait. June to September brings the famous spawning aggregations — snapper, batfish and barracuda massing off Shark and Yolanda — while day boats reach the SS Thistlegorm. Diveable all twelve months, it pairs easy training bays with genuinely big-animal drift diving.
destinations.image_credit: Photo: Roland Unger (CC BY-SA 3.0) via Wikimedia Commons
destinations.underwater: At Shark and Yolanda Reef you drift twin pinnacles from 10 m to past 40 m over the scattered toilet-bowl cargo of the Yolanda, anthias streaming off every coral head. Tidal currents funnel through the Straits of Tiran, so the signature dives run as drifts, while sheltered bays and Ras Um Sid's gorgonian forest stay gentle. Summer water hits 28-29°C with 30 m visibility; winter dips to 22°C. The Thistlegorm, her holds still packed with motorbikes and trucks, is an early-start day trip away.
destinations.encounters: Summer brings the spawning aggregations: walls of bohar snapper, circular batfish and barracuda massing off Shark and Yolanda, with scalloped hammerheads hanging in the blue off Jackson Reef. The rest of the year leans on the residents - Napoleon wrasse, grey reef sharks, eagle rays and turtles - cruising reefs permanently carpeted in orange anthias.
destinations.image_dest_credit: Photo via Pexels

destinations.name: Arraial do Cabo
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm year-round; hooded vest for upwelling days
destinations.current_note: Sheltered inside-sea coves are near-still, but the outer points around Ilha do Cabo Frio and Ilha dos Porcos pick up current and surge when wind and upwelling kick in.
destinations.currents: Calm in the protected coves of the inside sea; moderate flow and surge at the exposed points around Ilha do Cabo Frio and Ilha dos Porcos, strongest when the NE wind and the upwelling are running.
destinations.highlights: Brazil's Capital do Mergulho: around 40 sites minutes from Praia dos Anjos, where the Cabo Frio upwelling pumps cold clear water over a gorgonian forest, an 1827 frigate wreck and the light-flooded Gruta Azul.

Jan.marine_life: green turtles and eagle rays working the gorgonian gardens while grouper and morays hold the rocky points
Jan.conditions: high summer with packed boats — an upwelling pulse can drop the water to 16-18°C overnight while the clarity jumps

Feb.marine_life: seahorses in the sheltered coves and turtles grazing while big reef-fish schools stack over Ilha dos Porcos
Feb.conditions: warm settled summer sea, with the 5mm suit still earning its keep on upwelling days

Mar.marine_life: eagle rays gliding through the sea-fan forest at Anequim while grouper, morays and dense reef fish carry every dive
Mar.conditions: warmest water of the year with lighter crowds after the holidays

Apr.marine_life: turtles and grouper busy on the inside-sea reefs while morays hunt the boulders and seahorses hold the calm coves
Apr.conditions: settled early autumn with comfortable seas and reliable boat schedules

May.marine_life: resident grouper, morays and reef fish steady on the points while the summer crowds of fish thin out
May.conditions: cooling sea with the first SW fronts sending swell into the outer sites

Jun.marine_life: grouper and morays holding the rocky reefs while seahorses sit tight in the sheltered coves
Jun.conditions: winter fronts and swell that can cut visibility, with quieter boats between systems

Jul.marine_life: turtles and reef fish on the calm inside sea while grouper station the deeper rocks
Jul.conditions: cool winter water and passing swells — migrating whales start surfacing offshore, watched from the boat on the ride out

Aug.marine_life: morays, grouper and schooling reef fish concentrated on the sheltered reefs
Aug.conditions: coldest stretch of the year with SW swell days; whale blows on the horizon from the boat

Sep.marine_life: eagle rays returning over the gorgonians while turtles and grouper pick up across the reefs
Sep.conditions: season building as spring NE winds start pulling clear upwelled water onto the sites

Oct.marine_life: turtles, eagle rays and thick reef-fish schools around Ilha dos Porcos
Oct.conditions: clear-water windows widen with the upwelling — cold pulses reward the 5mm suit

Nov.marine_life: seahorses in the coves, morays on the boulders and grouper thickening ahead of summer
Nov.conditions: spring winds trade warmth for clarity as upwelling pulses sweep the cape

Dec.marine_life: green turtles and eagle rays back over the sea fans while big schools crowd the points
Dec.conditions: high season opening with warm surface water and upwelling-driven clarity

dive_sites.name: Gruta Azul (Blue Grotto)
dive_sites.blurb: A 120 m-long grotto in Ilha do Cabo Frio whose entrance sits at about 10 m; sunlight filtering through the opening floods the chamber with electric-blue light. Reserved for divers comfortable in overhead environments.

dive_sites.name: Naufrágio Dona Paula
dive_sites.blurb: Remains of a frigate lost in 1827 off Ilha dos Franceses near Praia Grande; wreckage is scattered from the shallows down to about 25 m and period cannons are still visible on the bottom.

dive_sites.name: Enseada do Cardeiros
dive_sites.blurb: Sheltered cove on Arraial's calm inside sea and the classic first-dive and training spot, with rocky reef slopes and abundant small reef fish over 7-16 m.

dive_sites.name: Saco do Anequim
dive_sites.blurb: Home to the region's largest colony of gorgonians — a genuine sea-fan forest patrolled by spotted eagle rays and dense reef-fish life on the sheltered side of the cape.

dive_sites.name: Ilha dos Porcos
dive_sites.blurb: Island with multiple moorings: easy 9-11 m platforms for novices and deeper rock formations, with points from 12 m to 35 m holding large fish schools for experienced divers.

destinations.description: Arraial do Cabo is Brazil's Capital do Mergulho — a town of about 30,000 that fills with visitors every summer weekend, with around 40 catalogued dive sites a 5-25 minute boat ride from Praia dos Anjos. The cape splits the diving in two: a calm inside sea of training coves and gorgonian gardens, and exposed points around Ilha do Cabo Frio where wind-driven upwelling pulls cold, clear South Atlantic water to the surface. Caverns, an 1827 frigate wreck and a genuine sea-fan forest fill out the roster.
destinations.underwater: The upwelling writes the script: NE winds pull cold, clear South Atlantic Central Water to the surface, so a January dive that starts near 24°C can suddenly read 16-18°C while visibility jumps toward 25 m — hence the 5mm suit year-round. Saco do Anequim holds the region's largest gorgonian colony, patrolled by spotted eagle rays; the Gruta Azul runs 120 m into Ilha do Cabo Frio and floods with electric-blue light; and the frigate Dona Paula scatters cannons from the shallows to 25 m.
destinations.encounters: Green turtles and dusky grouper anchor the reefs year-round, with green and spotted morays in the boulders and longsnout seahorses holding the calm coves. Spotted eagle rays patrol the sea-fan forest at Saco do Anequim, and big fish schools stack over the points at Ilha dos Porcos. From July to October, migrating humpback whales pass along this coast — a surface spectacle watched from the boat on the ride out, not an underwater encounter.

destinations.name: Rio de Janeiro
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm most of the year; 3mm in midsummer
destinations.current_note: Sheltered channels like Ressurreta are easy; the outer sites — Ilha Rasa and the Buenos Aires wreck — commonly carry surface current, so descents go down the line.
destinations.currents: Mild inside the protected Cagarras archipelago; moderate surface current is typical at Ilha Rasa and over the Buenos Aires wreck, where a line descent is standard.
destinations.highlights: Big-city diving with a protected twist: the Cagarras Islands natural monument sits 5 km off Ipanema, backed by Ilha Rasa's walls to 42 m and the scattered 1890s wreck of the steamer Buenos Aires.

Jan.marine_life: green turtles and stingrays on the Tijucas reefs while grouper and octopus work the Cagarras boulders
Jan.conditions: warm summer sea with the calmest crossings and the year's best clarity at the outer islands

Feb.marine_life: big reef-fish schools around the monument with morays in the cracks and dolphins passing through
Feb.conditions: warmest water of the year and steady boat schedules

Mar.marine_life: turtles, stingrays and dense schools over the island reefs while octopus hunt the shallows
Mar.conditions: settled late-summer sea, still warm with long clear windows offshore

Apr.marine_life: grouper and morays holding the walls while turtles graze the sheltered channels
Apr.conditions: mild autumn water and generally workable seas for the offshore runs

May.marine_life: stingrays on the sand flats and reef fish thick in the Ressurreta channel
May.conditions: cooling sea with decent windows between the first cold fronts

Jun.marine_life: resident grouper, morays and octopus steady on the rocky reefs while the schools thin
Jun.conditions: winter fronts bring swell; weekend boats still run from Marina da Gloria

Jul.marine_life: morays and octopus in the boulders with grouper sitting deep on the Rasa walls
Jul.conditions: coolest water of the year and SW swell that limits the outer islands

Aug.marine_life: reef fish and crustaceans carrying the sheltered sites while pelagics stay scarce
Aug.conditions: winter swell and variable clarity, with the inner channels the safer call

Sep.marine_life: turtles reappearing at the Tijucas while grouper and morays hold station
Sep.conditions: late-winter water still cool, with the seas gradually settling

Oct.marine_life: turtles and stingrays active again while the schools rebuild around the monument
Oct.conditions: spring warming with more reliable crossings to Ilha Rasa

Nov.marine_life: dolphins visiting the archipelago while turtles, grouper and octopus fill the reefs
Nov.conditions: warming sea and lengthening calm spells before high summer

Dec.marine_life: turtles, stingrays and big schools across the islands with morays busy in the rocks
Dec.conditions: summer season opening with warm water and clear outer-island days

dive_sites.name: Ilhas Cagarras (main island)
dive_sites.blurb: The flagship dive of the marine monument 5 km off Ipanema: starts shallow and sheltered, then walls and drop-offs past 30 m with reef fish, crustaceans and passing pelagics; dolphins visit through the year.

dive_sites.name: Ressurreta
dive_sites.blurb: Calm channel between islets in the Cagarras archipelago used for first dives and even night dives — easy conditions inside the protected monument.

dive_sites.name: Ilha Rasa
dive_sites.blurb: Lighthouse island regarded as one of Rio's best dives, with rocky walls from 8 m down to 42 m. Conditions swing widely — 2-20 m visibility, 15-23°C — and the sheltered Portinho side offers easier profiles.

dive_sites.name: Naufrágio Buenos Aires
dive_sites.blurb: German steamer that struck Ilha Rasa in the early 1890s and sank on impact; wreckage is scattered from 12 m down to the propeller at about 42 m, usually under surface current — a dive for experienced divers.

dive_sites.name: Ilhas Tijucas
dive_sites.blurb: Archipelago minutes by boat from Barra da Tijuca, between Barra and São Conrado beaches; easy rocky reefs with big schools of fish, stingrays and green turtles.

destinations.description: Brazil's most visited city keeps a genuine dive scene on its doorstep: the Cagarras Islands, a federally protected natural monument where fishing is banned, rise just 5 km off Ipanema beach. Boats run from Marina da Gloria downtown and from Barra da Tijuca — mostly at weekends — to sheltered channels like Ressurreta, the easy turtle reefs of the Ilhas Tijucas, and, for experienced divers, Ilha Rasa's walls to 42 m and the scattered wreck of the steamer Buenos Aires, lost there in the early 1890s.
destinations.underwater: The contrast is the appeal: drift a sheltered channel at Ressurreta over easy rock at 8-12 m, or drop the line at Ilha Rasa where walls fall from 8 m past 40 m and conditions swing hard — visibility from 2 m to 20 m, water from 15°C to 23°C. The Buenos Aires lies broken from 12 m down to its propeller near 42 m, usually under surface current. Summer brings 25-26°C water and the clearest outer-island days; winter fronts cool the sea to around 22°C and push swell into the exposed sites.
destinations.encounters: Green turtles graze the Tijucas reefs and southern stingrays settle on the sand between the boulders, with dusky grouper, morays and octopus resident on the rocky structure year-round. Dolphins visit the protected Cagarras archipelago through the year, and passing pelagics sweep the drop-offs in season. Summer concentrates the schools around the islands; winter leaves the residents to carry quieter, cooler dives.

destinations.name: Salvador
destinations.current_note: The bay is sheltered and calm most days; tidal flow picks up at the bay-mouth wrecks near the Farol da Barra.
destinations.currents: Generally light inside the Baía de Todos os Santos; tidal currents strengthen at the bay-mouth wrecks off the Farol da Barra, so the deep sites are planned for slack tide.
destinations.highlights: Brazil's historic-wreck bay: more than 25 sites in the warm, sheltered Baía de Todos os Santos, from a 6 m training wreck beside the lighthouse to a 1668 Portuguese galleon on white sand at 32 m.

Jan.marine_life: hawksbill turtles over the wrecks while angelfish, parrotfish and snapper schools fill the hulls
Jan.conditions: dry-season calm with the bay at its clearest and warmest

Feb.marine_life: seahorses on the shallow wrecks and grunt schools packed inside the Blackadder
Feb.conditions: hot settled weather with light winds across the bay

Mar.marine_life: stingrays on the sand around the galleon while turtles and reef fish crowd the shallow hulks
Mar.conditions: warmest water of the year and long clear days before the rains

Apr.marine_life: angelfish and parrotfish busy on the encrusted wrecks while snapper hold the shadows
Apr.conditions: first rains arrive — inshore sites can silt up while the outer wrecks stay workable

May.marine_life: hawksbills and seahorses steady on the sheltered wrecks with grunt schools thick in the structure
May.conditions: rainy-season showers with variable inshore clarity

Jun.marine_life: snapper and grunts packed into the hulls while stingrays settle on the sand
Jun.conditions: heaviest rains cut inshore visibility; pick the bay-mouth wrecks on better days

Jul.marine_life: resident angelfish and parrotfish grazing the wreck growth while turtles pass through
Jul.conditions: wet-season lows with runoff staining the inshore sites, water still warm

Aug.marine_life: seahorses holding the quiet shallow wrecks with snapper schools deep in the Cavo Artemidi
Aug.conditions: rains easing late in the month with warm water throughout

Sep.marine_life: hawksbill turtles back over the wrecks while grunt and snapper schools rebuild
Sep.conditions: drying weather and steadily improving clarity across the bay

Oct.marine_life: stingrays, parrotfish and angelfish across the bay-mouth wrecks
Oct.conditions: settled spring seas with slack-tide windows for the deep sites

Nov.marine_life: turtles and seahorses on the shallow hulks while the fish schools thicken toward summer
Nov.conditions: warm pre-season calm with clarity stretching out

Dec.marine_life: hawksbills, angelfish and dense snapper schools across the wrecks
Dec.conditions: dry season returning with the year's best light on the shallow wrecks

dive_sites.name: Naufrágio Blackadder
dive_sites.blurb: Three-masted clipper lost in a 1905 storm off Boa Viagem beach; the roughly 70 m hull rests shallow at about 9 m with the structure still recognisable — Salvador's classic easy wreck.

dive_sites.name: Naufrágio Cavo Artemidi
dive_sites.blurb: 160 m Greek cargo ship wrecked in September 1980, now lying at about 31 m — a big, fish-covered hulk in the bay's warm water and the marquee deep dive.

dive_sites.name: Galeão Sacramento
dive_sites.blurb: Portuguese galleon sunk on 5 May 1668 on the Banco de Santo Antônio, four nautical miles from Porto da Barra; cannons and period anchors rest on white sand at about 32 m — one of Brazil's most historic dives.

dive_sites.name: Germânia & Bretagne
dive_sites.blurb: Two wrecks entwined near the Farol da Barra rocks: the Germânia grounded in 1873 and the Bretagne collided with its remains in 1903. Boilers, masts and anchors are easy to identify in one shallow dive.

dive_sites.name: Naufrágio Maraldi
dive_sites.blurb: Very shallow wreck beside the lighthouse, maxing out at 6 m, with a photogenic boiler at its centre — Salvador's standard try-dive and training site.

destinations.description: Salvador faces the Baía de Todos os Santos, and the bay reads like a wreck ledger: more than 25 catalogued sites, most only minutes from the coast, spanning three centuries of losses. The Portuguese galleon Sacramento, sunk in 1668, rests with its cannons at about 32 m; the entwined Germânia (1873) and Bretagne (1903) share one shallow dive off the Farol da Barra; and the 160 m freighter Cavo Artemidi, lost in 1980, lies at 31 m. Shore entries around the Barra headland and 26-29°C water keep it easy.
destinations.underwater: Wreck diving arranged by depth like a syllabus: the Maraldi's boiler at 6 m beside the lighthouse, the Blackadder's 70 m clipper hull still recognisable at about 9 m, the twin Germânia and Bretagne readable in a single shallow dive, then the galleon's cannons on white sand near 32 m and the fish-covered Cavo Artemidi at 31 m. The sheltered bay stays calm most days, though tidal flow at the bay mouth means planning the deep wrecks for slack tide. Peak-season visibility opens to 15-30 m.
destinations.encounters: Hawksbill turtles work the encrusted hulls while French angelfish and parrotfish graze the growth, and longsnout seahorses hold the quiet shallow wrecks. Snapper and grunt schools pack the interiors — the wrecks act as fish magnets across the sandy bay — with southern stingrays settled on the bottom nearby. Life stays consistent through the year in the warm water; the dry summer simply lets you see more of it.

destinations.name: Rhodes
destinations.current_note: Mostly gentle; the summer meltemi raises surface chop and light current on exposed east-coast capes, while the bays at Kalithea and Lindos stay sheltered.
destinations.currents: Generally light in the sheltered eastern bays; the summer meltemi can push chop and a light current across the exposed capes, but Kalithea and St Paul's Bay stay calm.
destinations.highlights: Sheltered limestone caverns at Kallithea, walls and amphora-strewn bays near Lindos, and the 110 m Giannoula K wreck at Plimiri — warm, clear Aegean diving from shore or day-boat, May through October.

Jan.marine_life: grouper and moray holding deep on the quiet reefs with little moving through
Jan.conditions: mild SE Aegean winter but boats rarely run and storms stir the shallows

Feb.marine_life: resident octopus and moray tucked into the limestone while the reefs sit near-empty
Feb.conditions: coldest stretch of winter with dive centres shut

Mar.marine_life: grouper stirring on the walls as the water begins its slow climb
Mar.conditions: late-winter seas still unsettled and centres yet to open

Apr.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus getting active again across the Kalithea reefs
Apr.conditions: first centres opening with cool water and thick suits

May.marine_life: grouper stationed on the walls while barracuda begin schooling off the capes
May.conditions: season under way with warming water and settled seas

Jun.marine_life: barracuda schooling over the walls while grouper, moray and octopus fill the caverns
Jun.conditions: warm clear water with calm mornings before the meltemi builds

Jul.marine_life: grouper and schooling barracuda on the walls, lionfish now regular on the reefs and the occasional loggerhead turtle passing
Jul.conditions: hot settled weather; meltemi chop on exposed capes while the bays stay flat

Aug.marine_life: reefs at their busiest with barracuda, grouper, octopus and the odd loggerhead turtle cruising the caverns
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year with 30 m-plus visibility on the east coast

Sep.marine_life: grouper and barracuda thick over the walls while lionfish hunt the ledges
Sep.conditions: still-warm sea, easing winds and some of the year's clearest water

Oct.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus staying busy while the barracuda schools begin to thin
Oct.conditions: pleasant water and quieter boats as the season winds down

Nov.marine_life: resident grouper, moray and octopus holding the reefs as the visiting fish depart
Nov.conditions: last boats of the year before most centres close for winter

Dec.marine_life: octopus and moray tucked deep into the limestone while the reefs go quiet
Dec.conditions: mild early-winter sea but dive operations effectively stopped

dive_sites.name: Kallithea Springs (Kalithea Bay)
dive_sites.blurb: Rhodes' signature house-reef beside the 1920s spa rotunda: limestone swim-throughs and mushroom-shaped cave formations in sheltered, clear water, with caves reaching about 19 m. Most local centres teach and night-dive here.

dive_sites.name: The Wall, Kalithea
dive_sites.blurb: A steep wall just outside Kalithea Bay dropping from about 6 m to 30 m, known for lively marine life and the best visibility on the island's east coast.

dive_sites.name: Anthony Quinn Bay (Cave of the Seal)
dive_sites.blurb: A boulder-strewn bay near Faliraki with ancient amphora fragments scattered through the rocks and the Cave of the Seal dropping past 30 m — one of the most dived spots on Rhodes.

dive_sites.name: MV Giannoula K Wreck, Plimiri
dive_sites.blurb: A 110 m Dutch-built cargo ship from 1953 that sank at Plimiri in 1981, lying mostly intact on 22 m with the upper deck at 15 m and the mast almost breaking the surface. Engine-room penetration is possible on a second dive.

dive_sites.name: St Paul's Bay, Lindos
dive_sites.blurb: The almost-enclosed bay beneath the Lindos acropolis, with rock formations and shallow caverns to about 12 m in calm, very clear water.

dive_sites.name: Lindos Walls & Caverns
dive_sites.blurb: Boat dives out of Lindos run walls and caverns in the 12-30 m band with 20 m-plus visibility; grouper, moray eels, octopus and schooling barracuda are the standard cast.

destinations.description: Rhodes spreads real variety along its sheltered east coast: limestone swim-throughs beside the 1920s spa rotunda at Kallithea, a steep wall dropping to 30 m just outside the bay, amphora fragments among the boulders of Anthony Quinn Bay and, at Plimiri in the south, the 110 m cargo ship Giannoula K resting on 22 m with its mast almost breaking the surface. Centres in Rhodes Town, Faliraki, Lindos and Pefkos work it all from shore and day-boat between spring and late autumn.
destinations.underwater: The east coast sets the tone: sheltered, warm and clear, with visibility running 20-30 m through the season and often past 30 m in late summer. Kallithea's caverns and mushroom formations sit in the 3-19 m band, the Wall falls from 6 m to 30 m, and Lindos boats work walls and caverns to 30 m. At Plimiri the Giannoula K reads as a proper wreck dive — upper deck at 15 m, hull on 22 m, engine-room penetration for the trained. Summer water hits 27°C; winter bottoms out near 17°C.
destinations.encounters: Dusky grouper, Mediterranean moray and common octopus are the dependable cast on every reef and inside the caverns, with barracuda schooling off the capes through summer. Lionfish, invasive and now regular across the Dodecanese, hunt the ledges. Loggerhead turtles are an occasional bonus rather than a promise. Winter thins everything out: the residents tuck into the limestone and the visiting schools disperse until the water warms again in May.

destinations.name: Corfu
destinations.current_note: Sheltered west-coast bays are near-still; only the offshore rocks like Colovri pick up mild current, which is what brings in the jacks and barracuda.
destinations.currents: Near-still inside the Paleokastritsa bays and cave systems; a mild flow around the offshore rocks at Colovri and Skeloudi is the exception, and it switches on the schooling fish.
destinations.highlights: Ionian cavern diving at its most concentrated — Colovri's wall and swim-throughs, the roofless Hole of Ha and the arch-and-tunnel Monastery site, all minutes from Paleokastritsa in visibility that often reaches 30 m.

Jan.marine_life: grouper and moray sitting deep in the caverns with little else moving
Jan.conditions: wet, windy Ionian winter with dive centres closed

Feb.marine_life: resident octopus and moray tucked into the limestone while the west coast sits empty
Feb.conditions: cold rough mid-winter sea with no boats running

Mar.marine_life: grouper stirring in the caves as the coldest water of the year starts to warm
Mar.conditions: sea at its 14°C minimum and operators still shut

Apr.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus waking across the Paleokastritsa caverns with nudibranchs on the walls
Apr.conditions: centres preparing to open; cool water, thick suits and rain-stirred visibility

May.marine_life: grouper on station at Colovri while dentex and the first barracuda patrol the drop-offs
May.conditions: season opening with warming water and the west coast clearing

Jun.marine_life: barracuda and jacks pulled onto Colovri by the mild current while grouper hold the wall
Jun.conditions: warm settled sea with long visibility through the cave systems

Jul.marine_life: grouper, dentex and schooling barracuda around the offshore rocks with octopus and nudibranchs in the caverns
Jul.conditions: calm, clear high summer with the caves at their brightest

Aug.marine_life: barracuda schools, hunting dentex and grouper thick on Colovri's wall
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year and visibility often past 30 m offshore

Sep.marine_life: dentex and barracuda hunting over the walls while moray and octopus show on every cavern dive
Sep.conditions: still-warm sea with settled early-autumn weather

Oct.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus staying active while the schooling fish thin out
Oct.conditions: mild water and the last full boat schedules of the year

Nov.marine_life: resident grouper, moray and octopus holding the caverns as autumn rain clouds the shallows
Nov.conditions: centres closing down as wind and rain arrive

Dec.marine_life: octopus and moray tucked deep into the rock while the caves sit dark and quiet
Dec.conditions: mild but wet and windy early winter with operators closed

dive_sites.name: Colovri Rock
dive_sites.blurb: An uninhabited islet off Paleokastritsa and Corfu's most famous dive: a maze of boulders, crevices and swim-throughs over a steep wall, with a cave and tunnel and dives to 40 m — the seabed beyond drops far deeper. Visibility regularly hits 30 m.

dive_sites.name: Hole of Ha
dive_sites.blurb: An easy cavern dive that goes inside the mountain to an open, roofless chamber where divers look up at trees ringing the skylight; stalactites and fossilised conch shells line the rock. Max depth about 18 m.

dive_sites.name: Monastery (Paleokastritsa)
dive_sites.blurb: 100 m off the monastery headland: an impressive arch at 15 m leading to a 10 m tunnel, an open-roofed cave with its entrance at just 3 m, and a deeper, colour-packed second cave at 22 m.

dive_sites.name: Skeloudi
dive_sites.blurb: A rock near Paleokastritsa with underwater cave chambers and dramatic formations to 40 m, patrolled by barracuda, grouper, moray eels, octopus and crayfish; visibility often exceeds 30 m with low current.

dive_sites.name: Paleokastritsa Caves
dive_sites.blurb: The west coast's multi-chamber limestone cave systems carved into the cliffs around Paleokastritsa, dived in the 8-25 m band with 25 m-plus visibility — the reason the resort became Corfu's diving hub.

destinations.description: Corfu's diving concentrates on the west coast around Paleokastritsa, where the limestone cliffs continue underwater as a network of caves, arches and tunnels. Colovri, an uninhabited islet offshore, is the marquee dive — boulders, crevices and swim-throughs over a wall running to 40 m — while the Hole of Ha goes inside the mountain itself to a roofless chamber ringed by trees. Centres at Paleokastritsa, Ermones and Nissaki run roughly May to October, working some two dozen mapped sites from shore and day-boat.
destinations.underwater: This is cavern country. The Monastery site stacks an arch at 15 m, a 10 m tunnel and an open-roofed cave whose entrance sits at just 3 m; the Hole of Ha ends in a skylight chamber lined with stalactites and fossilised conch shells. Offshore, Colovri and Skeloudi drop to 40 m in visibility that regularly reaches 30 m, while the bays stay near-still. Summer water reaches 26°C; the March sea bottoms out around 14°C, the coldest of the year.
destinations.encounters: Dusky grouper hold the walls at Colovri and Skeloudi, with Mediterranean moray, common octopus and nudibranchs filling the caverns. When mild current brushes the offshore rocks it switches the fish on: barracuda and jacks arrive to hunt, and common dentex patrol the drop-offs through the warm months. In winter the west coast empties, the residents sit deep in the limestone, and the caves wait quietly for the boats to return in May.

destinations.name: Zakynthos
destinations.current_note: The sheltered south coast inside the marine park is usually still; a modest current at Barracuda Reef is what switches on the schooling fish.
destinations.currents: Generally still along the protected south coast and inside the Keri caves; Barracuda Reef is the exception, where a modest flow gathers the schools around the pinnacle.
destinations.highlights: Loggerhead turtle encounters over the marine-park reefs at Marathonisi, plus a cave-riddled Keri coast with a 75 m blue cavern, deep walls and a barracuda pinnacle — all worked from one small harbour.

Jan.marine_life: moray and octopus deep in the limestone while the caves sit empty
Jan.conditions: quiet winter island with no dive boats running

Feb.marine_life: resident grouper holding deep off Keri with little else moving
Feb.conditions: coldest sea of the year and every dive base closed

Mar.marine_life: grouper and moray stirring along the Keri walls as the water slowly warms
Mar.conditions: still winter-quiet with operators dark until spring

Apr.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus getting active again along the cave walls
Apr.conditions: bases readying for the season with cool water and thick suits

May.marine_life: loggerhead turtles possible around Marathonisi while grouper hold the Keri walls
May.conditions: season opening with clearing water and calm seas inside the marine park

Jun.marine_life: loggerhead turtles cruising the Marathonisi shallows while barracuda gather on the pinnacle
Jun.conditions: warm clear water with the Keri caves at their glass-clear best

Jul.marine_life: loggerhead turtles on the marine-park reefs, schooling barracuda when the current runs and the occasional monk seal resting in the Keri caverns
Jul.conditions: hot flat-calm high season across the sheltered south coast

Aug.marine_life: turtles gliding over the Marathonisi reef while grouper, moray and octopus fill the cave walls
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year with superb clarity through the caverns

Sep.marine_life: loggerhead turtles still meeting divers at Marathonisi while barracuda and big fish work the deep pinnacle
Sep.conditions: still-warm settled sea and clear caves as the crowds thin

Oct.marine_life: turtles still possible at Marathonisi while grouper, moray and octopus stay busy along the Keri walls
Oct.conditions: pleasant water with the last boats of the season running

Nov.marine_life: resident grouper, moray and octopus holding the caves as the island empties
Nov.conditions: bases closed or closing amid unsettled autumn weather

Dec.marine_life: octopus and moray tucked into the cave walls while the reefs rest
Dec.conditions: mild but shut-down early winter with no dive operations

dive_sites.name: Keri Caves
dive_sites.blurb: The signature dive of the island: reef walls, tunnels and caverns off Keri including a cathedral-like 75 m blue cavern; average depth about 18 m with the wall dropping past 40 m. Monk seals are occasionally found feeding or resting inside.

dive_sites.name: Marathonisi (Turtle Island)
dive_sites.blurb: The islet in Laganas Bay inside the National Marine Park of Zakynthos, averaging about 10 m with a 20 m maximum — the place divers most often meet loggerhead turtles.

dive_sites.name: Barracuda Reef
dive_sites.blurb: A pinnacle whose tip just breaks the surface; when current runs, schools of barracuda feed around the deep rock, and some of the island's biggest fish — including tuna — patrol below 45 m.

dive_sites.name: Shrimp Cave
dive_sites.blurb: A true overhead cave at 30 m near Keri whose walls crawl with shrimp; the depth plus the ceiling restrict it to experienced divers with overhead training.

dive_sites.name: Poseidon Cave
dive_sites.blurb: One of the dozen or so named sites worked from Keri harbour, part of the limestone cave-and-tunnel coast between Keri and Marathia — the island counts some 400 sea caves in all.

destinations.description: Zakynthos concentrates its diving on the sheltered south coast, where about a dozen sites work out of little Keri harbour and Laganas Bay sits inside the National Marine Park of Zakynthos, nesting ground of the loggerhead turtle. Marathonisi — the islet divers call Turtle Island — is where loggerheads are most often met, while the Keri coast hides walls, tunnels and caverns including a cathedral-like 75 m blue cavern. The island counts some 400 sea caves; dive bases run May to October and shut for winter.
destinations.underwater: Keri sets the standard: reef walls dropping past 40 m, tunnels and caverns averaging around 18 m, and the long blue cavern lit from its openings. Marathonisi is the easy counterpoint — roughly 10 m average over marine-park reef. Barracuda Reef's pinnacle tips the surface and falls past 45 m, and Shrimp Cave is a true overhead at 30 m for divers with cave training. The sheltered south coast stays near-still, and from May to September the water climbs from 19 to 27°C with the caves at their clearest.
destinations.encounters: Loggerhead turtles are the headline: Laganas Bay sits inside their protected marine park, and dives at Marathonisi meet them more reliably than anywhere else on the island through the summer season. A Mediterranean monk seal is an occasional surprise, sometimes found resting or feeding in the Keri caves. Around them, dusky grouper, moray and octopus hold the walls, barracuda school the pinnacle when current runs, and tuna patrol below 45 m.

destinations.name: Naples
destinations.current_note: Sheltered inner-gulf sites carry little to no current; the shallow Baia park is dived at anchor over flat bottoms.
destinations.currents: Little to no current inside the sheltered Gulf of Pozzuoli, where the Baia archaeological sites are dived at anchor over flat shallow bottoms; the Gaiola shoals off Posillipo are similarly gentle.
destinations.highlights: Rome underwater in Italy's third most visited city: Emperor Claudius's sunken nymphaeum, an intact black-and-white villa mosaic and the drowned harbor of Portus Julius in the Baia submerged park, plus the Gaiola reserve's villa-strewn shoals off Posillipo.

Jan.marine_life: octopus, moray and conger holding inside the drowned Roman walls while the sandy flats sit quiet
Jan.conditions: cold water in thick suits, with winter swell often stirring the shallow archaeological sites

Feb.marine_life: residents tucked deep into the ancient masonry with little else moving over the cold sand
Feb.conditions: coldest water of the year; centers dive weather permitting but swell can blow out the shallows

Mar.marine_life: octopus and nudibranchs beginning to stir among the sunken villa walls as the water bottoms out
Mar.conditions: still-cold late-winter sea with unsettled weather and dives run only when the gulf lies flat

Apr.marine_life: nudibranchs showing on the encrusted masonry while octopus and moray grow active in the ruins
Apr.conditions: cool water but calming spring seas — often clearer over the archaeological flats — as Gaiola tours resume

May.marine_life: saddled seabream shoaling over the mosaic floors while grouper appear on the Gaiola shoals
May.conditions: warming water with settled spring weather and full schedules in both parks

Jun.marine_life: dusky grouper stationed on the Cavallara banks while seabream swarm the sunken harbor and octopus den among the pilae
Jun.conditions: warm calm sea and easy long dives over the shallow ruins

Jul.marine_life: octopus, moray and conger busy in the ruins with saddled seabream thick over the archaeological flats
Jul.conditions: warm settled water in the busiest season for the guided park itineraries

Aug.marine_life: grouper on the outer shoals and seabream clouding the drowned streets while nudibranchs work the encrusted walls
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year with flat seas across the sheltered gulf

Sep.marine_life: dusky grouper, moray and conger showing everywhere as seabream keep grazing the sunken villa gardens
Sep.conditions: still-warm sea with late-summer calm bringing the year's cleanest water

Oct.marine_life: octopus and moray active in the masonry while grouper hold on the Gaiola banks into the autumn
Oct.conditions: pleasant water and autumn calm that often gives the clearest archaeological dives

Nov.marine_life: residents settling back into the ruins as the seabream shoals thin over the cooling flats
Nov.conditions: cooling sea with thicker suits and dives increasingly hostage to swell

Dec.marine_life: octopus, moray and conger tucked into the sunken walls while the shallows go quiet
Dec.conditions: cold early-winter water; local centers still run weather permitting but days are lost to wind

dive_sites.name: Ninfeo di Claudio (Punta Epitaffio, Baia)
dive_sites.blurb: Emperor Claudius's banquet nymphaeum, drowned by bradyseism and dived at about 5 m; replica statues now stand where the originals — displayed in the Baia castle museum — were found. Restored and reopened to divers in 2020.

dive_sites.name: Villa a Protiro (Baia)
dive_sites.blurb: A Roman maritime villa whose courtyard keeps a large black-and-white geometric mosaic floor in remarkable condition — local divers rate it the highlight of the submerged park.

dive_sites.name: Portus Julius (Baia)
dive_sites.blurb: The submerged Roman military harbor of 37 BC, with streets, warehouses and fish-tanks still traceable across the shallow seabed of the Gulf of Pozzuoli.

dive_sites.name: Secca delle Fumose (Baia)
dive_sites.blurb: Roman pilae — harbor pillars — on a shoal where volcanic sulfur gas streams continuously through the sand: diving inside the active Campi Flegrei caldera.

dive_sites.name: Villa dei Pisoni (Baia)
dive_sites.blurb: The drowned estate of the Pisoni family, its perimeter walls and the traces of a garden colonnade wrapped in sponges and sea grass.

dive_sites.name: Secca della Cavallara (Gaiola)
dive_sites.blurb: Rocky shoal off the Gaiola islets on the Posillipo coast, inside the Gaiola MPA: guided itineraries to about 25 m over the Cavallara banks, past remains of maritime villas, fishponds and tuff quarries drowned by bradyseism.

destinations.description: Italy's third most visited city dives its own drowned history. In the Baia submerged archaeological park — sunk by the slow volcanic breathing of the Campi Flegrei — Emperor Claudius's nymphaeum sits at about 5 m, a villa courtyard keeps its black-and-white mosaic floor, and the streets of Portus Julius, the Roman military harbor of 37 BC, run across the shallow seabed. Across the bay, the Gaiola underwater park off Posillipo adds villa-strewn shoals down to about 25 m, all guided-only on short day-boat runs from Baia and Pozzuoli.
destinations.underwater: The Baia sites are shallow, flat and dived at anchor: replica statues in Claudius's nymphaeum at 5 m, mosaic floors brushed clear of sand, Roman pilae rising from a shoal where sulfur gas bubbles stream up through the seabed of the live caldera. Visibility is modest — 5-10 m on the sandy flats, 10-20 m out on the Gaiola shoals — and best in the calm of late summer and autumn. Water runs 14°C in winter to 26°C in August; currents are barely a factor in the sheltered gulf.
destinations.encounters: This is a residents' destination: octopus, moray and conger eels have colonised the drowned walls and fish-tanks, nudibranchs graze the encrusted masonry, and saddled seabream shoal over the mosaic floors. Dusky grouper hold on the deeper Gaiola banks, thickening through the warm months. From June to September everything is active in warm water; in winter the residents tuck into the Roman stonework and the shallow flats go quiet.

destinations.name: Sorrento & Capri
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm Jun-Oct; 7mm semi-dry in shoulder months; drysuit for rare winter dives
destinations.current_note: Sheltered bays and the Vervece are usually gentle, but exposed points like Punta Campanella and Capri's Punta Carena funnel strong currents between the peninsula and the island.
destinations.currents: Mostly gentle in the sheltered bays and around the Vervece, but the exposed points — Punta Campanella itself and Punta Carena on Capri — funnel strong tidal and wind-driven currents through the gap between the peninsula and the island.
destinations.highlights: The Punta Campanella marine reserve between Sorrento and Capri: the Vervece islet's gorgonian walls falling to 50 m past a bronze Madonna at 12 m, the five fish-packed pinnacles of the Banco di Santa Croce, and current-swept points below Capri's lighthouse.

Jan.marine_life: grouper sitting deep on the pinnacles while moray and octopus hold their dens through the cold
Jan.conditions: most centers paused for winter; dives only by arrangement in cold, storm-prone seas

Feb.marine_life: resident moray and octopus tucked into the rock with little moving over the reefs
Feb.conditions: coldest water of the year and operations still dormant outside special arrangement

Mar.marine_life: grouper and octopus stirring on the pinnacles as the water sits at its coldest
Mar.conditions: late-winter seas with storms still passing and centers yet to reopen

Apr.marine_life: octopus and moray growing active on the reefs while grouper reclaim the pinnacle tops
Apr.conditions: season opening around Easter with cool water, thick suits and spring plankton dimming the blue

May.marine_life: dentex patrolling the pinnacles while grouper station on the Vervece walls and bream shoal in the reserve
May.conditions: warming water and full boat schedules from the three marinas

Jun.marine_life: barracuda schooling over the Banco di Santa Croce while grouper and dentex work the gorgonian walls
Jun.conditions: warm settled sea with the reserve's sites at their busiest

Jul.marine_life: grouper near-guaranteed on the Vervece while barracuda and dentex hunt across the pinnacles
Jul.conditions: warm calm water in high season; exposed Capri points still demand current planning

Aug.marine_life: barracuda thick over the pinnacle caps with grouper, moray and octopus showing on every wall
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year and long calm days on the reserve

Sep.marine_life: dentex and barracuda hunting the fish-crowded pinnacles while john dory hang along the deeper walls
Sep.conditions: still-warm sea, thinning crowds and the year's clearest water arriving

Oct.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus busy on the walls while late barracuda linger over the banks
Oct.conditions: comfortable water with autumn clarity at its best before the season closes

Nov.marine_life: residents holding on the pinnacles as the visiting hunters thin out for winter
Nov.conditions: cooling sea with the last boats of the season running into early November

Dec.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus settled deep into the rock while the reserve sits quiet
Dec.conditions: centers paused for winter with dives possible only by arrangement

dive_sites.name: Scoglio del Vervece
dive_sites.blurb: Rock islet 1 km off Marina della Lobra inside one of the MPA's two no-take zones; a bronze Madonna rests at 12 m and the walls drop to about 50 m through white, yellow and red gorgonian forests with near-guaranteed grouper and barracuda.

dive_sites.name: Banco di Santa Croce
dive_sites.blurb: Five rocky pinnacles rising from about 50 m to a cap at 9 m in the Gulf of Naples off Vico Equense — counted among the Mediterranean's richest dives for its density of gorgonians and fish life.

dive_sites.name: Punta Carena (Capri)
dive_sites.blurb: Below Capri's lighthouse on the island's southwest tip; rated the island's most spectacular dive, but strong currents and constant boat traffic keep it for experienced divers.

dive_sites.name: Grotta di Mitigliano
dive_sites.blurb: A cavern in the limestone of the Sorrento Peninsula near Punta Campanella — torch-lit swim-throughs with sponge- and coral-crusted walls.

dive_sites.name: Isca
dive_sites.blurb: Small islet off the Massa Lubrense coast toward Marina del Cantone with easy reef contours, octopus dens and schooling bream in the MPA's buffer zone.

dive_sites.name: Vetara
