dive_sites.blurb: Isolated islet between Punta Campanella and the Li Galli islands; deeper rocky slopes with parazoanthus cover and pelagic fly-bys on the seaward side.

destinations.description: The Sorrentine Peninsula points straight at Capri, and the Punta Campanella marine reserve fills the water between them. Day boats out of Marina Grande, Marina della Lobra and Marina del Cantone reach the Vervece islet — a no-take rock where a bronze Madonna rests at 12 m and gorgonian walls fall to about 50 m — the five pinnacles of the Banco di Santa Croce rising from 50 m to a 9 m cap, limestone caverns, and the current-swept wall under Capri's lighthouse. The season runs roughly Easter to early November with the resort trade.
destinations.underwater: This is classic Tyrrhenian rock: pinnacles and walls carpeted in white, yellow and red gorgonians, torch-lit swim-throughs in the peninsula's limestone, and open blue on the seaward islets. The sheltered sites dive gently, but Punta Campanella and Capri's Punta Carena funnel strong currents through the strait and stay on the experienced-diver list. Visibility runs 10-25 m — best in late summer and autumn, dimmest in the spring bloom — on water from 14°C in winter to 26°C in August.
destinations.encounters: Dusky grouper are the reserve's signature — near-guaranteed on the Vervece — with moray eels and octopus in every wall crack. The warm months bring the hunters: Mediterranean barracuda schooling over the Banco di Santa Croce's caps, dentex patrolling the pinnacles, john dory holding along the deeper walls into autumn. By late October the visitors thin out, and winter leaves the residents settled deep on the quiet rock.

destinations.name: Taormina
destinations.wetsuit: 3-5mm high summer; 5-7mm at the season's edges (May, October)
destinations.current_note: Most Isola Bella sites are minutes from shore and gently swept; exposed outer rocks like Mermaid Rock and the Capo Taormina slope pick up current when the Ionian wind swings.
destinations.currents: Gentle around the Isola Bella reserve's inshore sites, but the exposed outer rocks — Mermaid Rock and the column slope off Capo Taormina — can pick up current when the Ionian wind swings.
destinations.highlights: Sicily's flagship resort dives the Isola Bella marine reserve at its feet — a light-flooded blue cavern, an easy house reef, and the cargo of a 2nd-century Roman stone-carrier: 37 marble columns still in situ at 22-29 m off Capo Taormina.

Jan.marine_life: resident grouper, moray and octopus wintering on the reserve's reefs with no one diving above them
Jan.conditions: dive centers closed for the winter and the resort town largely shut

Feb.marine_life: octopus and moray holding their dens through the coldest water of the year
Feb.conditions: no local operations running; the season resumes in spring

Mar.marine_life: reef residents stirring on the boulder fields as the Ionian slowly warms
Mar.conditions: centers still shut ahead of the spring reopening

Apr.marine_life: octopus, moray and nudibranchs active over the Isola Bella boulders as the reefs wake up
Apr.conditions: season opening in cool water with thicker suits and quiet sites

May.marine_life: grouper and parrotfish showing on the reserve's reefs while nudibranchs work the cavern walls
May.conditions: fresh 18°C water with full schedules resuming from town and Giardini Naxos

Jun.marine_life: dusky grouper on the deeper contours while parrotfish graze the boulders and octopus den along the house reef
Jun.conditions: warm settled Ionian with long days over the reserve

Jul.marine_life: amberjack passing the outer rocks while grouper, moray and octopus show on every reserve dive
Jul.conditions: warm calm sea in the resort's busiest weeks

Aug.marine_life: amberjack sweeping Mermaid Rock's deeper contours while parrotfish and bream crowd the shallows
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year with flat seas and superb clarity

Sep.marine_life: grouper and amberjack busy on the outer rocks while octopus and nudibranchs fill the caverns
Sep.conditions: still-warm sea with the clearest water of the year arriving

Oct.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus staying active on the reefs as the last amberjack pass through
Oct.conditions: still 23°C with autumn clarity as the season winds toward its close

Nov.marine_life: residents settling onto the quiet reefs after the last boats of October
Nov.conditions: centers closed for winter at the end of the season

Dec.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus tucked into the rock with the reserve left to itself
Dec.conditions: no diving operations; the town itself largely shuts down

dive_sites.name: Greek/Roman Columns & Shrimp Cave
dive_sites.blurb: The cargo of a 2nd-century AD Roman stone-carrier: 37 marble columns and two blocks still in situ on the rocky slope at 22-29 m off Capo Taormina, combined with swim-throughs and a shrimp-filled cave.

dive_sites.name: Grotta Azzurra (Blue Cave)
dive_sites.blurb: Light-flooded cavern just north of Isola Bella, reached by a short boat ride; the dive follows a wall at about 12 m with the open Ionian blue on one side, to a maximum depth of about 25 m.

dive_sites.name: Isola Bella house reef
dive_sites.blurb: Rocky reef ringing the pearl-shaped islet of the Isola Bella marine reserve, minutes from shore — the standard first dive and training site, with octopus, moray eels and bream over boulder fields.

dive_sites.name: Mermaid Rock
dive_sites.blurb: Offshore rock dropping to a maximum of about 36 m, with canyon-cut topography, parazoanthus-covered walls and passing pelagics on the deeper contours.

dive_sites.name: Naxos Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: Shallow wreckage lying between the surface and 15 m off Giardini Naxos — an easy second dive and a favorite for training and long, unhurried bottom times.

destinations.description: Sicily's flagship resort town looks straight down on its own dive sites: the Isola Bella marine reserve sits at the foot of the cliffs, dived from the bay itself and on 5-15 minute boat runs out of Giardini Naxos. The headline is Roman — the cargo of a 2nd-century AD stone-carrier, 37 marble columns still lying in situ at 22-29 m off Capo Taormina — backed by a light-flooded blue cavern, an easy boulder-field house reef, a shallow training wreck and the deeper canyons of Mermaid Rock. Centers run April or May to the end of October.
destinations.underwater: Rocky Ionian seabeds in clear water — 10-30 m through the season, at its best in late summer and autumn. The house reef is boulder fields at 5-18 m, minutes from shore; the Blue Cave floods with light along a 12 m wall with open blue on one side; and on the Capo Taormina slope the Roman columns lie in rows at 22-29 m beside a shrimp-filled cave. Mermaid Rock drops to about 36 m for the deep end. Summer water peaks at 26°C in a 3-5 mm suit; May and October ask for 5-7 mm.
destinations.encounters: Dusky grouper, Mediterranean moray and octopus are the reserve's dependable cast, joined by Mediterranean parrotfish grazing the boulder shallows and nudibranchs on the cavern walls. High summer adds movement, with amberjack sweeping the deeper contours of Mermaid Rock and the outer points. From November to March the centers close and the reefs are left to their residents until the spring reopening.

destinations.name: Puerto Galera
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm year-round; 5mm or hooded vest for the 27°C months (Dec-Feb)
destinations.current_note: Verde Island Passage tidal currents power the marquee drift dives like The Canyons; sheltered bay sites stay mild, and dive plans follow the tide tables.
destinations.currents: Strong tidal flow at The Canyons and Verde Island Drop-Off, timed to the tables; the north-facing Sabang bays and wreck sites stay gentle.
destinations.highlights: The Philippines' flagship dive town on the Verde Island Passage: the current-swept Canyons and the Verde Island Drop-Off for the drift crowd, scuttled wrecks and nudibranch-carpeted walls minutes from Sabang for everyone else, divable year-round from sheltered north-facing bays.

Jan.marine_life: trevally and barracuda stacking in the passage current while nudibranchs and frogfish fill the sheltered walls
Jan.conditions: dry-season amihan winds with cooler water and full boat schedules

Feb.marine_life: sweetlips and snapper holding in the canyons while ghost pipefish and cuttlefish work the wreck sand
Feb.conditions: coolest water of the year, calm and clear on the amihan pattern

Mar.marine_life: jacks, sweetlips and barracuda thick in the canyons while turtles cruise the walls and macro life crowds every ledge
Mar.conditions: warming, settled seas at the start of the clearest months

Apr.marine_life: trevally schooling hard in the current while nudibranchs carpet the walls and batfish hang over the wrecks
Apr.conditions: warm flat seas with the year's best clarity out to Verde Island

May.marine_life: barracuda, sweetlips and trevally packed into the canyons with turtles and cuttlefish on the quieter reefs
May.conditions: warmest water of the year and calm seas before the monsoon turns

Jun.marine_life: canyon pelagics still stacking on the tides while frogfish and ghost pipefish hold on the sheltered sand
Jun.conditions: habagat onset with building southwest swell; north-facing bays stay divable

Jul.marine_life: frogfish, nudibranchs and scorpionfish on the sheltered wrecks while the exposed passage sites go quiet
Jul.conditions: southwest-monsoon rain and swell with boats holding to sheltered bays

Aug.marine_life: macro carrying the diving — ghost pipefish, nudibranchs and cuttlefish inside the protected bays
Aug.conditions: peak habagat with rain, reduced visibility and typhoon watch

Sep.marine_life: sweetlips and snapper waiting out the swell while the wreck sand keeps producing frogfish
Sep.conditions: late-monsoon rain and typhoon risk; sheltered sites only on rough days

Oct.marine_life: trevally and barracuda returning to the tide lines as the walls busy up with nudibranchs
Oct.conditions: transition month as habagat fades and boat schedules fill back out

Nov.marine_life: jacks and sweetlips rebuilding in the canyons while turtles graze the walls
Nov.conditions: amihan returning with drier weather and steadily clearing water

Dec.marine_life: barracuda and trevally on the current lines with frogfish and nudibranchs dense on the walls
Dec.conditions: cooling, clear dry-season water and busy holiday boats

dive_sites.name: The Canyons
dive_sites.blurb: Puerto Galera's signature drift: three current-swept canyons on the Verde Island Passage where trevally, sweetlips, snapper and barracuda stack up in the flow; strong currents and a blue-water ascent make it a genuinely advanced dive.

dive_sites.name: Verde Island Drop-Off
dive_sites.blurb: An hour's banca ride into the Verde Island Passage; a rock pinnacle and wall starting at 5m and plunging far beyond recreational depths, with the clearest water and densest reef-fish life in the area.

dive_sites.name: Sabang Wrecks
dive_sites.blurb: Three small scuttled vessels — a steel yacht and two wooden boats — on sand right off Sabang Beach; a macro haven of frogfish, ghost pipefish, scorpionfish and nudibranchs at friendly depths.

dive_sites.name: Alma Jane Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A 30m steel cargo vessel scuttled off Sabang in 2003 as an artificial reef; sits upright at 30m with resident batfish schools and easy hold access for trained wreck divers.

dive_sites.name: Sinandigan Wall
dive_sites.blurb: A nudibranch-carpeted wall east of Sabang dropping to 30m — leaf fish, frogfish, crocodilefish and shrimp-filled mushroom corals make it a photographer's staple.

dive_sites.name: Hole in the Wall
dive_sites.blurb: A narrow sponge-and-crinoid-lined swim-through at about 12m that funnels divers onto the coral shoulder next to The Canyons; often paired with it as a two-stage drift.

destinations.description: On the north coast of Mindoro, Puerto Galera fronts the Verde Island Passage — the 'center of the center' of marine shore-fish biodiversity — and its 60-plus dive shops work dozens of named sites from Sabang's sheltered bays. Tidal current makes the marquee dives: The Canyons' three swept bowls minutes from the beach and the Verde Island Drop-Off an hour out, while scuttled wrecks on the sand and nudibranch-carpeted walls keep macro divers busy at friendly depths year-round.
destinations.underwater: The Canyons sets the tone: drop in on the tide, funnel through the sponge-lined Hole in the Wall at about 12 m, then hang in the flow as trevally, sweetlips, snapper and barracuda stack overhead before a blue-water ascent. Off Sabang the pace flips — three small scuttled boats on 16-22 m sand hide frogfish, ghost pipefish and scorpionfish, and Sinandigan Wall drops to 30 m under a carpet of nudibranchs. Water holds 27-30°C; dry-season visibility runs 15-25 m, past 30 m at Verde Island.
destinations.encounters: Macro is the constant: the passage's walls and wreck sand produce nudibranchs, frogfish, ghost pipefish and cuttlefish year-round. The current sites add the action — giant trevally, chevron barracuda, sweetlips and batfish stacking in the flow at The Canyons, densest through the March-May clear season — while green and hawksbill turtles cruise the walls. The July-September monsoon pushes boats into the sheltered bays, where the small stuff never stops.

destinations.name: Coron (Busuanga)
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm year-round; a skin suffices in Barracuda Lake's 38°C layer
destinations.current_note: Most wrecks sit in sheltered bay water with little current; the Irako and outer sites can pull moderate tidal flow, so penetration dives are timed on slack.
destinations.currents: Mild inside Coron Bay across most of the fleet; moderate tidal flow possible at the Irako and the outer sites, timed on slack for penetration dives.
destinations.highlights: Southeast Asia's premier WWII wreck fleet: a Japanese support flotilla sunk on 24 September 1944 lies across Coron Bay at recreational depths, from the coral-crusted 160 m Okikawa Maru to the near-intact Irako, with the surreal 38°C thermocline of Barracuda Lake as the chaser.

Jan.marine_life: jacks and grouper patrolling the superstructures while turtles graze the bay reefs and sea kraits hunt the holds
Jan.conditions: calm amihan seas with the year's most reliable wreck visibility

Feb.marine_life: scorpionfish and lionfish thick on the wreck decks with batfish schooling above the masts
Feb.conditions: dry, settled weather and clear bay water in the prime season

Mar.marine_life: grouper and snapper inside the hulls while batfish school overhead and dugong day trips run to north Busuanga
Mar.conditions: warm, flat dry-season seas with dependable boat schedules

Apr.marine_life: wreck residents busy — giant puffers, sea kraits, lionfish — with turtles on the shallow bay reefs
Apr.conditions: hot, calm late-dry-season days as the water peaks toward 30°C

May.marine_life: batfish and great barracuda hanging over the wrecks in the last of the settled season
May.conditions: warmest water of the year with the dry pattern starting to break

Jun.marine_life: grouper and scorpionfish holding on the sheltered wrecks as the outer sites turn patchy
Jun.conditions: habagat onset with rain squalls; the sheltered bay keeps wrecks divable

Jul.marine_life: wreck life unchanged — snapper, giant puffers, sea kraits — under a silty rainy-season bay
Jul.conditions: southwest-monsoon rain and swell cutting visibility and cancelling outer boats

Aug.marine_life: residents only: grouper deep in the hulls and lionfish on the decks
Aug.conditions: peak habagat rain with typhoon watch and sheltered-bay diving only

Sep.marine_life: snapper and batfish inside the sheltered wrecks while outer trips wait on weather
Sep.conditions: late-monsoon rain and swell with boats picking weather windows

Oct.marine_life: great barracuda and jacks returning over the wrecks as the bay begins to clear
Oct.conditions: transition month with easing rain and improving schedules

Nov.marine_life: batfish schools rebuilding over the fleet with turtles back on the bay reefs
Nov.conditions: amihan returning with drying weather and steadily better visibility

Dec.marine_life: batfish and snapper schooling over the wrecks with dugongs possible on north-Busuanga day trips
Dec.conditions: calm, clear early-dry-season water at the start of the best months

dive_sites.name: Irako Maru
dive_sites.blurb: The most intact of Coron's WWII wrecks, a 147m refrigeration ship upright at 34-43m with engine-room and galley penetrations for trained wreck divers; jacks, grouper and scorpionfish patrol the superstructure.

dive_sites.name: Okikawa Maru
dive_sites.blurb: Coron's largest wreck, a roughly 160m oil tanker; the coral-crusted deck at 10-16m suits newer wreck divers while the bow break and hull reach 26m — a huge, forgiving introduction to the fleet.

dive_sites.name: Akitsushima
dive_sites.blurb: The only true warship of the group, a 114m seaplane tender lying on her side with the flying-boat crane still swung over the sand at 36m; big grouper and schooling snapper inside.

dive_sites.name: Kogyo Maru
dive_sites.blurb: A 129m freighter sunk carrying airfield-building materials — a bulldozer, cement mixer and cement sacks still sit in hold two; lies on her starboard side on a 34m seabed.

dive_sites.name: Morazan Maru
dive_sites.blurb: Long misidentified as the Olympia Maru, this English-built freighter of about 90m rests at 25m with easy cargo-hold swim-throughs, banded sea kraits and huge puffers — the classic second wreck dive.

dive_sites.name: Barracuda Lake
dive_sites.blurb: A cliff-ringed brackish sinkhole lake on Coron Island where a thermocline at about 14m jumps the water from 28°C to 38°C — a surreal hot-spring dive with a resident great barracuda, sacred to the Tagbanwa people.

destinations.description: Coron Town on Busuanga Island fronts a bay holding Southeast Asia's densest WWII wreck fleet: a Japanese support flotilla caught by US carrier aircraft on 24 September 1944 and sunk at recreational depths. The roughly 160 m Okikawa Maru's coral-crusted deck starts at 10 m, the refrigeration ship Irako sits near-intact down to 43 m, and between them lie freighters still carrying their cargo. Barracuda Lake adds a 38°C thermocline novelty, and north-Busuanga day trips carry roughly even odds of a dugong.
destinations.underwater: Wreck diving here is about scale and atmosphere: hulls of 90 to 160 m lying in sheltered, often silty bay water of 5-15 m visibility — 15-25 m on good dry-season days. Trained divers thread the Irako's engine room and the Kogyo Maru's holds, where a bulldozer and cement mixer still sit; newer divers get the Okikawa's shallow deck and the Morazan's easy swim-throughs. In Barracuda Lake a thermocline at about 14 m jumps the water from 28°C to a bathwater 38°C.
destinations.encounters: The wrecks are their own reef: batfish and snapper school over the superstructures, grouper and giant puffers hold the cargo spaces, lionfish and scorpionfish crust the decks, and banded sea kraits hunt through the holds. Hawksbill and green turtles graze the bay reefs, Barracuda Lake keeps its single resident great barracuda, and dry-season day trips to north Busuanga's seagrass meet a dugong on roughly half of attempts.

destinations.name: Panglao & Balicasag
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm year-round; 5mm comfortable for repetitive diving in the 27°C months (Jan-Feb)
destinations.current_note: Tidal flow runs along Balicasag's walls and at Doljo Point, so sanctuary dives are timed to neap and slack tides; most Alona-side sites stay gentle.
destinations.currents: Mild on the Alona-side reefs; moderate tidal flow along Balicasag's sanctuary walls and at Doljo Point, with dives timed to neap and slack tides.
destinations.highlights: Balicasag Island's sanctuary walls — capped at 150 divers a day — deliver turtles on nearly every dive and a resident bigeye jack school over the Black Forest's black corals, with deep gorgonian points, an arch tunnel and a shore-reached sardine bait ball back on Panglao itself.

Jan.marine_life: turtles on nearly every sanctuary dive while the bigeye jack school wheels over Black Forest
Jan.conditions: cool, calm amihan seas in the heart of the dry season

Feb.marine_life: jacks, barracuda and grazing turtles along the Balicasag walls in the year's clearest water
Feb.conditions: coolest water of the year with flat seas and long visibility

Mar.marine_life: turtles at safety-stop depth, snapper and barracuda on the drop-offs, macro thick on the house reefs
Mar.conditions: settled, warming dry-season water at its clearest

Apr.marine_life: the bigeye jack school dense over the black coral trees while turtles crowd the reef tops
Apr.conditions: warm calm seas with excellent clarity late in the dry season

May.marine_life: turtles and barracuda steady on the walls with frogfish and nudibranchs on Panglao's slopes
May.conditions: hot, mostly calm water as the dry pattern starts to break

Jun.marine_life: sanctuary walls still busy — turtles, snapper, jacks — as the first southwest swell arrives
Jun.conditions: habagat onset with building swell; north-coast sites take over on rough days

Jul.marine_life: turtles and reef fish holding on the sheltered north-coast walls while Balicasag crossings wait on weather
Jul.conditions: southwest-monsoon rain and swell with operators shifting to Doljo and Napaling

Aug.marine_life: the Napaling sardine bait ball and resident turtles carrying the diving through the rains
Aug.conditions: peak habagat rain and typhoon watch; sheltered shore sites keep diving going

Sep.marine_life: macro on the house reefs and turtles in the shallows between rain squalls
Sep.conditions: late-monsoon rain and occasional swell with weather-window crossings

Oct.marine_life: jacks regrouping at Black Forest and barracuda back on the walls as seas settle
Oct.conditions: transition month with easing rain and steadily clearing water

Nov.marine_life: turtles, snapper and the bigeye jack school reliable again across the sanctuary sites
Nov.conditions: amihan returning with drier weather and improving visibility

Dec.marine_life: turtles grazing at safety-stop depth while jacks and barracuda work the walls
Dec.conditions: calm, clear early-dry-season water and busy holiday boats

dive_sites.name: Black Forest (Balicasag)
dive_sites.blurb: Balicasag's famous slope where a 'forest' of black coral trees begins around 25m; the resident school of bigeye jacks and passing turtles are the headline act.

dive_sites.name: Diver's Heaven (Balicasag)
dive_sites.blurb: A steep sanctuary wall with fans and crevices dropping past 55m; green turtles graze the reef top at safety-stop depth on almost every dive.

dive_sites.name: Cathedral Wall (Balicasag)
dive_sites.blurb: Sheer sanctuary-side wall cut with overhangs that catch shafts of light; snapper, barracuda and turtles along the drop.

dive_sites.name: Arco Point (Panglao)
dive_sites.blurb: Panglao's 'hole in the wall' — a natural arch tunnel entered around 10m and exiting deeper on the wall, lined with sponges and banded sea snakes.

dive_sites.name: Doljo Point (Panglao)
dive_sites.blurb: Current-brushed point on Panglao's northwest tip with gorgonian fans, elephant-ear sponges and black corals stretching beyond 65m — a favorite for deep and tec divers.

dive_sites.name: Napaling (Panglao)
dive_sites.blurb: Vertical wall with dramatic overhangs on Panglao's north coast, home to a resident sardine bait ball in the shallows — Bohol's answer to Moalboal, reachable from shore.

destinations.description: Off Bohol's southwest tip, Panglao pairs a long-established dive strip at Alona Beach with Balicasag Island, a marine sanctuary 30-45 minutes offshore capped at 150 divers a day. Balicasag's walls — Black Forest's black-coral slope, Diver's Heaven, Cathedral — carry the reputation, while Panglao itself adds the Arco Point arch tunnel, the deep gorgonian point at Doljo and a resident sardine bait ball at Napaling, reachable from shore.
destinations.underwater: Balicasag is wall diving at its most reliable: sheer sanctuary faces dropping past 35 m, green turtles grazing the reef tops at safety-stop depth and the bigeye jack school wheeling over Black Forest, where the black coral trees begin around 25 m. On Panglao, Arco Point's arch tunnel is entered at about 10 m and exits deeper on the wall, and Doljo's fans and sponges stretch beyond recreational depths. Dry-season water runs 27-29°C with 15-30 m visibility.
destinations.encounters: Turtles are the signature — green and hawksbill graze Balicasag's reef tops on nearly every dive, all year. The resident bigeye jack school over Black Forest and chevron barracuda along the walls supply the schooling drama, frogfish and nudibranchs fill Panglao's house reefs, and the Napaling sardine bait ball swirls in the shallows. A passing whale shark is an occasional natural bonus rather than a promise.

destinations.name: Moalboal
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm year-round; some divers prefer 5mm in the 27°C late-winter months
destinations.current_note: Mostly gentle along the shore walls; Pescador Island can see moderate current on tide changes — an easy destination for drift-shy divers.
destinations.currents: Gentle on the Panagsama house-reef wall and sanctuary slopes; moderate tidal current possible around Pescador Island on the changes.
destinations.highlights: A resident school of millions of sardines lives metres off Panagsama Beach all year — a shore-entry wall dive — backed by Pescador Island's cavern-cut walls, reliable green turtles and pygmy seahorses in the sea fans.

Jan.marine_life: the sardine bait ball swirling off Panagsama with turtles grazing the reef shoulder
Jan.conditions: cool, calm amihan seas at the start of the clearest stretch

Feb.marine_life: millions of sardines in the year's clearest water while whitetip reef sharks rest on Pescador's ledges
Feb.conditions: coolest water of the year with flat seas and superb clarity

Mar.marine_life: the sardine school shape-shifting along the wall, turtles below and pygmy seahorses in Pescador's fans
Mar.conditions: settled dry-season weather with visibility at its long-range best

Apr.marine_life: sardines from the surface to 20 m with frogfish and nudibranchs dense on the walls
Apr.conditions: warm flat seas late in the dry season with easy Pescador crossings

May.marine_life: the bait ball unmoved, turtles at Turtle Point and macro on the sanctuary slopes
May.conditions: hot, mostly calm water as the dry pattern starts to break

Jun.marine_life: sardines and turtles as reliable as ever while the first swell tests the crossings to Pescador
Jun.conditions: habagat onset with warm water and building southwest chop

Jul.marine_life: the resident sardine school riding out the rains metres off the beach
Jul.conditions: southwest-monsoon rain and chop; the shore wall usually stays divable

Aug.marine_life: sardines, turtles and wall macro between squalls on the west-facing shore
Aug.conditions: peak habagat rain with occasional typhoon disruption

Sep.marine_life: the bait ball constant off Panagsama while boats pick weather windows for Pescador
Sep.conditions: late-monsoon rain and swell with shore diving carrying the schedule

Oct.marine_life: sardines thick on the wall and whitetip reef sharks back in view at Pescador as visibility rebuilds
Oct.conditions: transition month with easing rain and steadily clearing water

Nov.marine_life: the sardine school, grazing turtles and Pescador's fan corals back in clearing water
Nov.conditions: amihan returning with drier weather and calmer crossings

Dec.marine_life: sardines swirling in cooling clear water with turtles on the seagrass
Dec.conditions: early-dry-season calm with busy holiday boats and good clarity

dive_sites.name: Sardine Point (Talisay Wall)
dive_sites.blurb: A shape-shifting school of millions of sardines lives metres off Panagsama Beach year-round, swirling from the surface to about 20m along the wall — dived straight from shore.

dive_sites.name: Pescador Island
dive_sites.blurb: Moalboal's signature offshore pinnacle, ringed by a narrow shelf that drops in vertical walls past 35m; the sponge-lit 'Cathedral' cavern and dense fan corals are the highlights.

dive_sites.name: Airplane Wreck (Tuble)
dive_sites.blurb: A two-seater light aircraft placed on the sandy slope as an artificial reef in the 1990s, sitting at about 22m beside a drop-off; frogfish and scorpionfish shelter in the frame.

dive_sites.name: Tuble Marine Sanctuary
dive_sites.blurb: A no-take sanctuary on the Tuble reef flat with recovering hard corals, dense anthias clouds and reliable turtles — a favorite easy second dive.

dive_sites.name: Dolphin House (Ronda)
dive_sites.blurb: A gentle coral slope that steepens into a soft-coral and sea-fan wall around 20m; named for the resort onshore — the corals are the real draw.

dive_sites.name: Turtle Point / White Beach
dive_sites.blurb: Green turtles graze the seagrass and reef shoulder just north of Panagsama near White Beach — close to shore and dived on almost every itinerary.

destinations.description: On Cebu's southwest coast, Moalboal runs on a single extraordinary fact: a school of millions of sardines lives metres off Panagsama Beach all twelve months, dived straight from shore along the house-reef wall. Pescador Island, a 10-25 minute banca ride out, adds vertical walls, the sponge-lit Cathedral cavern and pygmy seahorses in the fans, while sanctuary slopes, a small airplane wreck and reliable turtles fill out an easy, shore-based week of diving.
destinations.underwater: The wall starts where the sand ends: step off the beach at Panagsama and the sardines open around you, a shape-shifting mass from the surface to about 20 m against the drop-off. Pescador's narrow shelf falls away in vertical faces past 35 m, fan corals and the Cathedral's light shafts below, whitetip reef sharks resting on the ledges. Currents stay mostly mild, water holds 27-30°C, and dry-season visibility can reach 30 m.
destinations.encounters: The sardine bait ball is the anchor — present every month, at its best in the clear December-May water — with green turtles grazing the seagrass at Turtle Point and along the Panagsama shoulder. The walls hold frogfish and nudibranchs, Pescador's sea fans hide pygmy seahorses, and whitetip reef sharks rest on the island's ledges. Thresher sharks and whale sharks turn up in the sardines only occasionally — a lucky-day event, not a schedule.

destinations.name: Ibiza
destinations.wetsuit: 3-5mm Jul-Sep; 5-7mm May-Jun and Oct; 7mm semi-dry plus hood in winter
destinations.current_note: Generally light around the coast, but the exposed offshore pinnacles — La Bota especially — can carry noticeably stronger flow across their flanks.
destinations.currents: Light around most coastal reefs and coves; noticeably stronger at the offshore pinnacle of La Bota and around Es Vedrà, where the walls sit open to moving water.
destinations.highlights: A seasonal Mediterranean island with a heavyweight centrepiece: the 142 m Don Pedro — widely cited as the largest wreck in the Mediterranean accessible to recreational divers — plus the Es Vedrà nature reserve, the La Bota seamount and Posidonia-fed clarity of 20-30 m.

Jan.marine_life: resident grouper and morays holding deep on the rock while the open water stays empty
Jan.conditions: cold winter sea with most dive centres closed for the season

Feb.marine_life: octopus and scorpionfish tucked into the reef while grouper sit low and little else moves
Feb.conditions: coldest water of the year on an island largely shut down

Mar.marine_life: morays and octopus stirring in the rock as the water slowly begins to warm
Mar.conditions: still-cold sea before the season with very few boats running

Apr.marine_life: grouper and octopus turning active across the reserve walls while the first barracuda appear
Apr.conditions: cool water and early boats as the first centres reopen

May.marine_life: barracuda gathering over the drop-offs while grouper station themselves on the Es Vedrà walls
May.conditions: season opening properly with cool water and settling seas

Jun.marine_life: barracuda schooling over the walls while dentex hunt at La Bota and eagle rays cross the seagrass
Jun.conditions: warming sea, long calm days and full boat schedules

Jul.marine_life: eagle rays over the Posidonia terraces while tuna and dentex patrol La Bota and barracuda wall up
Jul.conditions: warm settled water with strong clarity in the busiest month

Aug.marine_life: tuna, dentex and horse mackerel sweeping the seamount while grouper and morays show on every wall
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year with flat seas and long sightlines

Sep.marine_life: barracuda and dentex hunting over abundant reef fish while eagle rays linger on the terraces
Sep.conditions: still-warm sea, thinner crowds and often the year's best visibility

Oct.marine_life: grouper, morays and octopus busy on the walls while the last pelagics pass the pinnacles
Oct.conditions: pleasant water and calm autumn seas as the season winds down

Nov.marine_life: resident grouper and octopus holding on while the visiting fish thin out
Nov.conditions: cooling sea with most centres closing for the winter

Dec.marine_life: morays and scorpionfish tucked into the rock while the reefs sit quiet
Dec.conditions: cold early-winter sea with the island's dive scene shut down

dive_sites.name: Don Pedro Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A 142 m ferry that struck the Dado Pequeño islet leaving Ibiza harbour and sank in July 2007; she now lies on her port side on sand at about 46 m — widely cited as the largest wreck in the Mediterranean accessible to recreational and technical divers.

dive_sites.name: Dado Pequeño
dive_sites.blurb: A beacon-topped rock five minutes by boat from Ibiza harbour — the very islet the Don Pedro hit. Shallow rock gardens for novices drop away to deeper terraces with barracuda, grouper, octopus and scorpionfish.

dive_sites.name: Es Vedrà
dive_sites.blurb: The 380 m limestone islet off Cala d'Hort, a nature reserve above and below the water. Dramatic walls, caves and Posidonia seagrass terraces hold eagle rays, dusky grouper and scorpionfish, with shallow ground for beginners and deep walls for advanced divers.

dive_sites.name: La Bota
dive_sites.blurb: A pinnacle about a mile northwest of Es Vedrà rising from beyond 50 m to within centimetres of the surface. Ibiza's big-fish dive: tuna, barracuda, dentex, grouper and horse mackerel patrol its current-swept flanks.

dive_sites.name: Ses Margalides
dive_sites.blurb: Twin islets off the cliffy northwest coast near Santa Agnès, dived from San Antonio. A shelf at about 9 m beside the rocks drops to 18 m, with a photogenic swim-through arch and rich fish life on the faces.

destinations.description: Ibiza runs a classic seasonal Mediterranean operation, with day boats out of Ibiza Town, San Antonio and Santa Eulalia from May to October. The headline is the Don Pedro, a 142 m ferry that struck the Dado Pequeño islet leaving harbour in 2007 and now lies on her port side at 46 m — widely cited as the largest wreck in the Mediterranean open to recreational divers. Around it sit the Es Vedrà nature reserve, the La Bota seamount and Posidonia seagrass meadows that keep the water among the clearest in the western Mediterranean.
destinations.underwater: Posidonia meadows keep the water clear — 20-30 m is common in season — so the drama registers in full: Es Vedrà's walls and caves dropping past seagrass terraces, the arch at Ses Margalides throwing light across its swim-through, and La Bota rising from beyond 50 m to graze the surface with big fish on its current-swept flanks. The Don Pedro is the deep set piece, a 142 m hull on sand at 46 m for advanced and technical divers. Summer water reaches 26°C; winter drops to 14°C.
destinations.encounters: Dusky grouper hold the Es Vedrà reserve walls alongside morays and octopus, with scorpionfish on every rock face. Summer brings the traffic: barracuda school over the drop-offs, eagle rays cross the Posidonia terraces, and tuna, dentex and horse mackerel patrol La Bota's flanks. Through the 14-15°C winter the residents sit deep and most centres close until May.

destinations.name: Gran Canaria
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm most of the year; 7mm or 5mm + hooded vest Jan-Apr; 3-5mm Sep-Oct
destinations.current_note: Weak at the sheltered bays and the Mogán wrecks, but Atlantic swell and occasional currents run at exposed headlands, the El Cabrón entry points and the offshore wrecks.
destinations.currents: Generally weak inside the sheltered bays and on the Mogán wrecks; Atlantic swell and occasional currents at exposed headlands, the El Cabrón shore entries and offshore sites like the Arona.
destinations.highlights: Year-round Atlantic diving built around the El Cabrón marine reserve — the Canaries' most celebrated shore diving, with 400-plus recorded species — plus the 110 m Arona wreck, the grunt-swarmed Cermona II, seahorse bays and angelsharks on the sand from November to April.

Jan.marine_life: angelsharks settled on the sand at El Cabrón and Tufia while morays and trumpetfish work the volcanic walls
Jan.conditions: cool Atlantic water and thicker suits in the heart of angelshark season

Feb.marine_life: angelsharks met regularly on the sand flats while grunt schools hold the wrecks
Feb.conditions: coolest stretch of the year with 7mm suits and reliable diving

Mar.marine_life: late-season angelsharks still on the sand while seahorses and nudibranchs fill the sheltered bays
Mar.conditions: cool spring water with occasional swell on exposed shore entries

Apr.marine_life: the last angelsharks of the season while butterfly rays cruise the sand and morays crowd the reef
Apr.conditions: cool 19°C water with changeable spring conditions

May.marine_life: seahorses at Sardina and rays over the sand while the reef fish thicken
May.conditions: coolest water of the year slowly warming, with settling seas

Jun.marine_life: grunt schools swarming the Cermona II while trumpetfish and morays fill the reserve walls
Jun.conditions: warming water, though trade winds can chop east-coast entries

Jul.marine_life: roncador clouds on the wrecks, rays over the sand and eel gardens waving at depth
Jul.conditions: warm water with summer trade winds limiting some shore days

Aug.marine_life: reef life at full density with butterfly rays, trumpetfish and morays across the volcanic rock
Aug.conditions: warm sea, though trade winds still chop exposed shore entries

Sep.marine_life: grunt schools, rays and seahorses everywhere as the reef hits full density
Sep.conditions: warmest water of the year arriving with the calmest seas

Oct.marine_life: rays, seahorses and thick reef fish across the reserve in prime conditions
Oct.conditions: 23-24°C water, long visibility and settled autumn seas

Nov.marine_life: the first angelsharks of the season settling on the sand while warm water keeps the reef at full density
Nov.conditions: warm sea, calm days and the start of angelshark season

Dec.marine_life: angelsharks on the sand at the reserve while morays, trumpetfish and grunts hold the walls and wrecks
Dec.conditions: mild winter water with reliable diving through the holidays

dive_sites.name: El Cabrón Marine Reserve
dive_sites.blurb: The Canaries' most celebrated shore diving, a protected reserve since 2001 on the east coast at Arinaga. Volcanic walls, arches and caves hold more than 400 recorded species, from eel gardens at 18 m to a gorgonian field near 30 m, and the sand flats are a focal area for angelshark encounters in winter.

dive_sites.name: Arona Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A 110 m merchant ship that burned and sank in 1972, six nautical miles from Las Palmas' Puerto de la Luz. She lies on her side with the deck at 25 m and the seabed at 35 m, and often enjoys exceptional visibility away from the port.

dive_sites.name: Cermona II
dive_sites.blurb: A 32 m fishing boat scuttled in 2002 close to Puerto de Mogán marina, resting on her starboard side at 18-20 m. Thousands of bastard grunts swarm the hull alongside barracuda, morays, trumpetfish and rays; weak currents make it an easy wreck.

dive_sites.name: Sardina del Norte
dive_sites.blurb: A fishing-village bay on the quieter northwest coast with a maximum depth of about 17 m. Famed for seahorses on the sand plus stingrays, moray eels and anemones — a favourite macro and night-dive spot.

dive_sites.name: Tufia
dive_sites.blurb: An east-coast marine protected area below a tiny clifftop hamlet. Gorgonian patches shelter seahorses and nudibranchs, while the sand flats turn up butterfly rays and, in season, angelsharks.

destinations.description: The second-busiest Canary island backs its resorts with a genuine year-round dive industry, and its centrepiece is shore-based: the El Cabrón marine reserve at Arinaga, protected since 2001, where volcanic walls, arches and caves hold more than 400 recorded species. Offshore lie the 110 m Arona, sunk in 1972 off Las Palmas, and the grunt-swarmed Cermona II at Mogán, while sheltered bays like Sardina del Norte and Tufia turn up seahorses, butterfly rays and — from November to April — angelsharks on the sand.
destinations.underwater: This is volcanic Atlantic diving: black walls, arches and caves at El Cabrón dropping past eel gardens at 18 m to a gorgonian field near 30 m, sand flats where angelsharks lie buried in winter, and wrecks at both ends of the scale — the easy Cermona II at 18-20 m under clouds of roncadores, and the 110 m Arona on her side at 25-35 m in often exceptional visibility. Water runs 19°C in spring to 23-24°C in autumn, with 15-30 m visibility year-round.
destinations.encounters: The angelshark is the headline: from roughly November to April, Squatina squatina settles on the sand at El Cabrón and Tufia, and meeting one is a realistic goal rather than a lottery. Year-round, seahorses hold the bays at Sardina and Tufia, butterfly rays cruise the flats, black, tiger and fangtooth morays crowd the rock, trumpetfish hang in the walls and bastard grunts swarm the wrecks in their thousands.

destinations.name: Lanzarote
destinations.wetsuit: 5mm in summer and autumn; 7mm or 5mm + hooded vest Dec-Apr
destinations.current_note: Little current inside the sheltered south-coast bays of Puerto del Carmen and Playa Blanca; tidal movement is noticeable on exposed sites and the boat wrecks.
destinations.currents: Mostly slack inside the sheltered southern bays where the main sites sit; tidal movement shows on exposed sites and around the deeper harbour wrecks.
destinations.highlights: Europe's first underwater sculpture museum plus the Cathedral cavern, the Blue Hole tunnel and Puerto del Carmen's harbour wrecks, all in calm clear Atlantic water — with angelsharks met almost daily through the winter season.

Jan.marine_life: angelsharks met almost daily on the sand at Playa Chica while grouper hold the Cathedral
Jan.conditions: cool 19°C water with winter clarity often beyond 30 m

May.conditions: cool 19°C water in the quiet gap between seasons

Jun.marine_life: stingrays and barracuda over the slopes while grouper and cardinalfish pack the Cathedral
Jun.conditions: warming water with early-summer plankton trimming visibility

Jul.marine_life: barracuda over the reef edge while rays work the sand and reef fish swarm the harbour wrecks
Jul.conditions: warm settled water in the busy summer season

Aug.marine_life: rays and barracuda on the slopes while octopus and cuttlefish fill the shallows
Aug.conditions: warm sea and calm south-coast bays at peak holiday traffic

Sep.marine_life: reef life at full density while stingrays cross the sand and grouper hold the cavern
Sep.conditions: warmest water of the year with calm seas — the sweet spot

Oct.conditions: warm calm water as visibility begins its winter climb

dive_sites.name: Museo Atlántico
dive_sites.blurb: Europe's first underwater sculpture museum, opened in 2016 in a sheltered bay off Las Coloradas near Playa Blanca: more than 300 life-size pH-neutral casts arranged in ten installations across a 50 x 50 m sandy seabed at 12-15 m. Boat access only, and now colonising as an artificial reef.

dive_sites.name: Playa Chica
dive_sites.blurb: Puerto del Carmen's famous training-and-everything bay, giving direct shore access to the Cathedral, Blue Hole and Red Coral reefs. Depths run from 5 m sand to walls beyond 40 m, with seahorses, octopus and passing rays.

dive_sites.name: The Cathedral (La Catedral)
dive_sites.blurb: A huge vaulted cave set into the reef wall off Playa Chica at around 30 m, roomy enough to enter like a church nave. Long-resident dusky grouper and shoals of cardinalfish shelter inside.

dive_sites.name: Blue Hole (Agujero Azul)
dive_sites.blurb: A natural chimney-tunnel connecting the sandy plateau to the open reef wall, named for the glowing blue light at its exit. Rays, barracuda and angelsharks are regulars on the surrounding slope.

dive_sites.name: Old Harbour Wrecks
dive_sites.blurb: Up to six vessels — a metal transport ship and five wooden fishing boats — purposely sunk off Puerto del Carmen's old harbour wall in the 1970s-80s. The deepest, La Hélice, keeps her propeller and rudder at about 40 m; the shallower hulls swarm with reef fish, rays and angelsharks.

destinations.description: Lanzarote pairs one of Europe's most accessible dive coastlines with a genuine oddity: the Museo Atlántico off Playa Blanca, Europe's first underwater sculpture museum, where more than 300 life-size casts stand in ten installations on sand at 12-15 m. Puerto del Carmen's Playa Chica bay opens straight from shore onto the Cathedral cavern, the Blue Hole tunnel and the old-harbour wrecks — and from roughly October to April, divers here meet angelsharks almost daily on the volcanic sand.
destinations.underwater: Most of the diving starts from the sand at Playa Chica and works outwards: down the reef to the Cathedral, a vaulted cave at around 30 m holding long-resident grouper and cardinalfish, through the Blue Hole's chimney to the glowing exit on the outer wall, or across to the harbour wrecks, where La Hélice keeps her propeller at 40 m. Off Playa Blanca the sculpture museum's ten installations sit in orderly rows at 12-15 m. Winter visibility often passes 30 m in 18-20°C water.
destinations.encounters: The angelshark defines the calendar: from roughly October or November until April, Squatina squatina lies buried on the sand at Playa Chica, the Blue Hole slope and the harbour wrecks, and divers meet them almost daily at the season's height. The rest of the cast is steady year-round — common stingrays over the sand, barracuda off the reef edge, seahorses, cuttlefish and octopus in the shallows, and dusky grouper stationed in the Cathedral.

destinations.name: Antalya
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm Jul-Sep; 5mm May-Jun and Oct-Nov; 7mm semi-dry winter
destinations.current_note: Currents are generally low — the Paris II is dived down a fixed shotline in near-still water — with only occasional light current on the exposed corners of the Üç Adalar reefs.
destinations.currents: Mild almost everywhere; the wrecks sit in near-still water and only the exposed reef corners at Üç Adalar occasionally carry light current.
destinations.highlights: Turkey's resort capital hides a genuine dive scene at Kemer and Tekirova: the 1917 Paris II steamer wreck, a scuttled C-47 Dakota, and the Üç Adalar reef complex — loggerhead habitat with big grouper — all in calm, clear Mediterranean water.

Jan.marine_life: grouper holding the reef walls and octopus deep in the crevices while little else moves
Jan.conditions: cool winter sea with 7mm semi-drys and only calm-day diving at the year-round centers

Feb.marine_life: resident morays and octopus tucked into the rock while grouper sit quiet on the walls
Feb.conditions: coldest water of the year with winter storms cutting visibility

Mar.marine_life: grouper and octopus stirring on the reefs as the water slowly begins to warm
Mar.conditions: still-cold sea recovering from winter storms with limited boat schedules

Apr.marine_life: grouper active across the reefs with moray and octopus showing before the season proper
Apr.conditions: cool water and 5mm suits as the weather improves ahead of the May opening

May.marine_life: barracuda and amberjack returning to the reefs while grouper station on the walls
May.conditions: Kemer boats opening the main season with comfortable water and settling seas

Jun.marine_life: loggerhead turtles moving through Üç Adalar while grouper thicken and barracuda school
Jun.conditions: warm water, daily wreck and reef tours and lengthening visibility

Jul.marine_life: turtles on the Üç Adalar reefs with amberjack sweeping through and octopus on every dive
Jul.conditions: hot calm days with 28°C water in the busiest weeks

Aug.marine_life: loggerheads and big grouper on the reefs while barracuda hunt over the wrecks
Aug.conditions: warmest sea of the year, flat calm and clear

Sep.marine_life: turtles still on the reefs with amberjack and barracuda active over abundant reef fish
Sep.conditions: still-warm water with settled weather and the year's best clarity

Oct.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus busy while passing barracuda linger into the autumn
Oct.conditions: pleasant sea with daily tours running to the end of the month

Nov.marine_life: resident grouper and octopus holding on as the visiting fish thin out
Nov.conditions: cooling water with schedules shortening as the main season closes

Dec.marine_life: grouper and moray tucked into the reef structure while the sites sit quiet
Dec.conditions: cool early-winter sea with year-round centers diving on calm days

dive_sites.name: Paris II Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A French armed steamer, built 1913 and sunk by Turkish shore artillery in 1917, rediscovered in 1995 about 1.5 km off Kemer marina; it sits on a flat seabed with a buoyed shotline, near-still water and usually very good visibility, kept for experienced divers by its depth.

dive_sites.name: Üç Adalar (Three Islands)
dive_sites.blurb: A marine area off Tekirova, about 45 minutes by boat from Kemer, with 17-plus dive spots across nine reefs and two underwater caves from 3 m to beyond 40 m — recognised habitat for protected loggerhead turtles and large dusky grouper.

dive_sites.name: C-47 Dakota (Kemer)
dive_sites.blurb: A decommissioned military C-47 Dakota transport — 20 m long with a 29 m wingspan — scuttled as an artificial reef at 22 m; it sits upright on sand and even newer divers can be guided into the open cargo bay.

dive_sites.name: Aquarium Bay (Kemer)
dive_sites.blurb: A sheltered cove about 20 minutes from Kemer's yacht harbour with a 12 m maximum depth and little wind or swell — the region's standard training and first-dive site.

dive_sites.name: Sıçan Adası (Rat Island)
dive_sites.blurb: A small island off central Antalya's Konyaaltı coast, reached in minutes by dive boat from the city, with abundant reef life and routes that suit both new and experienced divers.

dive_sites.name: Side Coast Guard Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A 30 m former coastguard boat sunk as an artificial reef in 2008, 3 km off Side on the eastern Antalya coast; just 10-15 minutes by boat, resting at a 25 m maximum depth.

destinations.description: Antalya is Turkey's resort capital, and its diving concentrates 40 km southwest at Kemer and Tekirova, with Sıçan Island only minutes from the city itself. History does the headlining: the Paris II, a French armed steamer sunk by shore artillery in 1917, rests at 31 m off Kemer, and a scuttled C-47 Dakota sits upright on sand nearby. Üç Adalar, 45 minutes from Kemer marina, spreads 17-plus spots over nine reefs and two caves — protected loggerhead habitat patrolled by large dusky grouper.
destinations.underwater: The water is calm and clear — 20-30 m visibility is typical, best from late summer into autumn — and current rarely registers: the Paris II is dived down a fixed shotline in near-still water. Depths run from the 12 m training cove of Aquarium Bay to reef walls beyond 40 m at Üç Adalar. Summer water reaches 29°C and a 3 mm suit; winter drops to 16-19°C and 7 mm semi-drys for the centers that keep diving on calm days.
destinations.encounters: Loggerhead turtles are the flagship — Üç Adalar is recognised habitat for them, along with the large dusky grouper that hold its reef walls. Moray eels and common octopus fill the crevices and colonise the wrecks, while summer brings the movement: amberjack sweeping the reefs and Mediterranean barracuda schooling above the islands. In winter the residents remain, but the visitors thin out with the boat schedules.

destinations.name: Bodrum
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm Jun-Sep; 5mm May and Oct-Nov; 7mm semi-dry winter
destinations.current_note: Currents are mild throughout the year; only the exposed reef corners — Big Reef and the Orak Island wall — occasionally carry light current.
destinations.currents: Mild year-round, with occasional light flow on the exposed corners of Big Reef and the Orak Island wall.
destinations.highlights: The most developed dive hub on the Turkish Aegean: sheer reef walls in the Kara Ada channel, three purpose-sunk wrecks including a C-47 Dakota falling away to 33 m, and the deep clear walls of Orak Island two hours east.

Jan.marine_life: grouper holding deep on the walls with conger and moray tucked into the cracks
Jan.conditions: 16-17°C Aegean water with 7mm semi-drys and boats on demand only

Feb.marine_life: resident octopus and eels in the reef crevices while little moves in open water
Feb.conditions: coldest water of the year with winter winds keeping most boats in the harbour

Mar.marine_life: grouper and bream stirring on the reefs as the water begins its slow warm-up
Mar.conditions: still-cold sea and unsettled weather ahead of the April opening

Apr.marine_life: grouper active on the walls with octopus and moray showing across the reefs
Apr.conditions: boat season opening with cool water and thick suits

May.marine_life: barracuda returning to the reef corners while grouper station on Big Reef's wall
May.conditions: comfortable water and fuller schedules as the season builds

Jun.marine_life: grouper thick on the walls with barracuda schooling and bream shoaling over the reefs
Jun.conditions: warm settled Aegean with daily two-tank boats

Jul.marine_life: barracuda hunting along the walls while grouper, moray and conger show on every dive
Jul.conditions: warm calm sea in the busiest weeks of the season

Aug.marine_life: big grouper and passing barracuda over reefs busy with bream and octopus
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year, around 26°C, with long clear days

Sep.marine_life: grouper and bream abundant while barracuda work the reef edges
Sep.conditions: still-warm sea with the year's best visibility on the Orak walls

Oct.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus staying busy while the barracuda begin to disperse
Oct.conditions: pleasant water with boats running to the end of the month

Nov.marine_life: resident eels and octopus holding the reefs as the open-water fish thin out
Nov.conditions: cooling sea with shorter schedules as the boat season closes

Dec.marine_life: grouper, conger and moray tucked deep into the walls while the reefs sit quiet
Dec.conditions: cool early-winter water with diving by arrangement at the year-round centers

dive_sites.name: Big Reef (Büyük Resif)
dive_sites.blurb: A submerged reef in the channel between Bodrum and Kara Ada whose wall drops sheer from 7 m to 34 m, packed with Mediterranean reef life; its twin, Small Reef (5-26 m), lies just 200 m closer to town.

dive_sites.name: Kargı Adası (Kargı Island)
dive_sites.blurb: A wall dive starting from the island's south-eastern cove off the Bodrum peninsula, its reef structures sheltering moray and conger eels, grouper and passing barracuda.

dive_sites.name: C-47 Dakota Plane Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: An ex-air-force Douglas C-47 Dakota — 20 m fuselage, 30 m wingspan — scuttled as an artificial reef; the airframe starts at 19 m and falls away to 33 m, making it Bodrum's signature deep dive.

dive_sites.name: Pınar 1 Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: A 37 m former navy water tanker resting with the stern at 20 m and the seabed around 30 m; a compact, photogenic wreck now well colonised by marine growth.

dive_sites.name: SG-115 Coast Guard Wreck
dive_sites.blurb: An old coastguard vessel sunk in 2007 as an artificial reef off Kara Ada, stern at 17 m and bow at 26 m — an accessible first wreck.

dive_sites.name: Orak Adası (Orak Island)
dive_sites.blurb: An island about two hours east of Bodrum harbour famed for its deep, clear walls — generally the best visibility in the area and a favourite full-day two-tank boat trip.

destinations.description: Bodrum is the most developed dive hub on the Turkish Aegean, its castle-fronted harbour sending day boats to reefs, walls and three purpose-sunk wrecks. Big Reef drops sheer from 7 m to 34 m in the Kara Ada channel, with its twin Small Reef 200 m closer to town; an ex-air-force C-47 Dakota lies between 19 m and 33 m, the Pınar 1 water tanker and the SG-115 coastguard boat give gentler steel, and Orak Island, two hours east, holds the deepest, clearest walls in the area.
destinations.underwater: Expect Aegean rather than tropical: 23-26°C at the height of summer, visibility of 20-30 m in season within a 10-25 m year-round range, and mild currents that only brush the exposed corners of Big Reef and Orak. The wrecks stack neatly by experience — the SG-115's stern at 17 m makes an accessible first wreck, the Pınar 1 sits at 20-30 m, and the Dakota falls away to 33 m as the signature deep dive.
destinations.encounters: Dusky grouper anchor the reef walls, with moray and conger eels sharing the cracks and common octopus working the rubble and wreck plating. Mediterranean barracuda pass along the walls in season, and sea bream — sargo and dentex — shoal over the reefs. Winter never quite empties the water: the Aegean here rarely drops much below 15-16°C and the residents stay put, though boats run on demand rather than schedule.

destinations.name: Fethiye
destinations.wetsuit: 3mm Jun-Sep; 5mm May and Oct-Nov; 7mm semi-dry winter
destinations.current_note: The Gulf of Fethiye is well sheltered; currents are light on almost all sites, with only occasional surge on the exposed Afkule wall.
destinations.currents: Light throughout the sheltered gulf; only the exposed Afkule wall picks up occasional surge.
destinations.highlights: A sheltered Turquoise-Coast gulf where the Afkule wall and cavern headline swim-through tunnels, a cave whose chamber walls carpet with thousands of cleaner shrimp, and shallow dives over sunken Byzantine bath ruins.

Jan.marine_life: grouper quiet on the Afkule wall while cleaner shrimp still carpet the cave chambers
Jan.conditions: cool winter gulf with diving by arrangement only

Feb.marine_life: resident morays and octopus in the crevices while the shrimp hold their cave walls
Feb.conditions: coldest water of the year with storms cutting visibility

Mar.marine_life: grouper and octopus stirring on the reefs as the gulf slowly warms
Mar.conditions: still-cold sea recovering from the winter storms

Apr.marine_life: grouper waking on the walls with moray and octopus active before the crowds arrive
Apr.conditions: local season opening with cool water and thick suits

May.marine_life: barracuda gathering off the headlands while grouper settle on the Afkule wall
May.conditions: comfortable water above 20°C with daily boats resuming

Jun.marine_life: the first loggerhead turtles over the seagrass bays while grouper thicken on the walls
Jun.conditions: warm settled sea with clarity building at Afkule

Jul.marine_life: turtles in the shallow bays, barracuda schooling and shrimp thick in the caves
Jul.conditions: hot calm days with superb clarity across the gulf

Aug.marine_life: loggerheads over the seagrass with grouper, octopus and barracuda on every dive
Aug.conditions: warmest water of the year and the Afkule wall at its clearest

Sep.marine_life: turtles feeding in the bays while barracuda hunt and grouper hold the walls
Sep.conditions: still-warm 28°C water with settled seas and long visibility

Oct.marine_life: grouper, moray and octopus busy while passing barracuda linger into the autumn
Oct.conditions: pleasant water with daily boats running late into the month

Nov.marine_life: resident grouper and octopus holding on with the shrimp caves unchanged
Nov.conditions: cooling gulf with the season winding down toward by-arrangement diving

Dec.marine_life: grouper and moray tucked into the wall while the cave shrimp carry the interest
Dec.conditions: cool early-winter sea with most daily boats stopped

dive_sites.name: Afkule (Aladdin's Cavern)
dive_sites.blurb: A dramatic wall beneath the cliff-top Afkule monastery, with an upper cavern at 4-12 m and the 'Shrimp Hole' at 24 m; one of Turkey's most celebrated dives, and the wall continues past 30 m toward 50 m for suitably qualified divers.

dive_sites.name: Aquarium Bay
dive_sites.blurb: A calm, glass-clear bay on the Fethiye peninsula used for training and first dives; sandy bottom with seagrass and reef patches around a 10 m maximum.

dive_sites.name: Turkish Bath (Hamam Bay)
dive_sites.blurb: A shallow reef beside sunken Byzantine bath ruins — the so-called 'Cleopatra's Hamam' — in the Gulf of Fethiye; a 10 m heritage-flavoured dive on the standard boat circuit.

dive_sites.name: Three Tunnels
dive_sites.blurb: A trio of swim-through rock tunnels between 10 m and 20 m in the cliffs outside Fethiye bay — torch-lit passages with soldierfish and shrimp, manageable for confident newer divers.

dive_sites.name: Shrimp Cave
dive_sites.blurb: A cave with its entrance at 26 m rising to a shrimp-filled chamber at 18 m; thousands of cleaner shrimp carpet the walls, making it Fethiye's best-known specialist dive.

dive_sites.name: Dalyan Reef (Dalyan Bay)
dive_sites.blurb: An easy reef and seagrass bay near Fethiye harbour, around 10 m deep, with octopus, bream and the occasional turtle — a staple second dive on daily two-tank trips.

destinations.description: Fethiye sits on a sheltered gulf of Turkey's Turquoise Coast, its day boats reaching most sites within 30-60 minutes. Afkule is the headline — a wall beneath a cliff-top monastery with a cavern at 4-12 m, the 'Shrimp Hole' at 24 m and rock continuing toward 50 m — backed by the Three Tunnels swim-throughs, a cave whose chamber walls carpet with cleaner shrimp, and shallow heritage dives over the sunken Byzantine bath ruins of Hamam Bay.
destinations.underwater: The gulf is well sheltered, so most dives are calm, with only occasional surge on the exposed Afkule wall. Visibility runs 15-30 m through the April-November season — up to 30 m at Afkule in summer — and drops to 10-15 m after winter storms. Depths suit every stage: 10 m seagrass bays at Aquarium and Dalyan, torch-lit tunnels at 10-20 m, and Shrimp Cave with its entrance at 26 m. The surface hits 29°C in August and 16-19°C in winter.
destinations.encounters: Loggerhead turtles cruise the seagrass bays — Dalyan Reef often turns one up on a second dive — while dusky grouper hold the deeper walls at Afkule. Moray eels and common octopus fill the reef crevices year-round, and Mediterranean barracuda school off the headlands in summer. The signature is smaller: thousands of cleaner shrimp carpeting the chamber walls of Shrimp Cave at 18 m, picked out by torchlight.
